column5: summary
column6: description   (clean-room v2: prose from model knowledge, flags deterministic from Intel SDM Vol 2)

column5: not an instruction - operand-size fragment
column6: Not an instruction. Duplicate `512`/`256`/`128` `bit memory location` fragment - it describes operand sizes, not an opcode. **Skip it.**

column2: v sse2 shuffle two pairs of double precision floating
column5: shufpd - shuffle packed doubles
column6: This garbled entry corresponds to `shufpd`, which builds a result by selecting one of the two double-precision elements from the destination for the low lane and one from the source for the high lane, with an 8-bit immediate supplying the two select bits. **Only the low two bits of the immediate are meaningful, and the low result element always comes from the destination while the high comes from the source** — getting that operand order backwards is the classic bug; it touches no EFLAGS.

column5: aaa - ASCII adjust AL after addition
column6: Adjusts the result in `AL` after adding two unpacked BCD digits so it holds a valid decimal digit, incrementing `AH` and setting `CF`/`AF` on a carry out of the low nibble. Operates implicitly on `AX` with no operands. **Invalid in 64-bit mode** — it only assembles in 16/32-bit code, and only makes sense immediately after an `add` of unpacked-BCD values.

column5: aad - ASCII adjust AX before division
column6: Converts the unpacked-BCD pair in `AH:AL` into a single binary value in `AL` (computing `AL = AH*10 + AL`, then clearing `AH`) so a following `div` produces BCD-style quotient and remainder. The base-10 multiplier is actually an immediate byte, so a non-standard encoding can adjust for any base. **Invalid in 64-bit mode**, and it sets `ZF`/`SF`/`PF` from the result while leaving `CF`/`OF`/`AF` undefined.

column5: aam - ASCII adjust AX after multiply
column6: Splits the binary product in `AL` into two unpacked-BCD digits, placing the tens in `AH` and units in `AL` (i.e. `AH = AL/10`, `AL = AL%10`). The divisor is an immediate byte in the encoding, so it can split by any base, not just 10. **Invalid in 64-bit mode**; flags `SF`/`ZF`/`PF` reflect `AL` while `CF`/`OF`/`AF` are left undefined, and a zero immediate raises #DE.

column5: aas - ASCII adjust AL after subtraction
column6: Adjusts `AL` after subtracting two unpacked-BCD digits, borrowing from `AH` and setting `CF`/`AF` when the low nibble underflows. Works implicitly on `AX` with no operands. **Invalid in 64-bit mode** — usable only in 16/32-bit code right after a `sub` of unpacked-BCD values.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: adc - add with carry
column6: Computes `dest = dest + src + CF`, the building block for multi-word addition. Run a plain `add` on the lowest limb first, then chain `adc` through the higher limbs to propagate the carry. **It reads `CF` on entry**, so clear it (e.g. with `clc`) before the first `adc` if no prior `add` set it, and don't let an intervening flag-touching instruction clobber `CF` between limbs.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: adcx - unsigned add with carry, CF only
column6: Adds two unsigned integers plus `CF` and writes only `CF`, leaving every other flag (notably `OF`) untouched. It exists so you can interleave two independent carry chains — one through `adcx` (`CF`) and one through `adox` (`OF`) — to build wide multiplies without the chains stepping on each other. **Only `CF` is updated**; `SF`/`ZF`/`PF`/`AF`/`OF` are preserved, so don't branch on them after `adcx`.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: add - add
column6: Computes `dest = dest + src` and sets the full arithmetic flag set from the result. Use `CF` to test for unsigned carry/wraparound and `OF` for signed overflow. **Operand size determines the width of the wraparound** — a 32-bit `add` zero-extends its result into the full 64-bit register, and it does not consume an incoming carry (use `adc` for that).

column5: addpd - add packed double-precision floats
column6: Adds two packed double-precision floats lane-wise: `xmm[0]+=src[0]` and `xmm[1]+=src[1]`. The legacy SSE2 form overwrites the destination; use the VEX form `vaddpd` for a non-destructive three-operand result. **Touches no `EFLAGS`** — floating-point exception state lands in `MXCSR`, not the integer flags, and a memory operand must be 16-byte aligned for the non-VEX encoding.

column5: addps - add packed single-precision floats
column6: Adds four packed single-precision floats lane-wise across the 128-bit register. The SSE form is two-operand and destructive; `vaddps` gives a separate destination. **No integer flags are affected** (results/exceptions go through `MXCSR`), and the non-VEX memory form requires 16-byte alignment.

column5: addsd - add scalar double-precision float
column6: Adds the low double-precision element of the source to the low element of the destination, leaving the upper 64 bits of the destination unchanged. This is the scalar form used for ordinary `double` arithmetic. **Only the low lane is computed**, no `EFLAGS` are touched, and the preserved upper bits matter when the register also holds packed data.

column5: addss - add scalar single-precision float
column6: Adds the low single-precision element of the source to the low element of the destination, preserving the upper 96 bits of the destination. This is the scalar form for ordinary `float` arithmetic. **Only the low 32-bit lane changes** and no integer flags are affected — floating-point status lives in `MXCSR`.

column5: addsubpd - alternating add/subtract packed doubles
column6: Subtracts in the low lane and adds in the high lane: `dest[0] = dest[0]-src[0]`, `dest[1] = dest[1]+src[1]`. This SSE3 primitive is the workhorse for complex-number arithmetic. **The two lanes use opposite operations**, so mind which datum sits in the low versus high half; no integer flags are affected.

column5: addsubps - alternating add/subtract packed singles
column6: Applies subtract to the even single-precision lanes and add to the odd lanes (`-,+,-,+`) across the 128-bit register. Like its double-precision sibling, it accelerates complex-arithmetic and interleaved butterfly patterns. **Even and odd lanes get different operations**, so lane ordering is load-bearing; it leaves `EFLAGS` untouched.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: adox - unsigned add with carry, OF only
column6: Adds two unsigned integers plus the *overflow* flag `OF` and writes only `OF`, leaving `CF` and all other flags intact. Paired with `adcx`, it lets you run a second independent carry chain through `OF` so two big-integer accumulations can interleave. **Only `OF` is updated**; despite the `OF` name it is used as a plain carry bit, and `CF`/`SF`/`ZF`/`PF`/`AF` are preserved.

column5: aesdec - one round of AES decryption
column6: Performs one full round of AES decryption (InvShiftRows, InvSubBytes, InvMixColumns, then XOR the round key) on a 128-bit state in `xmm`. Chain one `aesdec` per middle round, feeding each round key in turn. **This is a middle round only** — use `aesdeclast` for the final round (which omits InvMixColumns), and it sets no `EFLAGS`.

column5: aesdeclast - last round of AES decryption
column6: Performs the final AES decryption round (InvShiftRows, InvSubBytes, XOR round key) with no InvMixColumns step. Use it once, after the chain of `aesdec` middle rounds, to finish a block. **Picking the wrong final-round instruction silently corrupts the plaintext**; pair `aesdeclast` with `aesdec`, and note it affects no flags.

column5: aesenc - one round of AES encryption
column6: Performs one full round of AES encryption (ShiftRows, SubBytes, MixColumns, then XOR the round key) on a 128-bit state in `xmm`. Issue one per middle round, supplying each expanded round key. **This is a middle round only** — the last round must use `aesenclast`, which drops MixColumns; it touches no `EFLAGS`.

column5: aesenclast - last round of AES encryption
column6: Performs the final AES encryption round (ShiftRows, SubBytes, XOR round key) without the MixColumns step. Use it once to terminate a chain of `aesenc` middle rounds. **Using `aesenc` instead of `aesenclast` for the last round produces wrong ciphertext**; it modifies no flags.

column5: aesimc - AES inverse mix columns
column6: Applies the AES InvMixColumns transform to a 128-bit value, the step needed to convert encryption round keys into the equivalent decryption round keys (the AES key schedule's 'equivalent inverse cipher'). Run it over each middle-round key when precomputing a decrypt schedule. **It is a key-schedule helper, not a cipher round**, and sets no `EFLAGS`.

column5: aeskeygenassist - AES round key generation assist
column6: Helps expand the AES key schedule by applying SubWord and RotWord to the source and XORing in an 8-bit round constant supplied as the immediate. You still combine its output with prior key words yourself to derive each new round key. **The round constant (RCON) is the immediate operand and changes per round**; the instruction affects no flags.

column5: and - bitwise logical AND
column6: Computes `dest = dest & src` bit by bit, commonly used to mask off bits or clear a register against itself. It updates `SF`/`ZF`/`PF` from the result and **always clears `CF` and `OF` to 0**, while leaving `AF` undefined — so a following `jc`/`jo` is meaningless, but `jz`/`js` reflect the masked value.

column5: andn - bitwise AND-NOT (BMI1)
column6: Computes `dest = (~src1) & src2`, inverting the first source before ANDing — handy for clearing selected bits or isolating the lowest set bit idioms. This BMI1 form is non-destructive three-operand. **It updates only `SF` and `ZF` (and clears `CF`/`OF`), leaving `PF`/`AF` undefined**, and note the inversion applies to the *first* source operand, not the second.

column5: andnpd - bitwise AND-NOT packed doubles
column6: Computes `dest = (~dest) & src` across all 128 bits, treating the operands as raw bit patterns rather than numbers. It is mostly used for sign-bit masking tricks on packed doubles. **The destination is the operand that gets inverted**, the operation is purely bitwise (no float semantics), and it touches no `EFLAGS`.

column5: andnps - bitwise AND-NOT packed singles
column6: Computes `dest = (~dest) & src` over the full 128-bit register as a raw bitwise operation on packed singles. Common in sign-flip and select/blend masks. **The first/destination operand is the one inverted**, it carries no floating-point rounding, and it affects no flags.

column5: andpd - bitwise AND packed doubles
column6: Computes `dest = dest & src` bitwise across 128 bits, typically to apply a mask (e.g. clearing the sign bit to take `fabs` of packed doubles). It is a raw bit operation with no float arithmetic. **Build the mask with the correct bit layout** since lanes aren't interpreted as numbers; no `EFLAGS` are affected.

column5: andps - bitwise AND packed singles
column6: Computes `dest = dest & src` over the full 128 bits, used to mask packed singles (e.g. AND off the sign bits for absolute value). Purely bitwise — no rounding or float semantics. **Get the mask bit pattern right**, and remember it leaves the integer flags untouched.

column5: arpl - adjust RPL field of segment selector
column6: Compares the requested-privilege-level (RPL) field of a segment selector against another and raises the destination's RPL to the larger, setting `ZF` when an adjustment was made. It is an OS-kernel instruction for sanitizing selectors passed in from user space. **Invalid in 64-bit mode** (its opcode is reused for `movsxd`), so it only appears in legacy/protected-mode 16/32-bit kernels.

column5: bextr - bit field extract (BMI1)
column6: Extracts a contiguous run of bits from the source into the destination, with the start position in bits `[7:0]` and the length in bits `[15:8]` of the control operand. The result is right-justified (shifted down to bit 0). **`ZF` is set if the extracted field is zero; `SF`/`OF`/`CF` are cleared and `AF`/`PF` are undefined** — and watch that the control operand packs start and length into one register, not two separate immediates.

column5: blendpd - blend packed doubles by immediate
column6: Selects each of the two double-precision lanes from either the source or the destination according to the low bits of an 8-bit immediate mask (bit set picks the source lane). This SSE4.1 form chooses lanes at assembly time via the immediate. **Only the two low mask bits matter**, and for a runtime/variable mask you need `blendvpd` instead; it affects no flags.

column5: blendps - blend packed singles by imm8 mask
column6: Selects each of the four 32-bit float lanes of the destination `xmm` from either the destination or the source according to the bits of an 8-bit immediate mask: a set bit picks the corresponding lane from `xmm2/m128`, a clear bit keeps the original. **The selector is a compile-time `imm8`, not a runtime register** - if you need a data-dependent mask use `blendvps` instead, and remember only the low 4 bits of the immediate are meaningful.

column5: blendvpd - variable blend packed doubles via XMM0
column6: Blends the two 64-bit double lanes of the destination with `xmm2/m128`, choosing each lane based on the sign bit (the top bit) of the corresponding lane in the mask register. **The legacy SSE4.1 form uses an implicit, hard-wired `xmm0` as the mask** - it is not an operand you can name, so you must load your mask into `xmm0` first and beware that any value already there will be clobbered as the selector. Only the high bit of each lane matters; the rest of the mask is ignored.

column5: blendvps - variable blend packed singles via XMM0
column6: Blends the four 32-bit float lanes of the destination with `xmm2/m128`, selecting each lane from the source when the high (sign) bit of that lane in the mask is set, otherwise keeping the destination. **Like `blendvpd`, the SSE4.1 encoding uses an implicit `xmm0` as the mask register** - it cannot be specified explicitly, so stage your selector in `xmm0` and only the most-significant bit of each 32-bit lane is consulted.

column5: blsi - isolate lowest set bit
column6: Computes `(-src) AND src`, leaving only the lowest set bit of the source in the destination (or zero if the source is zero). `CF` is set when the source was non-zero and cleared when it was zero, `SF` reflects the result's sign bit and `ZF` is set when the result is zero, while `OF` is always cleared. **This is a BMI1 instruction and `AF`/`PF` are left undefined** - test `CF` (not `ZF`) if you want to branch on whether the input had any set bits.

column5: blsmsk - mask up to lowest set bit
column6: Produces `(src - 1) XOR src`, a mask with all bits set from bit 0 up to and including the lowest set bit of the source. `CF` is set when the source is zero (in which case the result is all-ones) and clear otherwise, `SF` takes the result's top bit, and `OF` is cleared. **`ZF` is always cleared here because the result is never zero**, and `AF`/`PF` are left undefined - so use `CF` to detect a zero input.

column5: blsr - reset lowest set bit
column6: Computes `(src - 1) AND src`, clearing the lowest set bit of the source and copying the rest into the destination. `CF` is set when the source was zero, `ZF` is set when the result is zero, `SF` reflects the sign bit, and `OF` is cleared. **A BMI1 instruction that leaves `AF`/`PF` undefined** - it is the classic primitive for iterating over set bits one at a time, pairing naturally with `blsi`/`tzcnt`.

column5: bndcl - MPX check lower bound
column6: Compares an address against the lower bound stored in a `bnd` register and raises a #BR bounds exception if the address is below it. This is part of the now-deprecated Intel MPX feature. **MPX is obsolete and unsupported on modern CPUs, where these encodings execute as NOPs** - do not rely on it for memory safety, and note it touches no arithmetic flags.

column5: bndcu - MPX check upper bound
column6: Compares an address against the upper bound in a `bnd` register (stored in ones-complement form internally) and faults with #BR if the address exceeds it; `bndcn` is the variant that takes the upper bound in its non-complemented form. Part of deprecated Intel MPX. **MPX has been removed from current hardware and these run as NOPs there** - treat it as legacy and note no EFLAGS are modified.

column5: bndldx - MPX load extended bounds
column6: Loads bounds from the MPX bound table in memory using address translation through the bound directory, given a pointer and index. Part of the deprecated Intel MPX feature. **MPX is no longer supported on current CPUs and this behaves as a NOP** - it modifies no flags and is effectively dead in modern code.

column5: bndmk - MPX make bounds
column6: Creates a new bounds pair in a `bnd` register from a base address and a length expressed through a memory operand. Part of the deprecated Intel MPX feature. **MPX is obsolete and these instructions are NOPs on modern hardware** - it sets no EFLAGS and should not be used in new code.

column5: bndmov - MPX move bounds
column6: Copies a bounds pair between two `bnd` registers or between a `bnd` register and memory, moving both the lower and upper bound together. Part of the deprecated Intel MPX feature. **Like the rest of MPX it runs as a NOP on current CPUs and affects no flags** - keep it out of new code.

column5: bndstx - MPX store extended bounds
column6: Stores a bounds pair from a `bnd` register into the MPX bound table in memory, using address translation through the bound directory. Part of the deprecated Intel MPX feature. **MPX is unsupported on modern processors and this acts as a NOP** - it changes no flags and has no practical use today.

column5: bound - check array index against bounds
column6: Checks that a signed array index in a register lies within the inclusive lower and exclusive-style upper bounds held in a two-word/-dword memory operand, raising a #BR exception if the index is out of range. **This is a legacy instruction that is invalid in 64-bit mode** - it only assembles in 16/32-bit code, and even there it is slow and essentially unused by modern compilers.

column5: breakpoint is not re - PDF extraction artifact
column6: **This is not a real instruction.** The entry is a fragment of sentence text ("...breakpoint is not re-triggered when the instruction is continued.") accidentally captured from a PDF manual during scraping. There is nothing to assemble here; the relevant real mnemonic is most likely `int3`/`int 3` (the one-byte software breakpoint `0xCC`). **Discard this entry from any instruction table — it carries no opcode and no operands.**

column5: broadcast an 8 - PDF extraction artifact
column6: **This is not a real instruction.** "broadcast an 8" (title "bit value from a GPR to all bytes in...") is a fragment of the prose describing `vpbroadcastb`, split mid-sentence ("broadcast an 8-bit value from a GPR to all bytes in the destination") by the PDF extractor. It is not a mnemonic and encodes nothing. **Discard it; the real instruction it describes is `vpbroadcastb`.**

column5: bsf - bit scan forward (lowest set bit index)
column6: Scans the source from bit 0 upward and writes the index of the first set bit into the destination register. `ZF` is set when the source is zero and clear otherwise. **When the source is zero the destination is left undefined, so you must check `ZF` before using the result** - and unlike `tzcnt` it gives no defined value (and no count) for an all-zero input.

column5: bsr - bit scan reverse (highest set bit index)
column6: Scans the source from the most-significant bit downward and writes the index of the highest set bit into the destination. `ZF` is set when the source is zero, clear otherwise. **On a zero source the destination is undefined - always branch on `ZF` first**, and note that unlike `lzcnt` the result is the bit position, not a leading-zero count.

column5: bswap - reverse byte order of a register
column6: Reverses the byte order of a 32- or 64-bit register, converting between little-endian and big-endian representations in place. **Use it only on 32-bit or 64-bit registers - applying `bswap` to a 16-bit register is undefined** (use `xchg` of the byte halves or `ror reg,8` for 16-bit swaps instead). It touches no flags.

column5: bt - copy a single bit into CF
column6: Copies the selected bit of the destination (chosen by the second operand's bit index) into `CF` without modifying the destination. When the destination is a register the bit index is taken modulo the operand size. **Only `CF` is affected; the other flags are left undefined** - and with a memory operand plus a register index `bt` can address bits outside the addressed word, so for memory the index is not masked the way it is for registers.

column5: btc - test bit into CF, then complement it
column6: Copies the selected bit into `CF` and then flips (complements) that bit in the destination. For register destinations the bit index is taken modulo the operand size. **Only `CF` holds a defined result; the remaining flags are undefined**, and on a memory destination this is a read-modify-write that is not atomic unless you prefix it with `lock`.

column5: btr - test bit into CF, then clear it
column6: Copies the selected bit into `CF` and then clears that bit (sets it to 0) in the destination. Register bit indices are taken modulo the operand size. **Only `CF` is defined afterward; the other flags are undefined**, and a memory read-modify-write needs a `lock` prefix to be atomic across cores.

column5: bts - test bit into CF, then set it
column6: Copies the selected bit into `CF` and then sets that bit to 1 in the destination. For register operands the index wraps modulo the operand size. **Only `CF` is meaningful; the rest of the flags are undefined**, and for a memory destination add a `lock` prefix if the set must be atomic (this is the building block for spinlock bit-test-and-set).

column5: bzhi - zero high bits from an index
column6: Copies the source to the destination but zeroes all bits at and above the position given by the low 8 bits of the index operand, effectively masking off the high part. `CF` is set when the index was greater than or equal to the operand size (meaning nothing was zeroed), `ZF` and `SF` reflect the result, and `OF` is cleared. **A BMI2 instruction whose count comes from the low byte of the second operand, not from `cl`** - and `AF`/`PF` are left undefined.

column5: call - call procedure (push return address)
column6: Pushes the address of the following instruction onto the stack and transfers control to the target, which may be a relative displacement, an absolute address in a register/memory, or a far pointer. **It decrements `rsp` by 8 in 64-bit mode, so the stack is no longer 16-byte aligned on entry to the callee** - account for that return-address push when meeting the ABI's alignment requirement, and note `call` itself sets no flags.

column5: clac - clear Alignment Check (SMAP) flag
column6: Clears the `AC` (alignment-check) flag in EFLAGS, which under SMAP blocks supervisor code from accessing user-mode pages. **This is a privileged, kernel-only instruction available only when SMAP is supported** - it faults in user mode, and it is paired with `stac` to temporarily allow then re-block user-memory access.

column5: clc - clear carry flag
column6: Clears the carry flag (`CF` becomes 0) and leaves all other flags untouched. **It affects only `CF`** - use it to seed a known carry before an `adc`/`sbb` chain or `rcl`/`rcr` rotate, where the incoming carry matters.

column5: cld - clear direction flag (forward strings)
column6: Clears the direction flag (`DF` becomes 0) so that string operations like `movs`, `stos`, `cmps`, `lods` and `scas` auto-increment their pointers and run forward through memory. **The SysV/Windows ABIs require `DF` to be clear on function entry and exit** - issue `cld` before any string op if you cannot prove the flag's state, and pair it with `std` only when you deliberately need backward iteration.

column5: cldemote - hint cache line to move to outer cache
column6: Hints the processor to demote the cache line containing the given address from the inner caches toward the last-level cache, so another core can pick it up faster. **It is only a hint and may be ignored, and on CPUs without the feature it executes as a NOP** - it never faults on a bad address and modifies no flags or memory contents.

column5: clflush - flush cache line to memory
column6: Writes back (if dirty) and invalidates the cache line containing the specified memory operand from every level of the cache hierarchy across all coherent processors. **It is ordered loosely with respect to other stores, so you typically need an `sfence`/`mfence` around it** to guarantee the flush completes before dependent ordering; it modifies no flags.

column5: clflushopt - optimized cache line flush
column6: A more weakly-ordered, higher-throughput variant of `clflush` that writes back and invalidates a cache line, allowing multiple flushes to pipeline rather than serialize. **Because it is even more relaxed about ordering than `clflush`, you must fence it explicitly (`sfence`) when persistence or visibility ordering matters** - common in persistent-memory flush loops; it sets no flags.

column5: cli - clear interrupt flag (mask interrupts)
column6: Clears the interrupt-enable flag (`IF` becomes 0), masking maskable hardware interrupts until `sti` re-enables them. **This is a privileged operation that faults in user mode (subject to IOPL/CPL)** - it does not block non-maskable interrupts or exceptions, and overusing it in a kernel raises interrupt latency.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: clrssbsy - clear busy flag on shadow stack token
column6: Clears the busy flag in a supervisor shadow-stack token at the given address, marking that shadow stack as no longer in use, as part of CET shadow-stack management. **It requires CET shadow stacks to be enabled and is essentially kernel/supervisor-only**; despite listing many flags, it primarily clears `ZF` on success (setting it on failure) and leaves `CF`/`PF`/`AF`/`SF`/`OF` cleared.

column5: clts - clear task-switched flag in CR0
column6: Clears the TS (task-switched) bit in control register `CR0`, which the OS uses to manage lazy FPU/SSE state saving on context switches. **This is a privileged instruction usable only at CPL 0** - it faults anywhere else and is purely a kernel-level operation; it modifies no EFLAGS.

column5: clui - clear user interrupt flag
column6: Clears the user-interrupt flag (UIF), disabling delivery of user interrupts to the current thread as part of the User Interrupts (UINTR) feature. **It is only valid when UINTR is enabled and faults otherwise**; it is distinct from `cli`, affecting user-level interrupt delivery rather than the architectural `IF`, and it sets no EFLAGS.

column5: clwb - cache line write back (keep line)
column6: Writes back a dirty cache line to memory but, unlike `clflush`, leaves the line valid in the cache so subsequent accesses still hit. **It is weakly ordered and must be followed by an `sfence` to guarantee the write-back is durable** - the standard tool for flushing persistent-memory stores without evicting the line; it modifies no flags.

column5: cmc - complement (toggle) carry flag
column6: Flips the carry flag (`CF`) to its opposite value, leaving all other flags unchanged. **It affects only `CF`** - it is a quick way to invert a carry between an addition and a subtraction-with-borrow chain without recomputing anything.

column5: cmova - move if above, unsigned (CF=0 and ZF=0)
column6: Unsigned conditional move taken when the first operand was strictly above the second (`CF=0` and `ZF=0`). **Use this after an unsigned `cmp`; the signed counterpart is `cmovg`** - mixing signedness gives wrong results across the 0x7F.../0x80... boundary. The source is always read, and no flags change.

column5: cmovae - move if above or equal, unsigned (CF=0)
column6: Unsigned conditional move taken when there was no borrow (`CF=0`), i.e. the first operand was at least the second. **It is the unsigned form - use `cmovge` for signed comparisons** - and because it keys off `CF` it also serves as 'move if carry clear'. The memory source is accessed unconditionally; no flags are modified.

column5: cmovb - move if below, unsigned (CF=1)
column6: Unsigned conditional move taken when a borrow occurred (`CF=1`), meaning the first operand was below the second. **This is the unsigned test and doubles as 'move if carry set'; for signed values use `cmovl`**. As always with `cmovcc`, the source is read even when the move is skipped, so it cannot guard a faulting load. No flags change.

column5: cmovbe - move if below or equal, unsigned (CF=1 or ZF=1)
column6: Unsigned conditional move taken when the first operand was below or equal (`CF=1` or `ZF=1`). **Use after an unsigned `cmp`; the signed equivalent is `cmovle`**, and note the source operand is always evaluated regardless of the condition. It modifies no flags.

column5: cmove - move if equal (ZF=1)
column6: Copies the source into the destination register only if `ZF` is set, typically used right after a `cmp` or `test` to branchlessly select a value when the operands were equal. **Unlike a `jcc`, `cmovcc` always reads the source operand, so a memory source is dereferenced even when the condition is false** - never use it to guard a load against an invalid pointer; it sets no flags and has no 8-bit form.

column5: cmovg - move if greater, signed (ZF=0 and SF=OF)
column6: Performs a signed conditional move, copying the source when the prior comparison found the first operand greater (`ZF=0` and `SF=OF`). **Use the signed `cmovg` family only after a signed `cmp`; for unsigned magnitudes use `cmova` instead**, and remember the memory source is always accessed whether or not the condition holds. It sets no flags.

column5: cmovge - move if greater or equal, signed (SF=OF)
column6: Signed conditional move taken when the first operand was greater than or equal (`SF=OF`). **Pair it with a signed `cmp`; the unsigned analog is `cmovae`**, and as with all `cmovcc` the source operand is read even when the move is suppressed, so it is unsafe as a guarded load. No flags are modified.

column5: cmovl - move if less, signed (SF<>OF)
column6: Signed conditional move taken when the first operand was less (`SF<>OF`). **This is the signed test - after comparing unsigned values use `cmovb` instead**, and note the source is always fetched regardless of the condition. It leaves all flags unchanged.

column5: cmovle - move if less or equal, signed (ZF=1 or SF<>OF)
column6: Signed conditional move taken when the first operand was less than or equal (`ZF=1` or `SF<>OF`). **Choose this signed form only after a signed `cmp`; for unsigned use `cmovbe`**, and be aware the source operand is always read even on the false path, so it cannot protect a bad pointer. It sets no flags.

column5: cmovne - move if not equal (ZF=0)
column6: Copies the source into the destination only when `ZF` is clear, the branchless complement of `cmove`, usually following a `cmp`/`test`. **The source is always read regardless of the condition, so a memory operand is loaded even when the move does not happen** - it cannot guard against a faulting address; it modifies no flags.

column5: cmovno - move if no overflow (OF=0)
column6: Conditional move taken when the overflow flag is clear (`OF=0`). **Use it immediately after the arithmetic that sets `OF`, since intervening instructions may clobber the flag**, and remember the source operand is always read even on the not-taken path. It sets no flags.

column5: cmovnp - move if parity odd (PF=0)
column6: Conditional move taken when the parity flag is clear (`PF=0`), i.e. the low byte of the result had an odd number of set bits. **Parity is computed only over the low 8 bits; it is mainly useful for the ordered/unordered case of floating-point compares**, and the source operand is always accessed. It modifies no flags.

column5: cmovns - move if not sign (SF=0, non-negative)
column6: Conditional move taken when the sign flag is clear (`SF=0`), i.e. the previous result was zero or positive. **Like `cmovs` it keys off the bare `SF` bit from the last arithmetic operation, not a comparison ordering**, and it reads the source unconditionally. It leaves all flags unchanged.

column5: cmovo - move if overflow (OF=1)
column6: Conditional move taken when the overflow flag is set (`OF=1`), signaling that the previous signed arithmetic overflowed. **It is meaningful only right after an operation that defines `OF`**, and as with every `cmovcc` the memory source is dereferenced whether or not the move happens. No flags are modified.

column5: cmovp - move if parity even (PF=1)
column6: Conditional move taken when the parity flag is set (`PF=1`), meaning the low byte of the last result had an even number of set bits. **`PF` reflects only the least-significant byte, and it is a common signal after `ucomisd`/`comiss` to detect unordered (NaN) float compares** - the source is read regardless of the condition. No flags change.

column5: cmovs - move if sign (SF=1, negative)
column6: Conditional move taken when the sign flag is set (`SF=1`), i.e. the previous result was negative. **It tests only the raw sign bit, not a full signed comparison, so it follows an arithmetic op rather than standing in for `cmovl`** - and the source is read even when the move does not occur. No flags are affected.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: cmp - compare two operands (sets flags only)
column6: Subtracts the source from the destination and sets `CF`, `PF`, `AF`, `ZF`, `SF`, and `OF` according to the result, but discards the difference and leaves both operands unchanged. **Which `jcc`/`setcc`/`cmovcc` you use next must match the signedness of the data: `ja`/`jb` (unsigned) read `CF`/`ZF`, while `jg`/`jl` (signed) read `SF`/`OF`/`ZF`** - choosing the wrong family is the classic comparison bug. The operand order matters: `cmp a, b` computes `a - b`.

column5: cmpccxadd - compare and conditionally add+exchange
column6: Atomically loads the memory destination, compares it against the first register operand under a condition code; if the condition holds it writes back the sum of memory and the second register, and in all cases returns the original memory value into the first register. It is an implicitly `lock`ed read-modify-conditional-write primitive (part of the AVX-512/newer ISA extensions). **The condition is tested against the flags produced by comparing the loaded value, and the operation is always atomic without an explicit `lock` prefix; the first source register is also a destination — it always receives the pre-operation memory contents.**

column5: cmppd - compare packed doubles by predicate imm8
column6: Compares the two packed double lanes of the destination `xmm` against `xmm2/m128` using the relation selected by an immediate predicate, writing an all-ones mask into each lane where the comparison is true and all-zeros where false. **The comparison kind is encoded in the `imm8` (eq, lt, le, unord, and their negations), and pseudo-ops like `cmpeqpd`/`cmpltpd` are just assembler shorthands for specific immediate values** - the result is a bitmask in the SSE register, and no EFLAGS are touched (use `comisd`/`ucomisd` if you want EFLAGS instead).

column5: cmpps - compare packed single-precision floats
column6: Compares the four packed single-precision floats in the source against the destination using an immediate predicate (`eq`, `lt`, `le`, `unord`, `neq`, `nlt`, `nle`, `ord`), writing an all-ones (`0xFFFFFFFF`) or all-zeros mask into each destination lane. The result is a SIMD mask meant to be consumed by `andps`/`blendvps`, not a boolean. **Unlike the integer `cmp`, this touches no EFLAGS at all — the comparison outcome lives entirely in the destination register, so don't follow it with a `jcc`.** Assemblers expose the named pseudo-ops (`cmpeqps`, `cmpltps`, ...) which just encode the matching immediate.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: cmps - compare string operands
column6: Compares the byte/word/dword/qword at `[rsi]` with the one at `[rdi]`, sets the arithmetic flags as if it had computed `[rsi] - [rdi]`, then advances both `rsi` and `rdi` by the operand size (forward if `DF=0`, backward if `DF=1`). Typically prefixed with `repe`/`repne` and driven by `rcx` to scan for the first match or mismatch. **Remember `cmps` compares source-minus-destination (the opposite mental order from `cmp dst, src`), set `DF` with `cld`/`std` before the loop, and load `rcx` with the element count — the size suffix or operand decides byte vs word vs dword vs qword.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: cmpsd - compare scalar double-precision float
column6: Compares the low double-precision float of the source and destination using an immediate predicate, writing an all-ones or all-zeros 64-bit mask into the low lane while the high lane of the destination is preserved. The result is a bitmask, not a flag. **No EFLAGS are modified — use `comisd`/`ucomisd` instead if you actually want to branch on a float comparison. Also beware the mnemonic collision: `cmpsd` is both this SSE2 instruction and the string-compare `cmps` doubleword form; the operand types disambiguate them.**

column5: cmpss - compare scalar single-precision float
column6: Compares the low single-precision float of the source and destination using an immediate predicate, writing an all-ones or all-zeros 32-bit mask into the low lane and leaving the upper bits of the destination unchanged. The output is a mask for blending, not a condition code. **It sets no EFLAGS, so you cannot branch on it directly; reach for `comiss`/`ucomiss` when you need ZF/CF/PF from a scalar float compare.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: cmpxchg - compare and exchange
column6: Compares the accumulator (`al`/`ax`/`eax`/`rax`) with the destination; if equal it stores the source register into the destination and sets `ZF`, otherwise it loads the destination back into the accumulator and clears `ZF`. This is the building block of lock-free CAS loops. **The accumulator is an implicit operand that gets overwritten on failure — reload your comparand each retry. For an atomic CAS across cores you must add a `lock` prefix; without it the read-modify-write is not atomic.**

column5: cmpxchg8b - compare and exchange 8/16 bytes
column6: Compares the 64-bit (or 128-bit for `cmpxchg16b`) value in `edx:eax` (`rdx:rax`) against the memory destination; if equal it stores `ecx:ebx` (`rcx:rbx`) there and sets `ZF`, otherwise it loads the memory value into `edx:eax` and clears `ZF`. Used for double-width atomic CAS, e.g. tagged pointers. **The four register halves are all implicit and hardcoded — you must marshal your old value into `edx:eax` and new value into `ecx:ebx` before issuing it, and add a `lock` prefix for atomicity. `cmpxchg16b` additionally requires its memory operand to be 16-byte aligned.**

column1: controls for the high and low 128
column5: vshufpd lanes - per-128-bit immediate control
column6: This is a documentation fragment, not an instruction: it describes how the wider AVX `vshufpd` form reuses the immediate's bits independently for the high and low 128-bit lanes of a `ymm`/`zmm` register. **There is nothing to assemble from this line; just remember that in 256-bit and 512-bit shuffles the lanes do not cross — each 128-bit half is shuffled with its own immediate bits — and no flags are affected.**

column5: cpuid - CPU identification
column6: Returns processor identification and feature information based on the leaf number in `eax` (and subleaf in `ecx`), writing results across `eax`, `ebx`, `ecx`, and `edx`. It also serializes instruction execution. **It clobbers all four of `eax`/`ebx`/`ecx`/`edx` unconditionally, so save anything live in them first; query the maximum supported leaf via leaf 0 before requesting higher leaves, and set `ecx` for leaves that take a subleaf (e.g. leaf 7).**

column5: crc32 - accumulate CRC32 value
column6: Accumulates a CRC32C (Castagnoli polynomial) checksum, folding the source operand (8/16/32/64-bit) into the running CRC held in the destination general-purpose register. Part of SSE4.2. **This computes the CRC-32C variant, not the zlib/PKZIP CRC-32 — don't expect it to match standard file checksums. The destination must be a 32-bit register (or 64-bit when consuming a qword source), and you must seed it with the initial CRC value before the first byte.**

column5: cvtps2dq - convert packed floats to packed int32
column6: Converts the four packed single-precision floats in the source to four packed signed 32-bit integers in the destination, rounding according to the current `MXCSR` rounding mode. **It uses the MXCSR rounding mode (round-to-nearest by default), not truncation — use `cvttps2dq` if you want C-style truncation toward zero. Out-of-range or NaN inputs produce the integer indefinite value `0x80000000` and may raise the invalid-operation SIMD exception.**

column5: cwd/cdq/cqo - sign-extend accumulator into dx/edx/rdx
column6: Sign-extends the accumulator into the `dx:ax`/`edx:eax`/`rdx:rax` pair: `cwd` fills `dx`, `cdq` fills `edx`, and `cqo` fills `rdx`, by replicating the top bit of the accumulator. Its sole job is to prepare the high half of the dividend for a signed `idiv`. **Use this — not a `xor edx, edx` — before a *signed* `idiv`, because the high register must carry the sign extension; zeroing `rdx` is only correct ahead of an *unsigned* `div`.**

column5: daa - decimal adjust AL after addition
column6: Adjusts `al` after a packed-BCD addition so it holds two valid decimal digits, using and updating `AF` and `CF` to propagate decimal carries. **This is a legacy BCD instruction that operates only on `al` and is invalid in 64-bit mode — it will not assemble or execute in long mode, so it only appears in 16/32-bit code.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: das - decimal adjust AL after subtraction
column6: Adjusts `al` after a packed-BCD subtraction so it holds two valid decimal digits, consuming and updating `AF` and `CF` to handle decimal borrows. **Like `daa`, this works only on `al` and is invalid in 64-bit mode, so you will only see it in legacy 16/32-bit BCD code.**

column5: dec - decrement by 1
column6: Subtracts 1 from the register or memory operand and updates the arithmetic flags. **Unlike `sub reg, 1`, `dec` deliberately leaves `CF` untouched — only `OF`, `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, `PF` change — which can create a partial-flags stall on some pipelines and means you cannot rely on the carry flag after it. For an atomic decrement of a memory location you must add a `lock` prefix.**

column5: div - unsigned divide
column6: Performs an unsigned divide of the implicit double-width dividend by the single operand: the quotient goes to `al`/`ax`/`eax`/`rax` and the remainder to `ah`/`dx`/`edx`/`rdx`, depending on operand size. **The dividend is implicit and double-width — you must zero the high half (`xor edx, edx` for 32-bit, `xor rdx, rdx`/`cqo`-equivalent zero for 64-bit) before dividing, or you'll divide the wrong value. A zero divisor or a quotient that overflows the destination raises a #DE divide-error exception, not a flag.**

column5: divpd - packed double-precision FP divide
column6: Divides two packed double-precision floats lane-by-lane, computing `dest[i] = dest[i] / src[i]` across both 64-bit lanes of an XMM register. The legacy SSE2 form overwrites `dest` in place; the VEX `vdivpd` form takes a separate destination. **Floating-point division is expensive and not associative** — it is far slower than `mulpd`, so if you divide by the same vector repeatedly, compute a reciprocal once and multiply instead. No EFLAGS are touched; division-by-zero and invalid results surface through the MXCSR exception flags, not the integer flags.

column5: divps - packed single-precision FP divide
column6: Divides two packed single-precision floats lane-by-lane, computing `dest[i] = dest[i] / src[i]` across all four 32-bit lanes of an XMM register. The legacy form is destructive; `vdivps` provides a non-destructive three-operand encoding. **Single-precision divide is high-latency** — for throughput-critical code consider `rcpps` plus a Newton-Raphson refinement step when full precision is not required. EFLAGS are untouched; overflow, divide-by-zero, and NaN propagation are reported only in MXCSR.

column5: divsd - scalar double-precision FP divide
column6: Divides the low double-precision lane, `dest[63:0] = dest[63:0] / src[63:0]`, and **leaves the upper 64 bits of the destination XMM register unchanged** in the legacy form. The VEX `vdivsd` form copies bits `[127:64]` from a second source operand instead. This high-bits-preserved behavior is a classic gotcha when you expected the whole register cleared — use a clearing idiom first if you depend on the upper lane. No EFLAGS are affected; check MXCSR for divide-by-zero.

column5: divss - scalar single-precision FP divide
column6: Divides the low single-precision lane, `dest[31:0] = dest[31:0] / src[31:0]`, and **leaves the upper 96 bits of the destination XMM register unchanged** in the legacy SSE form. `vdivss` instead fills `[127:32]` from a second source operand. Forgetting that the high bits survive bites you when the register is later read as a full vector. EFLAGS are not modified; numerical exceptions appear only in MXCSR.

column5: dppd - dot product of packed doubles
column6: Computes a dot product of two packed double vectors under control of an 8-bit immediate: the high nibble selects which lanes are multiplied and summed, the low nibble selects which destination lanes receive the broadcast result. **The immediate mask is mandatory and easy to get wrong** — get the nibble layout backwards and you will multiply the wrong lanes or write the sum to the wrong place. With only two doubles the operation is limited compared to `dpps`. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: dpps - dot product of packed singles
column6: Computes a dot product across the four single-precision lanes of two XMM registers, gated by an 8-bit immediate whose high nibble chooses which products enter the sum and whose low nibble chooses which lanes receive the broadcast result. **The immediate is not optional and its two nibbles do different jobs** — high selects inputs, low selects outputs — so a swapped mask silently produces garbage. It is often slower than doing the multiply-and-horizontal-add by hand. EFLAGS untouched.

column5: emms - empty MMX technology state
column6: Clears the x87 tag word to mark all FP/MMX registers empty, returning the shared register file from MMX mode back to a state where x87 floating-point instructions can be used. **You must execute `emms` after any block of MMX code before calling into x87 FP code** (or returning to a caller that uses it), or the next x87 op will see a full stack and fault or produce NaNs. Pure SSE/AVX integer code does not need this — `emms` is only relevant to legacy MMX. No EFLAGS are affected.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: encodekey128 - wrap a 128-bit AES key (Key Locker)
column6: Wraps a 128-bit AES key from `xmm0` into an opaque handle stored across several XMM registers, so later AES Key Locker instructions can use the key without it ever sitting in plaintext in registers. A 32-bit source operand supplies handle restriction flags. **This is part of Intel Key Locker, not classic AES-NI** — the produced handle is bound to the platform's internal wrapping key and is not portable across reboots or machines. It zeroes `OF`, `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, `PF`, and `CF`.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: encodekey256 - wrap a 256-bit AES key (Key Locker)
column6: Wraps a 256-bit AES key taken from `xmm1:xmm0` into an opaque Key Locker handle spread across multiple XMM registers, with a 32-bit operand giving handle restriction flags. The handle lets later wide-key AES Key Locker ops run without exposing the raw key. **The handle is non-portable and tied to the CPU's internal wrapping key**, so it must be regenerated after reboot and cannot be moved to another host. All six arithmetic flags (`OF SF ZF AF PF CF`) are cleared.

column5: endbr32 - CET indirect-branch landing (32-bit)
column6: Marks a valid target for an indirect branch under Control-flow Enforcement Technology in 32-bit/compatibility mode; on CET-enabled hardware an indirect `jmp`/`call` must land on an `endbr32` or the CPU raises a control-protection fault. On older CPUs it executes as a NOP. **Place it as the very first instruction of any function reachable through a function pointer or vtable**, otherwise indirect calls to it will fault when CET is active. It affects no EFLAGS and no architectural state.

column5: endbr64 - CET indirect-branch landing (64-bit)
column6: Marks a legal landing site for indirect branches under CET in 64-bit mode; with indirect-branch tracking enabled, any `call`/`jmp` through a register or memory must arrive at an `endbr64` or trigger a #CP fault. It decodes as a harmless NOP on hardware without CET. **Compilers emit this automatically at function entry, but hand-written assembly targeted by function pointers must add it manually** or break on CET-enforcing systems. No EFLAGS or registers are modified.

column5: enqcmd - enqueue command to device
column6: Atomically writes a 64-byte command descriptor from memory to an enqueue-store-capable device portal (such as a DSA/accelerator MMIO address), issuing it as a single non-posted operation. The descriptor must be read from a 64-byte-aligned source and the destination must be a device ENQCMD region. **It sets `ZF` to report whether the device accepted (0) or rejected/retry (1) the command** — you must branch on `ZF`, since a rejection means the queue was full and you should retry. `OF SF AF PF CF` are cleared.

column5: enqcmds - enqueue command, supervisor form
column6: The supervisor (ring-0) variant of `enqcmd`: it atomically enqueues a 64-byte descriptor to a device portal but takes the PASID and privilege from the descriptor itself rather than the current thread, so an OS can submit work on behalf of a process. **Like `enqcmd`, the result is reported in `ZF`** — `ZF=0` means accepted, `ZF=1` means the device declined and the caller must retry. It is privileged and faults in user mode. `OF SF AF PF CF` are cleared.

column5: enter - build a stack frame
column6: Builds a stack frame: it pushes `rbp`, optionally copies a chain of nested-frame pointers for lexically nested procedures (the second immediate, the nesting level), then subtracts the first immediate from `rsp` to reserve locals. **It is microcoded and slow** — almost all modern code skips it in favor of the explicit `push rbp` / `mov rbp, rsp` / `sub rsp, N` sequence, and a nesting level above 0 is essentially never used outside Pascal-style languages. Pair it with `leave` to unwind. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: extractps - extract one packed single to GPR/mem
column6: Extracts one of the four single-precision floats from an XMM register, selected by a 2-bit immediate, and writes those 32 bits to a general-purpose register or memory. When the destination is a register the value lands as a raw 32-bit bit pattern in the GPR, not in an XMM lane. **Note the result goes to integer/memory space, not to another XMM** — if you want the float to stay in vector registers use a shuffle (`shufps`/`pshufd`) or `insertps` instead. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: f2xm1 - compute 2^x - 1 on ST(0)
column6: Computes `2^ST(0) - 1` and replaces `ST(0)` with the result, the core primitive for building general exponentials on the x87 stack. **The input is only valid in the range -1.0 to +1.0** — outside that interval the result is undefined, so to evaluate a full power you split the exponent into integer and fractional parts and recombine with `fscale`. The '-1' bias preserves precision for small `x`; add 1 back afterward if you need plain 2^x. No EFLAGS are affected (x87 condition codes live in the FPU status word).

column5: fabs - absolute value of ST(0)
column6: Clears the sign bit of `ST(0)`, yielding its absolute value in place. It operates purely on the sign bit so it never rounds, never raises a precision exception, and is exact for every value including infinities. **Note `fabs(-0.0)` gives `+0.0` and `fabs(NaN)` returns the NaN with a cleared sign** — it does not test for or signal NaN. No EFLAGS are affected; only the x87 status word is updated.

column5: fadd - x87 floating-point add
column6: Adds two floating-point values on the x87 stack: `fadd` adds a memory operand or register to `ST(0)`, `faddp` adds `ST(0)` into `ST(i)` and then pops the stack, and `fiadd` converts an integer operand to FP before adding. **The `p` suffix pops — so `faddp` shrinks the stack by one**, a frequent source of stack-underflow bugs if your pushes and pops are unbalanced. With no operands `faddp` defaults to adding `ST(0)` into `ST(1)` and popping. No EFLAGS are affected; results go to the FPU status word.

column5: fbld - load 80-bit packed BCD
column6: Loads an 80-bit packed binary-coded-decimal value from memory, converts it to extended-precision floating point, and pushes it onto the x87 stack as the new `ST(0)`. It is the input half of x87's decimal support, useful for reading human-entered decimal numbers exactly. **The source is a fixed 10-byte packed-BCD field, not an ASCII or integer value** — the top byte holds the sign and the remaining 18 digits are packed two-per-byte. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fbstp - store ST(0) as 80-bit BCD and pop
column6: Rounds `ST(0)` to an integer, converts it to an 80-bit packed BCD value, stores it to a 10-byte memory field, and pops the x87 stack. It is the counterpart to `fbld` for emitting exact decimal results. **The value is rounded to an integer per the current rounding mode before conversion** — any fractional part is lost, so scale beforehand if you need decimal places. It clears the x87 `DF` (D-bit/denormal) context as listed; integer EFLAGS are not the FPU's; the architectural `DF` direction flag is not what x87 manipulates.

column5: fchs - change sign of ST(0)
column6: Toggles the sign bit of `ST(0)`, negating it in place. Like `fabs` it touches only the sign bit, so it is exact for all values and raises no rounding exception. **It flips the sign of zeros and NaNs too** — `fchs` on `+0.0` produces `-0.0` and on a NaN just inverts the sign bit without signaling. No EFLAGS are affected; the FPU status word reflects the operation.

column5: fclex - clear x87 exception flags
column6: Clears the floating-point exception flags (and the busy and exception-summary bits) in the x87 status word. `fclex` is the checked form that first handles any pending unmasked exception; `fnclex` is the no-wait form that skips that check. **Use `fnclex` when you are deliberately discarding a pending exception**, since the plain `fclex` may trigger the pending fault before clearing. This affects only the FPU status word, not the integer EFLAGS.

column5: fcmovb - x87 conditional move if below (CF=1)
column6: Moves `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` only when `CF=1`, i.e. when an earlier unsigned-style comparison (typically `fcomi`/`ucomiss`) set carry. It is a branchless way to select between two FP values on the x87 stack. **The condition reads the integer EFLAGS, so a preceding `fcomi` or SSE compare must set them — a legacy `fcom` (which writes only the FPU status word) will not drive this move.** The move itself leaves `CF PF ZF` as they were.

column5: fcmovbe - x87 conditional move if below/equal
column6: Copies `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `CF=1 or ZF=1` (below-or-equal in unsigned terms). Used after `fcomi` to pick the smaller of two operands without branching. **It tests integer EFLAGS set by `fcomi`/`ucomisd`, not the x87 status word**, so an old-style `fcom` comparison cannot feed it. The conditional move does not itself alter `CF PF ZF`.

column5: fcmove - x87 conditional move if equal (ZF=1)
column6: Moves `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `ZF=1`, i.e. when a preceding FP compare found the operands equal. **Beware that an unordered (NaN) `fcomi` result also sets `ZF` (along with `PF` and `CF`), so an equal test can fire on unordered operands** — check `PF` first if NaNs are possible. It reads integer EFLAGS, so it must follow `fcomi`/`comiss`, not `fcom`. The move leaves the flags unchanged.

column5: fcmovnb - x87 conditional move if not below
column6: Copies `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `CF=0` (not below). It mirrors `fcmovb` for the above-or-equal case after an unsigned-style FP comparison. **The condition comes from the integer EFLAGS produced by `fcomi`/SSE compares, not from the x87 status word**, so feed it with `fcomi` rather than `fcom`. The move preserves `CF PF ZF`.

column5: fcmovnbe - x87 conditional move if not below/equal
column6: Moves `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `CF=0 and ZF=0` (strictly above). Useful after `fcomi` to keep the larger operand branchlessly. **It depends on EFLAGS set by `fcomi`/`ucomiss`; the legacy `fcom` writes only the FPU status word and will not trigger it.** The conditional move itself does not change `CF PF ZF`.

column5: fcmovne - x87 conditional move if not equal
column6: Copies `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `ZF=0` (operands not equal). **Because an unordered `fcomi` (a NaN comparison) sets `ZF=1`, a 'not equal' move will not fire on unordered inputs even though they are genuinely incomparable** — test `PF` to distinguish unordered from truly-not-equal. It reads integer EFLAGS, so it must follow `fcomi`. The move leaves `CF PF ZF` intact.

column5: fcmovnu - x87 conditional move if not unordered
column6: Moves `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `PF=0`, i.e. when the previous FP comparison was ordered (no NaN involved). **`PF` is x87/SSE's unordered indicator after `fcomi`/`comiss`, so this is the standard 'inputs were comparable' guard** — pair it with the other `fcmov` variants to handle NaNs explicitly. It reads integer EFLAGS and does not modify `CF PF ZF`.

column5: fcmovu - x87 conditional move if unordered
column6: Copies `ST(i)` into `ST(0)` when `PF=1`, meaning the prior FP comparison was unordered because at least one operand was NaN. It lets you branchlessly substitute a value when a comparison was meaningless. **`PF` is the unordered flag only after an `fcomi`/SSE compare sets EFLAGS — a `fcom` will not set it** — so guard your NaN handling on the right comparison. The move preserves `CF PF ZF`.

column5: fcom - compare floats via FPU status word
column6: Compares `ST(0)` with a source operand and sets the x87 condition-code bits (`C0`, `C2`, `C3`) in the FPU status word; `fcomp` pops once and `fcompp` pops twice afterward. **It does NOT touch the integer EFLAGS** — to branch you must follow it with `fstsw ax` then `sahf` (or `test`) to copy the condition codes into flags, which is why `fcomi` is usually preferred. The condition bits are arranged so that after `sahf` you can use unsigned `jcc`. No EFLAGS are modified by the compare itself.

column5: fcomi - compare ST(0) to ST(i), set EFLAGS
column6: Compares `ST(0)` with `ST(i)` and writes the result directly into the integer EFLAGS (`ZF`, `PF`, `CF`), so you can branch with ordinary `jb`/`je`/`ja` without juggling the FPU status word. The `ip` forms pop afterward; the `u` forms are the quiet 'unordered' variant that does not signal on QNaN inputs. **An unordered result sets `ZF=PF=CF=1`, so always test `PF` (`jp`) for NaN before trusting an equality or ordering branch.** This is the modern replacement for `fcom`+`fstsw`+`sahf`.

column5: fcos - cosine of ST(0)
column6: Replaces `ST(0)` with `cos(ST(0))`, with the angle taken in radians. **The argument must lie within roughly ±2^63 in magnitude; outside that the operation does not reduce and instead sets `C2` in the status word and leaves `ST(0)` unchanged**, so you must range-reduce large angles yourself. Accuracy also degrades for large inputs because of the fixed internal pi value. No EFLAGS are affected — results land in the FPU status word.

column5: fdecstp - decrement x87 stack-top pointer
column6: Decrements the TOP field of the x87 status word (modulo 8), rotating which physical register is called `ST(0)` without moving any data. It is a way to cycle the register stack by hand. **It changes only the stack-top pointer, not the register contents or the tag word** — no value is pushed or marked, so it is not equivalent to a push and can leave you pointing at a register tagged empty. It clears `C1` and does not affect integer EFLAGS.

column5: fdiv - x87 floating-point divide
column6: Divides `ST(0)` by the source, computing `ST(0) = ST(0) / src`. `fdivp` divides `ST(i)` by `ST(0)` and pops; `fidiv` converts an integer memory operand to FP first. **Division is the slowest x87 arithmetic op by far, and the register-form operand order with the implicit `ST(0)` is easy to reverse — use `fdivr` when you actually want `src/ST(0)`.** Dividing by zero signals the zero-divide exception. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fdivr - x87 reverse divide
column6: Performs reversed floating-point division: where `fdiv` computes `ST(0)/src`, `fdivr` computes `src/ST(0)` and stores it back. `fdivrp` divides and pops; `fidivr` takes an integer operand. **The 'reverse' is the whole point and the whole trap — operand order is swapped versus `fdiv`, so it is extremely easy to compute the reciprocal of what you meant.** Division by zero raises the x87 zero-divide exception. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: ffree - mark an x87 register empty
column6: Marks `ST(i)` as empty in the x87 tag word without changing the stack-top pointer or the data in the physical register. It is used to discard a value you no longer need so the slot can be reused. **It does not pop the stack or move TOP** — so it leaves `ST(0)` where it was; pair it with `fincstp` (the `ffreep` idiom) if you actually want to free and pop. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: ficom - compare ST(0) with integer in memory
column6: Compares `ST(0)` against a 16- or 32-bit signed integer loaded from memory, converting the integer to FP for the comparison, and sets the x87 condition codes; `ficomp` pops afterward. **Like `fcom` it writes only the FPU status word, not the integer EFLAGS**, so you still need `fstsw`/`sahf` to branch. The memory operand is a true integer, not a float — there is no register form. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fild - load integer onto the FP stack
column6: Reads a 16-, 32-, or 64-bit signed integer from memory, converts it to extended-precision floating point, and pushes it as the new `ST(0)`. It is the standard way to bring integer data into x87 math. **The source is always a memory operand and is interpreted as signed two's complement — there is no immediate or register form, and no unsigned variant**, so load an unsigned 32-bit value into a 64-bit slot to keep it positive. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fincstp - increment x87 stack-top pointer
column6: Increments the TOP field of the x87 status word (modulo 8), rotating which register is `ST(0)` without touching any data or tags. **It is not a pop — it does not mark the vacated register empty in the tag word, so the old `ST(0)` value persists and the slot stays tagged in-use.** Use a real pop (or `ffree`+`fincstp`) when you intend to discard a value. It clears `C1`; integer EFLAGS are unaffected.

column5: finit - initialize the x87 FPU
column6: Resets the x87 FPU to a known state: it sets the control word to the default (round-to-nearest, all exceptions masked, 64-bit precision), clears the status word, and empties the tag word so the whole register stack is free. `fninit` is the no-wait form that skips checking for pending unmasked exceptions. **It does not zero the data registers — it just marks them empty via the tag word**, and it silently discards any pending exception state (especially with `fninit`). No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fist - store ST(0) as an integer
column6: Rounds `ST(0)` to an integer using the current FPU rounding mode and stores it to a 16- or 32-bit (64-bit for `fistp`) memory location; `fistp` also pops the stack. **The rounding follows the control word, which defaults to round-to-nearest-even, NOT C-style truncation** — to truncate you must either change the rounding mode or use `fisttp`. An out-of-range or NaN value stores the integer indefinite and signals invalid-operation. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fisttp - store integer with truncation and pop
column6: Converts `ST(0)` to a 16-, 32-, or 64-bit integer using truncation (round-toward-zero), stores it to memory, and pops the stack — regardless of the current rounding mode. It is the SSE3-era fix for C's truncating float-to-int cast. **Unlike `fist`/`fistp`, it ignores the FPU rounding-mode field and always truncates**, so you no longer have to save, set, and restore the control word around a cast. It requires SSE3 support. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fld - push a floating-point value
column6: Loads a single-, double-, or extended-precision float from memory (or copies an existing `ST(i)`) and pushes it as the new `ST(0)`, converting to the internal 80-bit format on the way in. **When the source is a register, remember the stack shifts after the push: `fld st(0)` duplicates the top, but `fld st(1)` references the slot before TOP moves**, an off-by-one that trips people building stack rotations. Loading a too-many-values stack signals a stack-overflow exception. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fldcw - load the x87 control word
column6: Loads a 16-bit value from memory into the x87 control word, setting the rounding mode, precision control, and exception masks. It is how you switch rounding behavior or unmask FP exceptions at runtime. **Loading a control word that unmasks an exception whose status flag is already set will trigger that exception on the very next FP instruction**, so clear pending flags with `fclex` first; also remember to save and restore the old control word around temporary changes. No EFLAGS are affected.

column5: fldenv - load x87 FPU environment from memory
column6: Loads the x87 environment (control word, status word, tag word, instruction and data pointers, and last opcode) from a 14- or 28-byte memory image previously written by `fstenv`/`fnstenv`. It does **not** restore the eight data registers `st(0)`-`st(7)` — only the control/status block. **Operand size of the memory image depends on the current mode (16- vs 32-bit), so a frame saved in one mode must be restored in the same mode; and any exception bits you load that are unmasked can fire on the next FPU instruction.**

column5: not an instruction - title fragment
column6: Not an instruction. `floating` / `Point Values` is a wrapped title fragment from the source PDF. **No mnemonic, nothing to encode.**

column5: fmul - multiply floating-point on the x87 stack
column6: Multiplies two floating-point values on the x87 register stack, storing the product in the destination. `fmulp` multiplies then pops `st(0)`, and `fimul` multiplies `st(0)` by an integer loaded from memory. With no operands `fmul` multiplies `st(0)` by `st(1)`. **Remember the stack semantics: `fmulp st(1), st(0)` collapses two slots into one, so mismatching your push/pop count silently corrupts the stack and eventually raises a stack-overflow/underflow via the C1 status bit.**

column5: fnop - x87 no-operation
column6: Performs no operation and only advances the instruction pointer; it touches no x87 register, the stack, or the status word. It exists mainly for padding or timing inside FPU code streams. **Unlike the integer `nop`, this is a real x87 instruction that updates the FPU instruction-pointer field, so it is not interchangeable with `nop` for alignment of non-FPU code.**

column5: not an instruction - pseudocode fragment
column6: Not an instruction. This duplicate `for j := 0 to kl` line is the same AVX-512 per-lane loop pseudocode pulled from a manual. **There is no opcode for it** - ignore it when generating assembler reference data.

column5: AMX pseudocode fragment - not an instruction
column6: A duplicate pseudocode loop header lifted from an AMX tile-operation specification; it describes per-row iteration over a tile and is not a real opcode. **There is nothing to encode here — it is documentation of an AMX matrix instruction's inner loop, with no operands, no immediate, and no effect on any flags.**

column5: fpatan - partial arctangent of st(1)/st(0)
column6: Computes `atan(st(1) / st(0))`, stores the result in `st(1)`, and pops the stack so the angle ends up in `st(0)`. Because it takes both operands it correctly returns a full-range result in (-pi, +pi], making it the standard way to implement `atan2(y, x)` with `y` in `st(1)` and `x` in `st(0)`. **Mind the operand order — `y` must be pushed first so it lands in `st(1)`; swapping them gives you the cotangent angle, not the angle you wanted.**

column5: fprem - partial remainder of st(0)/st(1)
column6: Computes the remainder of `st(0)` divided by `st(1)` using truncation (round-toward-zero) and leaves it in `st(0)`. It is *partial*: for large quotients it may reduce only part of the magnitude per execution, setting the C2 condition bit to indicate the reduction is incomplete. **You must loop on `fprem` and re-test C2 in the status word until it clears; a single `fprem` does not guarantee a fully reduced remainder. Prefer `fprem1` for IEEE-compliant range reduction.**

column5: fprem1 - IEEE partial remainder of st(0)/st(1)
column6: Computes the IEEE-754-compliant remainder of `st(0)` divided by `st(1)` (rounding the quotient to nearest-even) and stores it in `st(0)`. Like `fprem` it is partial and signals incomplete reduction through the C2 condition bit. **Loop while C2 is set to finish the reduction; and use `fprem1` rather than `fprem` whenever you need true IEEE remainder semantics for argument reduction in transcendental functions.**

column5: fptan - partial tangent of st(0)
column6: Replaces `st(0)` with `tan(st(0))` (angle in radians) and then pushes the constant `1.0` onto the stack, leaving the tangent in `st(1)` and `1.0` in `st(0)` to support division-based identities. **The argument must lie within roughly +/-2^63; out of range, C2 is set and `st(0)` is left unchanged — you must reduce the angle (e.g. via `fprem1` against pi) first. Also remember the extra `1.0` push, which advances the stack one slot.**

column5: frndint - round st(0) to integer
column6: Rounds `st(0)` to an integral value according to the current rounding mode set in the x87 control word, leaving the result (still in floating-point format) in `st(0)`. **The direction of rounding is governed entirely by the RC field of the control word, not by the instruction — if you need a specific behavior (truncate, round-to-nearest, floor, ceil) you must set the control word yourself; the inexact exception is raised when the value actually changes.**

column5: frstor - restore full x87 FPU state from memory
column6: Restores the complete x87 state — environment plus all eight data registers `st(0)`-`st(7)` — from a 94- or 108-byte memory image previously written by `fsave`/`fnsave`. This fully reconstructs the FPU as it was at save time. **The image size and layout depend on operating mode (16- vs 32-bit), so save and restore must use the same mode; and any unmasked pending exceptions loaded from the image may trigger on the next FPU instruction.**

column5: fsave - save full x87 FPU state to memory
column6: Writes the entire x87 state (environment and all eight data registers) to a 94/108-byte memory block and then re-initializes the FPU as if by `finit`. `fsave` first checks for and handles pending unmasked exceptions; `fnsave` skips that check (no preceding `fwait`). **Note the side effect: after `fsave` the FPU is reset, so the saved registers are gone from hardware — you must `frstor` to get them back. Match the save/restore mode (16- vs 32-bit) and ensure the buffer is large enough.**

column5: fscale - scale st(0) by 2^trunc(st(1))
column6: Multiplies `st(0)` by `2` raised to the truncated integer value of `st(1)`, i.e. `st(0) = st(0) * 2^trunc(st(1))`, which is a fast exponent adjustment with no rounding error. **`st(1)` is truncated toward zero, not rounded, and it is not consumed — it stays on the stack — so this differs from a normal pop-style operation; pair it with `fxtract`/`fyl2x` when building `exp2`/`pow` and remember to clean up `st(1)` yourself.**

column5: fsin - sine of st(0)
column6: Replaces `st(0)` with `sin(st(0))`, where the argument is interpreted as radians. **The argument must be within about +/-2^63 in magnitude; if it exceeds that range, C2 is set in the status word and `st(0)` is left unchanged, so you must range-reduce the angle (typically with `fprem1` modulo 2*pi) before relying on the result.**

column5: fsincos - sine and cosine of st(0)
column6: Computes both `sin(st(0))` and `cos(st(0))` in one operation: it replaces `st(0)` with the sine and pushes the cosine, leaving sine in `st(1)` and cosine in `st(0)`. This is cheaper than separate `fsin` and `fcos` when you need both. **The same +/-2^63 argument-range limit applies — out of range sets C2 and leaves the stack untouched — and remember it pushes a new slot, so the cosine ends up on top, not the sine.**

column5: fsqrt - square root of st(0)
column6: Replaces `st(0)` with its square root, computed in extended precision and correctly rounded per the current rounding mode. **A negative non-zero operand raises the invalid-operation exception (and yields a QNaN if masked); negative zero returns negative zero. Unlike SSE `sqrtsd`, this operates only on the top of the x87 stack, so the value must already be in `st(0)`.**

column5: fst - store st(0) to register or memory
column6: Copies `st(0)` to another x87 register or to a single/double-precision memory location, converting precision on the way out. `fstp` does the same and then pops the stack. **A plain `fst` to a 32- or 64-bit memory target rounds the 80-bit value down and can raise precision/overflow exceptions; and forgetting `fstp` (using `fst`) leaves the value on the stack, which eventually overflows the eight-slot register file.**

column5: fstcw - store x87 FPU control word to memory
column6: Stores the 16-bit x87 control word (rounding-control, precision-control, and exception masks) into a memory operand. `fstcw` waits for pending unmasked exceptions first; `fnstcw` stores without checking. **Typical idiom: `fnstcw` into memory, modify the RC bits, `fldcw` it back to change rounding for a single computation, then restore — and don't forget to `fldcw` the original value back, or the rounding mode change leaks into the rest of your code.**

column5: fstenv - store x87 FPU environment to memory
column6: Writes the x87 environment block (control, status, and tag words plus the instruction/data pointers and last opcode) to a 14/28-byte memory image, leaving the data registers in place. `fstenv` checks pending unmasked exceptions first; `fnstenv` does not. **A side effect of `fstenv`/`fnstenv` is that it masks all FPU exceptions after storing, so re-enable any you need afterward; and the image layout is mode-dependent (16- vs 32-bit).**

column5: fstsw - store x87 FPU status word
column6: Stores the 16-bit x87 status word to a memory operand or to `ax`. `fstsw` first checks pending unmasked exceptions; `fnstsw` skips the wait. The classic use is `fnstsw ax` followed by `sahf` so the FPU condition codes C0/C2/C3 land in CF/PF/ZF for an ordinary `jcc`. **Only `ax` is a legal register destination (not a general 32/64-bit register), and you must route C0-C3 through the flags with `sahf` before integer conditional jumps will see the comparison result.**

column5: fsub - floating-point subtract
column6: Computes destination = destination - source on the x87 stack. `fsubp` subtracts then pops `st(0)`, and `fisub` subtracts an integer loaded from memory. With no operands it does `st(0) = st(0) - st(1)`. **Watch the operand order against `fsubr`, and note that `fsubp st(1), st(0)` computes `st(1) - st(0)` into `st(1)` before popping — the destination is the minuend, so the non-commutative direction is easy to flip.**

column5: fsubr - reverse floating-point subtract
column6: Computes the *reversed* difference: destination = source - destination, the opposite operand order of `fsub`. `fsubrp` reverses, subtracts, then pops; `fisubr` reverses with an integer memory operand. **The whole point is the swapped order — `fsubr st(0), st(1)` yields `st(1) - st(0)` — so picking `fsub` vs `fsubr` is easy to get backwards; double-check which operand you mean to be the minuend.**

column5: ftst - compare st(0) against +0.0
column6: Compares `st(0)` with `+0.0` and sets the x87 condition codes C0/C2/C3 accordingly, without changing the stack. It is the floating-point analog of testing a value's sign and zeroness. **The result lands in the FPU status word, not the EFLAGS — you must follow with `fnstsw ax` and `sahf` (or `fcomi`) before a conditional jump can read it. If `st(0)` is a NaN, C2 (unordered) is set rather than a clean less/greater answer.**

column5: fucom - unordered compare of st(0) and st(i)
column6: Compares `st(0)` with another x87 register and sets condition codes C0/C2/C3; unlike `fcom`, it treats QNaN operands as merely unordered rather than raising invalid-operation. `fucomp` pops once, `fucompp` pops twice. **Results go to the FPU status word, so route them through `fnstsw ax`/`sahf` before branching; and only QNaNs are quiet here — an SNaN operand still raises the invalid-operation exception.**

column5: fxam - examine and classify st(0)
column6: Examines `st(0)` and reports its class — zero, normal, denormal, infinity, NaN, empty, or unsupported — along with its sign, by setting the C0/C1/C2/C3 condition codes. It does not change the stack. **The classification is encoded across all four condition bits, so you must read the full C0/C2/C3 pattern (sign in C1) via `fnstsw ax` and decode it; a single flag test is not enough to distinguish the categories.**

column5: fxch - exchange st(0) with another x87 register
column6: Swaps the contents of `st(0)` with `st(i)` (or `st(1)` if no operand is given), letting you bring any stack slot to the top for instructions that only operate on `st(0)`. It sets C1 to 0 and is effectively free on most hardware. **It is the standard tool for reordering operands on the rigidly stack-based FPU, but each `fxch` is real data movement in your mental model — losing track of which logical value sits in `st(0)` is the most common source of x87 stack bugs.**

column5: fxrstor - restore x87, MMX, XMM, MXCSR state
column6: Restores x87/MMX registers, the XMM registers, and MXCSR from a 512-byte memory image previously written by `fxsave`. It is the legacy combined FP/SSE state restore used in context switches. **The memory operand must be 16-byte aligned or it faults, and the image must come from a matching `fxsave`; reserved MXCSR bits in the image must be zero or `fxrstor` raises a general-protection fault. It does not restore AVX (`ymm`/`zmm`) upper state — use `xrstor` for that.**

column5: fxsave - save x87, MMX, XMM, MXCSR state
column6: Saves the x87/MMX register file, the XMM registers, and MXCSR into a 512-byte memory image in one instruction, for fast context save. **The destination must be 16-byte aligned or the instruction faults; it captures only legacy FP/SSE state, not the AVX upper halves (`ymm`/`zmm`) — use `xsave` with the appropriate state mask if you need to preserve AVX. Pair it with `fxrstor` using the same alignment and layout.**
