No flags touched.**

column5: phsubw/phsubd - horizontal subtract adjacent word/dword
column6: Subtracts the second element of each horizontally adjacent pair from the first — words for `phsubw`, dwords for `phsubd` — and packs the wrapping differences, low half from the destination and high half from the source. **Differences wrap on overflow (use `phsubsw` for saturation), the subtraction is first-minus-second per pair, and the cross-lane interleave means the result layout is not a straight element map. Flags unchanged.**

column5: pinsrb/d/q - insert a byte/dword/qword into a lane
column6: Inserts a byte, dword, or qword from a general-purpose register or memory into the immediate-selected lane of the destination XMM register, leaving the other lanes intact. **Only the lane named by the immediate is written — all other bytes of the destination are preserved, so this is a read-modify-write of the vector, not a zeroing move. The index is taken modulo the lane count and no flags change.**

column5: pinsrw - insert one word into a selected lane
column6: Inserts the low 16 bits of a general-purpose register or a memory word into the immediate-selected word lane of the destination XMM register, leaving the other seven words unchanged. **Only the bottom 16 bits of a GPR source are used and only the chosen lane is modified — the rest of the register is preserved. The 3-bit index selects the lane and no flags are affected.**

column5: pmaddubsw - multiply unsigned*signed bytes, add pairs
column6: Multiplies each *unsigned* byte of the destination by the corresponding *signed* byte of the source, then horizontally adds adjacent product pairs into saturated signed 16-bit words. **The operand roles are asymmetric — the first operand is treated as unsigned, the second as signed — so order matters, and the pairwise sums saturate to signed 16-bit. No flags affected.**

column5: pmaddwd - multiply signed words, add adjacent pairs
column6: Multiplies the signed 16-bit words of the two operands lane-by-lane, then adds each adjacent pair of 32-bit products to produce signed dword results. **All inputs are treated as signed and the pairwise sum is full 32-bit with no saturation — the only case that can overflow is two `-0x8000` inputs in both lanes of a pair, which wraps. Commonly used for dot products. No flags touched.**

column5: pmaxsb/w/d/q - per-lane max of signed integers
column6: Writes the per-lane maximum of the two operands, treating elements as signed, with byte/word/dword/qword granularity across the four forms. **Comparison is signed — pair these with the `pmaxu*` family for unsigned data. The `b`/`d` forms are SSE4.1, `w` is SSE2, and `pmaxsq` requires AVX-512. No flags are affected.**

column5: pmaxub/pmaxuw - per-lane max of unsigned ints
column6: Stores the per-lane maximum of the two operands treating elements as unsigned, with `pmaxub` on bytes and `pmaxuw` on words. **Comparison is unsigned, so use the `pmaxs*` forms instead when your data is signed. `pmaxub` dates to SSE2 while `pmaxuw` needs SSE4.1. No flags touched.**

column5: pmaxud/pmaxuq - per-lane max of unsigned ints
column6: Writes the per-lane unsigned maximum of the operands, with `pmaxud` on 32-bit dwords and `pmaxuq` on 64-bit qwords. **Comparison is unsigned; switch to `pmaxsd`/`pmaxsq` for signed data. `pmaxud` is SSE4.1 and `pmaxuq` is AVX-512. No flags affected.**

column5: pminsb/pminsw - per-lane min of signed integers
column6: Stores the per-lane minimum of the two operands treating elements as signed, with byte granularity for `pminsb` and word for `pminsw`. **Comparison is signed — use the `pminu*` forms for unsigned data. `pminsw` is SSE2, `pminsb` is SSE4.1. No flags affected.**

column5: pminsd/pminsq - per-lane min of signed integers
column6: Writes the per-lane signed minimum of the operands, with `pminsd` on 32-bit dwords and `pminsq` on 64-bit qwords. **Comparison is signed; reach for `pminud`/`pminuq` when your data is unsigned. `pminsd` is SSE4.1 and `pminsq` is AVX-512. No flags touched.**

column5: pminub/pminuw - per-lane min of unsigned integers
column6: Stores the per-lane minimum of the two operands treating elements as unsigned, with `pminub` on bytes and `pminuw` on words. **Comparison is unsigned — use the `pmins*` forms for signed data. `pminub` is SSE2 while `pminuw` is SSE4.1. No flags affected.**

column5: pminud/pminuq - per-lane min of unsigned integers
column6: Writes the per-lane unsigned minimum of the operands, with `pminud` on 32-bit dwords and `pminuq` on 64-bit qwords. **Comparison is unsigned; choose `pminsd`/`pminsq` for signed data. `pminud` is SSE4.1 and `pminuq` is AVX-512. No flags touched.**

column5: pmovmskb - gather byte sign bits into a GPR mask
column6: Collects the most-significant bit of each byte in the source XMM register and packs them into the low 16 bits of a general-purpose register, zeroing the upper bits. **This is the standard way to turn a packed compare result into a branchable bitmask — one bit per byte lane — that you can then `test`, `bsf`, or `popcnt`. The destination's high bits are cleared and no flags are set.**

column5: pmovsx - sign-extend narrow lanes to wider lanes
column6: Sign-extends each element of the (memory or low-half register) source to a wider lane in the destination, in forms named by the source/destination widths such as `pmovsxbw`, `pmovsxbd`, `pmovsxwd`, `pmovsxdq`, and so on. **It reads only as many low bytes of the source as it needs to fill the destination and sign-fills the new high bits — pick the correct width-pair suffix, and remember a memory source is only partially read. No flags affected.**

column5: pmovzx - zero-extend narrow lanes to wider lanes
column6: Zero-extends each element of the source to a wider lane in the destination, in width-pair forms like `pmovzxbw`, `pmovzxbd`, `pmovzxwd`, `pmovzxdq`, etc. **It consumes only the low portion of the source needed to fill the destination and fills the upper bits with zeros — choose the correct suffix and note a memory operand is read only partially. Use this instead of `pmovsx` for unsigned widening. No flags touched.**

column5: pmuldq - signed 32x32->64 packed multiply
column6: Multiplies the even-indexed (lanes 0 and 2) signed doubleword elements of the source and destination, producing full 64-bit signed products stored as packed quadwords. The odd doublewords (lanes 1 and 3) are ignored as inputs. **Only the even-numbered 32-bit elements are read** — if you want the odd ones you must shuffle them down first; flags are untouched.

column5: pmulhrsw - packed signed mul, round, high word
column6: Multiplies packed signed words, takes bits [30:15] of each 32-bit product, adds 1 and shifts right by 1 to round, yielding a rounded high-word result per lane. This is the fixed-point Q15 multiply used in audio/DSP. **It is not a plain high-word multiply** — the rounding bias means results differ from `pmulhw`, and no flags are affected.

column5: pmulhuw - unsigned mul, keep high words
column6: Multiplies packed unsigned 16-bit words and stores the high 16 bits of each 32-bit product back into the lanes. Pair it with `pmullw` if you need the full product. **Inputs are treated as unsigned** — use `pmulhw` for signed words; this instruction modifies no flags.

column5: pmulhw - signed mul, keep high words
column6: Multiplies packed signed 16-bit words and stores the high 16 bits of each signed 32-bit product. Combine with `pmullw` to reconstruct full products. **Signedness matters** — the high half differs from `pmulhuw` for negative inputs, and flags are left unchanged.

column5: pmulld - packed 32-bit mul, keep low dwords
column6: Multiplies packed doubleword integers and stores the low 32 bits of each product, discarding overflow (`pmullq` does the same for 64-bit quadwords). Sign of the inputs is irrelevant since the low half is identical for signed and unsigned. **High bits are silently dropped** — there is no high-result form, so use `pmuldq`/`pmuludq` if you need full 64-bit products; no flags change.

column5: pmullw - packed 16-bit mul, keep low words
column6: Multiplies packed 16-bit words and stores the low 16 bits of each 32-bit product. Sign does not matter for the low half. **Overflow is discarded** — pair with `pmulhw` (signed) or `pmulhuw` (unsigned) to recover the high words; touches no flags.

column5: pmuludq - unsigned 32x32->64 packed multiply
column6: Multiplies the even-indexed (lanes 0 and 2) unsigned doubleword elements and stores full 64-bit unsigned products as packed quadwords. The odd doublewords are not read. **Only the even 32-bit elements participate** — shuffle odd lanes into even positions if you need them; this is the unsigned counterpart of `pmuldq` and sets no flags.

column5: pop - pop a value off the stack
column6: Loads the value at the top of the stack into the destination register or memory operand and increments `rsp` by the operand size (2, 4, or 8 bytes). In 64-bit mode the default operand size is 8. **`pop rsp` and using `rsp` in a memory-operand pop have order-sensitive semantics**, and popping into a segment register can fault; the instruction itself leaves flags unchanged (use `popf` to restore flags).

column5: popa - pop all GPRs (legacy 32-bit only)
column6: Pops eight general-purpose registers off the stack in one shot (the saved `esp` slot is discarded rather than loaded). `popad` is the 32-bit form. **This instruction is invalid in 64-bit mode** — it raises #UD, so in long mode you must pop registers individually; flags are unaffected.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: popcnt - count set bits (population count)
column6: Counts the number of 1 bits in the source operand and writes the total to the destination register. It requires the POPCNT CPUID feature bit. **`ZF` is set only when the source was zero and every other flag (`CF PF AF SF OF`) is cleared** — do not test `CF`/`SF` as if this were a normal arithmetic op, and beware the false dependency on the destination on some CPUs.

column5: popf - pop stack into the flags register
column6: Pops a word/dword/qword off the stack into the flags register, restoring `CF PF AF ZF SF OF DF` and others. `popfq` pops 8 bytes in 64-bit mode. **This is the canonical way flags get clobbered wholesale** — certain bits (IOPL, VM, VIP, VIF) are only updated at sufficient privilege and are otherwise ignored, so pair it with `pushf` and don't rely on it to set system bits in user code.

column5: por - bitwise OR of packed data
column6: Computes the bitwise OR across the full width of the destination and source registers, with no lane boundaries since the operation is purely bit-for-bit. Commonly used to merge masked results. **It is a whole-register logical op, not element-aware** — element size is irrelevant, and it modifies no flags.

column5: precision floating - PDF extraction artifact
column6: **This is not a real instruction.** It is the same scraping artifact, a sliver of the phrase "...precision floating-point value" that became its own row by mistake. There is no opcode to emit. **Discard this entry; it is noise, not an instruction.**

column5: prefetchh - prefetch line with locality hint
column6: Prefetches the cache line containing the addressed byte into the cache hierarchy according to a temporal-locality hint (T0/T1/T2/NTA select the target level). The address is never checked, so a bad pointer cannot fault here. **It is advisory only** — correctness must never depend on it, it modifies no register or flag, and the hint can be ignored by the CPU.

column5: prefetchw - prefetch a line for writing
column6: Hints the processor to fetch the cache line containing the addressed byte into the cache hierarchy, anticipating a subsequent write (often bringing the line into an exclusive/modified state). It is a hint only and never faults on a bad address. **It has no architectural effect and cannot be relied on for correctness** — it only affects performance, touches no register or flag, and may be a no-op if the CPU lacks the feature.

column5: prefetchwt1 - prefetch for write, T1 hint
column6: Prefetches the cache line containing the addressed byte with intent-to-write and a temporal locality hint targeting the L2 (T1) level. Like all prefetches it is advisory and never faults. **It is purely a performance hint** — it changes no architectural state, no flags, and is silently ignored on CPUs without the feature.

column5: psadbw - sum of absolute byte differences
column6: Computes the absolute difference of each pair of unsigned bytes, then horizontally sums groups of eight into a 16-bit result placed in the low word of each 64-bit lane (the rest of the lane is zeroed). It is the workhorse of motion estimation and fast byte reductions. **The result lives only in the low 16 bits of each quadword lane** — the upper bits are cleared, and no flags are affected.

column5: pshufb - byte shuffle by index vector
column6: Permutes the destination bytes using indices taken from the source: each source byte's low nibble selects which input byte to copy. **If the high bit (bit 7) of a control byte is set, that output byte is forced to zero**, which is a deliberate way to mask lanes; indexing is per-128-bit lane in the wider forms, and no flags change.

column5: pshufd - shuffle packed doublewords by imm8
column6: Rearranges the four 32-bit doublewords of the source into the destination according to a 2-bits-per-lane immediate, so any input dword can be routed to any output position (including duplication). **The selector is a compile-time `imm8`, not a register** — each 2-bit field picks a source lane, and in 256-bit form the same pattern applies within each 128-bit half; flags are untouched.

column5: pshufhw - shuffle the high four words
column6: Shuffles only the high four 16-bit words of each 128-bit lane using a 2-bits-per-element immediate, copying the low four words through unchanged. **It leaves the low quadword alone** — use `pshuflw` for the low words; the selector is an `imm8` and no flags are affected.

column5: pshuflw - shuffle the low four words
column6: Shuffles only the low four 16-bit words of each 128-bit lane via a 2-bits-per-element immediate, passing the high four words through unchanged. **It is the mirror of `pshufhw` and leaves the high quadword intact** — the control is a compile-time `imm8`, and flags are not modified.

column5: pshufw - shuffle four words in an MMX register
column6: Rearranges the four 16-bit words of a 64-bit MMX register according to a 2-bits-per-lane immediate. This is the legacy MMX shuffle. **It operates on `mm` registers, so it puts the FPU/MMX state in use** (issue `emms` before float code) and uses an `imm8` selector; no flags are touched.

column5: psignb - apply sign of source to dest lanes
column6: For each byte (word/dword) lane, negates the destination element when the corresponding source element is negative, zeroes it when the source is zero, and leaves it unchanged when the source is positive. **A source lane of zero forces that result lane to zero**, not just unchanged sign — handy for conditional negate but easy to forget; no flags are affected.

column5: pslldq - byte-shift whole 128-bit lane left
column6: Shifts each 128-bit lane left by a whole number of BYTES given by the immediate, filling the low end with zeros. Despite the name it is a byte shift, not a bit shift. **The count is in bytes and comes from an `imm8`, not `cl`** — a count >= 16 zeroes the lane, and no flags change.

column5: psllw - logical left shift of packed elements
column6: Shifts each packed word (`pslld` dword, `psllq` quadword) left by the same bit count, taken from an `imm8` or the low bytes of an XMM/MMX source, shifting zeros in. **A shift count greater than or equal to the element width produces all zeros (it is not masked like GPR shifts)** — the count register here is a vector operand, never `cl`, and flags are unaffected.

column5: psraw - arithmetic right shift of packed elements
column6: Shifts each packed word (`psrad` dword, `psraq` quadword) right by a common count, replicating the sign bit into the vacated high bits. **There is no `psraq` on older SSE and no arithmetic-right form for bytes or quadwords pre-AVX-512** — counts at or above the element width saturate to all-sign-bits rather than wrapping, and no flags change.

column5: psrldq - byte-shift whole 128-bit lane right
column6: Shifts each 128-bit lane right by a whole number of BYTES from the immediate, filling the high end with zeros. Like `pslldq` this is a byte shift in spite of the 'dq' name. **The count is bytes from an `imm8`, not `cl`** — a count >= 16 clears the lane, and flags are left alone.

column5: psrlw - logical right shift of packed elements
column6: Shifts each packed word (`psrld` dword, `psrlq` quadword) right by a shared count from an `imm8` or an XMM/MMX source, shifting zeros into the high bits. **Counts >= the element width yield all zeros and are not masked** — the count is a vector/immediate operand rather than `cl`, and no flags are modified.

column5: psubb - subtract packed integers (wraparound)
column6: Subtracts packed bytes (`psubw` words, `psubd` dwords) lane-by-lane with two's-complement wraparound on overflow. Signedness is irrelevant since wrapping results are identical. **Overflow wraps silently with no saturation and no flags** — use the `psubs*`/`psubus*` forms if you need clamping.

column5: psubq - subtract packed quadwords (wraparound)
column6: Subtracts packed 64-bit quadword integers with two's-complement wraparound. There are one or two lanes depending on register width. **Overflow wraps and is not flagged** — there is no saturating quadword subtract, so handle large-magnitude cases yourself.

column5: psubsb - signed saturating subtract
column6: Subtracts packed signed bytes (`psubsw` signed words) and clamps each result to the signed range, so underflow pins to the minimum (e.g. 0x80) and overflow to the maximum (e.g. 0x7F). **Results saturate instead of wrapping** — this differs from `psubb`/`psubw`, and the operation sets no flags.

column5: psubusb - unsigned saturating subtract
column6: Subtracts packed unsigned bytes (`psubusw` unsigned words) and clamps each result to the unsigned range, so any borrow pins the lane to zero rather than wrapping. **Underflow saturates to 0, never wraps to a large value** — choose this over `psubb` when you need non-negative clamped differences; no flags are affected.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: ptest - test bitwise AND of two vectors
column6: Computes the bitwise AND of source and destination to set `ZF` (set when the AND is all zero), then the AND of the source with the bitwise-NOT of the destination to set `CF` (set when that is all zero); `AF PF SF OF` are cleared. **It uses `ZF` and `CF` together, not the usual sign/overflow flags** — test `jz` for 'no common bits' and `jc` for 'all dest bits covered by src', and remember the destination is read-only here.

column5: ptwrite - write data to a PT trace packet
column6: Writes the value of the source operand into a Processor Trace PTWRITE packet, letting software embed arbitrary data into the hardware trace stream. It is gated by enable bits in the PT MSRs and CPUID. **It does nothing visible unless PT and PTWRITE are enabled** — when disabled it may #UD or no-op depending on configuration, and it affects no general registers or flags.

column5: push - push a value onto the stack
column6: Decrements `rsp` by the operand size and stores the source (register, memory, or sign-extended immediate) at the new top of stack. In 64-bit mode the default and effective push size is 8 bytes. **You cannot push 32-bit GPRs in long mode (pushes are 16- or 64-bit), and `push imm` sign-extends to the operand size** — flags are unchanged (use `pushf` to save flags).

column5: pusha - push all GPRs (legacy 32-bit only)
column6: Pushes all eight general-purpose registers onto the stack in a fixed order in one instruction; `pushad` is the 32-bit form. **This instruction is invalid in 64-bit mode and raises #UD** — in long mode you must save registers individually or via the ABI; flags are not affected.

column5: pushf - push the flags register onto the stack
column6: Pushes the flags register onto the stack (`pushfq` pushes 8 bytes in 64-bit mode), the standard way to snapshot `CF PF AF ZF SF OF DF` before code that clobbers them. **In 64-bit mode the VM and RF bits read as zero in the pushed image**, and the privileged system bits behave specially — pair it with `popf`/`popfq` to restore; it does not itself modify flags.

column5: pxor - bitwise XOR of packed data
column6: Computes the bitwise XOR across the whole width of destination and source with no regard for element boundaries. `pxor xmm, xmm` with the same register is the idiomatic way to zero a vector register. **It is a whole-register op, so element size is irrelevant, and it touches no flags** — note self-XOR for zeroing does not break dependency chains the way some other zeroing idioms do on every microarchitecture.

column5: rcl - rotate left through carry flag
column6: Rotates the destination left treating `CF` as an extra high bit, so each shifted-out top bit enters `CF` and the old `CF` re-enters at the bottom — a 9/17/33/65-bit rotate including carry. The count is an immediate or `cl`. **The count register is `cl` only, and it is masked to 5 bits (6 for 64-bit operands); `OF` is defined only for a count of 1 and undefined otherwise** — chain `rcl` across words for multi-precision shifts.

column5: rcpps - approximate packed single reciprocals
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal `1/x` for each of the four packed single-precision floats in the source and writes them to the destination `xmm`. This is a fast estimate intended for code that then refines the result with a Newton-Raphson step. **The approximation has a relative error of up to about 2^-12, so it is not bit-exact and never produces a correctly rounded `1.0/x` — do not use it where IEEE-precise division is required.** Touches no flags.

column5: rcpss - approximate scalar single reciprocal
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal `1/x` of the low single-precision float in the source and writes it to the low dword of the destination, leaving the upper three lanes of `xmm` unchanged. Like its packed sibling it is an estimate meant to seed a refinement step. **Accuracy is only ~2^-12 relative error, so it is not a substitute for `divss` when you need a correctly rounded quotient.** Affects no flags.

column5: rcr - rotate right through carry flag
column6: Rotates the destination right through `CF`, so the low bit shifts into `CF` while the old `CF` enters at the top, giving a carry-inclusive rotate one bit wider than the operand. **The count comes only from `cl` (or `imm8`) and is masked to 5 bits, 6 for 64-bit operands; `OF` is meaningful only when the count is 1** — it is the complement of `rcl` for multi-word right shifts.

column5: rdfsbase/rdgsbase - read FS/GS base register
column6: Reads the FS (or GS) segment base address into a general-purpose register `r32`/`r64` without a kernel round-trip. Commonly used by thread-local storage runtimes to fetch the per-thread base quickly. **The instruction only executes when `CR4.FSGSBASE` is enabled by the OS; otherwise it raises `#UD`. It is 64-bit-mode only.** No flags are affected.

column5: rdmsr - read model-specific register
column6: Reads the 64-bit model-specific register selected by `ecx`, returning the low 32 bits in `eax` and the high 32 bits in `edx`. Used to query CPU control, performance, and feature registers. **It is privileged (CPL 0 only) and faults with `#GP` from user mode or on a reserved/unimplemented MSR index, and the result lands split across `edx:eax`, not a single 64-bit register.** Affects no flags.

column5: rdmsrlist - read a list of MSRs
column6: Bulk-reads multiple model-specific registers in one instruction, taking a table of MSR indices and a bitmap of which entries to process from memory pointed to by `rsi`, and storing the values to the `rdi` table. Intended to speed up context switches that touch many MSRs. **It is privileged (CPL 0) and requires the dedicated MSRLIST feature; the index and data tables must be properly aligned per the spec.** No flags are affected.

column5: rdpid - read processor ID
column6: Reads the processor ID value (the `TSC_AUX` MSR) into a destination general-purpose register, giving software a cheap way to learn which logical CPU it is running on. Often used by per-CPU data structures and vDSO helpers. **In 64-bit mode the destination is always a full 64-bit register but only the low 32 bits are written; availability is unprivileged but gated by `CR4.TSD`-independent enabling, so check the CPUID feature bit first.** Touches no flags.

column5: rdpkru - read protection-key rights register
column6: Reads the 32-bit PKRU (user-page protection-key rights) register into `eax`, with `edx` cleared. The PKRU controls per-key read/write disable bits used by memory protection keys. **`ecx` must be 0 on entry or the instruction faults with `#GP`, and the feature requires `CR4.PKE` to be set; it does not change `edx` to anything but zero.** No flags are affected.

column5: rdpmc - read performance-monitoring counter
column6: Reads the performance-monitoring counter selected by `ecx` and returns its value in `edx:eax`. Used to sample event counts such as cache misses or retired instructions. **The result is split across `edx:eax`, and from user mode it only works if `CR4.PCE` permits it — otherwise it raises `#GP`; an invalid counter index also faults.** Affects no flags.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: rdrand - read hardware random number
column6: Loads a hardware-generated random value from the CPU's on-chip DRBG into the destination register `r16`/`r32`/`r64`, setting `CF=1` on success. **You must test `CF` after every call: `CF=0` means the entropy buffer was momentarily empty and the destination is zero, so loop and retry rather than trusting the value. `OF`, `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, and `PF` are cleared.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: rdseed - read hardware random seed
column6: Loads a hardware random value drawn directly from the raw entropy source (suitable for seeding other PRNGs) into the destination register, setting `CF=1` on success. It has higher entropy quality but lower throughput than `rdrand`. **Always check `CF`: `CF=0` means no seed was available this time and the destination is zero, so retry. The other arithmetic flags `OF`, `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, and `PF` are cleared.**

column5: rdsspd/rdsspq - read shadow stack pointer
column6: Reads the current shadow stack pointer (`SSP`) into a general-purpose register — `rdsspd` for the low 32 bits, `rdsspq` for the full 64. Used by CET-aware code to inspect the shadow stack. **If CET shadow stacks are not enabled the instruction executes as a `NOP` and leaves the destination unchanged, so do not assume it always returns a meaningful value.** No flags are affected.

column5: rdtsc - read time-stamp counter
column6: Reads the 64-bit time-stamp counter into `edx:eax`, providing a high-resolution cycle/time count. Widely used for micro-benchmarking. **The result is split across `edx:eax` (the upper bits of `rax`/`rdx` are zeroed), and because it does not serialize, the CPU may reorder it around your measured code — use `lfence`/`rdtscp` or a serializing instruction to fence it. It can also `#GP` from user mode if `CR4.TSD` is set.** No flags affected.

column5: rdtscp - read time-stamp counter and CPU ID
column6: Reads the 64-bit time-stamp counter into `edx:eax` and simultaneously loads the `TSC_AUX` processor-ID value into `ecx`, partially ordering itself against prior instructions. Useful for timing on systems where threads may migrate cores. **It waits for earlier instructions to retire but does not fence later ones, so add an `lfence` afterward for a tight measurement window; the count still arrives split across `edx:eax`.** No flags affected.

column5: rep - repeat string-operation prefix
column6: A prefix that repeats the following string instruction (`movs`, `stos`, `lods`, `scas`, `cmps`) using `rcx` as the iteration count, decrementing it each step; the `repe`/`repz` and `repne`/`repnz` forms additionally stop based on `ZF` for `cmps`/`scas`. **`rcx` is the loop counter, not a byte count for word/dword variants, and if `rcx` starts at 0 the operation executes zero times. Plain `rep` ignores `ZF`; only the conditional forms watch it, so pick the right suffix for compares.** The prefix itself sets no flags.

column5: ret - return from procedure
column6: Pops the return address off the stack into `rip` and transfers control there, optionally adding an immediate to `rsp` (`ret imm16`) to clean up caller-pushed arguments. **The stack pointer must point exactly at the saved return address — any imbalance from mismatched pushes/pops sends execution to a garbage address. Under the System V and Windows x64 ABIs the caller normally cleans the stack, so use plain `ret`, not `ret imm16`, unless you are matching a callee-cleanup convention.** No flags affected.

column5: rol - rotate left (no carry in the cycle)
column6: Rotates the destination bits left, wrapping the top bit around to the bottom and also copying it into `CF`. Unlike `rcl`, `CF` is not part of the rotated value. **Only `CF` and (for count 1) `OF` are touched — `SF`, `ZF`, etc. are left unchanged; the count is `cl`/`imm8` masked to 5 or 6 bits, and `OF` is undefined for counts other than 1.**

column5: ror - rotate right (no carry in the cycle)
column6: Rotates the destination bits right, wrapping the low bit around to the top and copying it into `CF`. `CF` is not part of the rotated word as it is with `rcr`. **It updates only `CF` and, for a count of 1, `OF`, leaving the arithmetic flags alone; the count is `cl` or `imm8` masked to 5 bits (6 for 64-bit), and `OF` is undefined when the count is not 1.**

column5: rorx - rotate right by immediate, no flags
column6: Rotates the source `r/m32`/`r/m64` right by an immediate count and writes the result to a separate destination register, a three-operand BMI2 form that does not overwrite the source. **Unlike the classic `ror`, this instruction leaves all flags untouched and the rotate count must be an immediate — there is no `cl` or register-count form. It needs BMI2 support.**

column5: roundpd - round packed doubles to integer
column6: Rounds each of the packed double-precision floats in the source to an integral value using the rounding mode in the immediate (nearest, down, up, truncate, or the current `MXCSR` mode) and writes the floating-point results to `xmm`. **The output is still a floating-point value, not an integer encoding — it does not convert to `int`. Bit 3 of the immediate suppresses the precision exception; choose the immediate's mode bits deliberately rather than assuming round-to-nearest.** No flags affected.

column5: roundps - round packed singles to integer
column6: Rounds each of the packed single-precision floats in the source to an integral value per the rounding mode in the immediate and stores the floating-point results to `xmm`. **The result remains a float, not an integer; for actual `float`-to-`int` conversion use `cvt*` instead. The immediate selects the rounding direction (or defers to `MXCSR`) and can mask the inexact exception, so set it explicitly.** Touches no flags.

column5: roundsd - round scalar double to integer
column6: Rounds the low double-precision float of the source to an integral value using the immediate's rounding mode and writes it to the low quadword of the destination, leaving the upper lane unchanged. **The result is a floating-point integral value, not an integer; and as a two-operand SSE form it copies the high half of the destination from the destination itself, so be mindful of which register supplies the upper bits. The immediate selects the rounding mode.** No flags affected.

column5: roundss - round scalar single to integer
column6: Rounds the low single-precision float of the source to an integral value per the immediate's rounding mode and writes it to the low dword of the destination, preserving the upper three lanes. **The output stays a float, not an integer encoding — use a `cvt*` instruction if you need an actual integer. The immediate picks the rounding direction or defers to `MXCSR`, so don't assume round-to-nearest.** Affects no flags.

column5: rsm - resume from System Management Mode
column6: Restores the saved processor state from SMRAM and returns from System Management Mode back to the interrupted context. It is the counterpart to an SMI entry and is used only inside SMM handlers. **It is legal exclusively while the CPU is in SMM; executing it anywhere else raises `#UD`. This is firmware-only and not something application or even normal kernel code ever issues.** Flag state is restored from the saved image rather than set by the instruction.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: rstorssp - restore saved shadow stack pointer
column6: Switches the active shadow stack by restoring `SSP` from a 'restore token' stored at the supplied memory address, used by CET when resuming a previously saved shadow stack (for example on a context switch or longjmp). **It validates the token before switching and faults if the token is invalid or shadow stacks are disabled; the memory operand must point at a properly formed restore token. CET must be enabled for this to be anything but `#UD`.** The arithmetic flags are left in an undefined/cleared state.

column5: sahf - store AH into low flags byte
column6: Copies bits of `ah` into the low byte of `RFLAGS`, loading `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, `PF`, and `CF` from `ah` while `OF` and the rest are unaffected. Paired with `lahf` to save/restore the condition codes through a register. **Only the low five status flags are loaded — `OF` is not touched by `sahf`, so it cannot fully reconstruct the flags after a signed-overflow-sensitive operation. In long mode it requires the CPUID `LAHF/SAHF` feature bit.**

column5: sal/sar/shl/shr - bitwise shift left/right
column6: Shifts the destination `r/m` left (`shl`/`sal`, identical encodings) or right, either logically (`shr`, zero-fill) or arithmetically (`sar`, sign-replicating), by an immediate or by the count in `cl`. The last bit shifted out lands in `CF`. **The count is masked to 5 bits (6 for 64-bit operands), so a shift of 32 on a 32-bit register is a no-op, not a zeroing; register-form counts must be in `cl`, no other register works. `OF` is only defined for 1-bit shifts and `sar` never sets `CF` from the sign on a zero shift.**

column5: sarx/shlx/shrx - shift by register, no flags
column6: Performs an arithmetic-right (`sarx`), logical-left (`shlx`), or logical-right (`shrx`) shift of the source by a count taken from a third general-purpose register, writing to a separate destination — a BMI2 three-operand form. **The shift count can come from any GPR, not just `cl`, and the count is masked to the operand width as usual. Crucially these leave all flags untouched, unlike `sar`/`shl`/`shr`, so don't branch on flags after them.** Requires BMI2.

column5: saveprevssp - save previous shadow stack pointer
column6: Writes a restore token for the previous shadow stack onto the current shadow stack, capturing the prior `SSP` so it can later be reloaded with `rstorssp`. Part of the CET shadow-stack switching dance. **It only works when CET shadow stacks are enabled, and it operates on the previously-saved SSP context rather than an arbitrary pointer — it is a NOP-or-`#UD` outside that setup, not a general save mechanism.** No flags are affected.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: sbb - subtract with borrow
column6: Subtracts the source plus the current carry flag from the destination (`dest = dest - src - CF`), the borrow-chaining counterpart to `adc` for multi-word subtraction. **You must seed `CF` correctly before the first limb — typically by doing the low word with `sub` (which sets the borrow) and then chaining higher words with `sbb`. Forgetting to clear or set `CF` first silently corrupts the high limbs. It sets all of `CF`, `OF`, `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, `PF`.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: scas - scan string for a value
column6: Compares the accumulator (`al`/`ax`/`eax`/`rax`) against the element at `[rdi]`, setting flags as if by `cmp`, then advances `rdi` by the element size in the direction set by `DF`. Combined with `repne` it scans for a matching (or non-matching) byte, e.g. finding a string's terminator. **The destination pointer is `rdi`, not `rsi`; the direction depends on `DF` (clear it with `cld` for forward scans); and with `repne`/`repe` the loop stops on `ZF`, so check `rcx` and `ZF` afterward to tell 'found' from 'ran out'. Sets all arithmetic flags.**

column5: senduipi - send user interprocessor interrupt
column6: Sends a user-level interprocessor interrupt to another logical processor, indexing into the UITT (user interrupt target table) with the register operand to deliver a notification without a kernel transition. Part of the user-interrupts (UINTR) feature. **It requires UINTR to be enabled and a valid UITT entry; an out-of-range or invalid index faults. This is 64-bit-mode functionality with specific OS setup, not generally available.** No flags are affected.

column5: serialize - serialize instruction execution
column6: Drains the pipeline so that all prior modifications to flags, registers, and memory are completed and all buffered writes are drained before any later instruction begins, without modifying any architectural state. A lightweight, unprivileged way to fully serialize execution. **It is a true serialization barrier (stronger than `lfence`/`mfence`) yet changes no registers, flags, or memory — its only effect is ordering. It requires the SERIALIZE CPUID feature.**

column5: seta - set byte if above, unsigned (CF=0 and ZF=0)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when an unsigned comparison shows the first operand was above (`CF=0` and `ZF=0`), else 0. **This is the unsigned counterpart to `setg` — mixing the signed and unsigned forms after a `cmp` is a classic bug, so match the test to your data's signedness. Only 8 bits are written; zero-extend for a clean boolean. No flags set.**

column5: setae - set byte if above/equal, unsigned (CF=0)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when `CF` is clear (unsigned above-or-equal, i.e. no borrow from the preceding `cmp`/`sub`), else 0. **Unsigned test — use `setge` for signed comparisons. It also doubles as 'carry clear'. Writes only the low byte, so zero-extend if you need a full-register 0/1. Sets no flags.**

column5: setb - set byte if below, unsigned (CF=1)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when `CF` is set (unsigned below, i.e. a borrow occurred), else 0. It is equivalently 'set if carry'. **Unsigned test — for signed less-than use `setl`. Only the low 8 bits are written; the rest of a wider register is unchanged, so `movzx` for a clean boolean. Reads flags, sets none.**

column5: setbe - set byte if below/equal, unsigned (CF=1 or ZF=1)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when an unsigned comparison shows below-or-equal (`CF=1` or `ZF=1`), else 0. **Unsigned test — pair it with unsigned `cmp` and use `setle` for signed data. It writes only 8 bits, leaving the upper register bits intact, so zero-extend for a full boolean. No flags are set.**

column5: sete - set byte if equal (ZF=1)
column6: Sets the byte destination `r/m8` to 1 if the zero flag is set (operands were equal after a `cmp`/`sub`), otherwise to 0. **It writes only an 8-bit destination and leaves the surrounding bits of a wider register untouched, so zero-extend (e.g. `movzx`) if you need a clean 0/1 in a full register. It reads flags but sets none.**

column5: setg - set byte if greater, signed (ZF=0 and SF=OF)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when a signed comparison shows the first operand was greater (`ZF=0` and `SF=OF`), else 0. Use it after a `cmp` of signed values. **This is the signed test — pair it with `cmp`, and use `seta` instead for unsigned operands. It writes only 8 bits, so zero-extend for a full-register result. Sets no flags.**

column5: setge - set byte if greater/equal, signed (SF=OF)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when a signed comparison shows greater-or-equal (`SF=OF`), else 0. **Signed test: use after `cmp` of signed values, and reach for `setae` on unsigned data. Only the low byte is written — zero-extend if you need a clean boolean in a wider register. No flags are set.**

column5: setl - set byte if less, signed (SF!=OF)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when a signed comparison shows the first operand was less (`SF!=OF`), else 0. **This is the signed less-than; for unsigned operands use `setb`. It affects only 8 bits and leaves the upper bits of a wider register alone, so `movzx` to get a clean 0/1. Reads flags, sets none.**

column5: setle - set byte if less/equal, signed (ZF=1 or SF!=OF)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 when a signed comparison shows less-or-equal (`ZF=1` or `SF!=OF`), else 0. **Signed test — use `setbe` for unsigned values instead. Writes only the low byte, so zero-extend if you want a full-register boolean. Sets no flags.**

column5: setne - set byte if not equal (ZF=0)
column6: Sets the byte destination `r/m8` to 1 when `ZF` is clear (operands differed after a `cmp`), otherwise to 0. **Only the low 8 bits are written; the rest of a wider register keeps its old contents, so `movzx` or pre-zero the register to get a clean boolean. Reads flags, sets none.**

column5: setno - set byte if overflow flag clear (OF=0)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 if the overflow flag is clear (no signed overflow), else 0. **`OF` is about signed overflow, not unsigned carry — don't confuse it with `CF`. Only the low 8 bits are written; the upper bits of a wider register are untouched, so zero-extend for a full boolean. Sets no flags.**

column5: setnp - set byte if parity odd (PF=0)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 if the parity flag is clear (odd number of set bits in the low byte of the last result), else 0. After `ucomiss`/`comisd` it indicates an ordered (non-NaN) comparison. **`PF` is computed only over the low byte, so it is not a parity check of a full word; outside the FP-ordered idiom it is seldom what you want. Writes only the low byte — zero-extend for a clean boolean. Sets no flags.**

column5: setns - set byte if sign flag clear (SF=0)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 if the sign flag is clear (the previous result was non-negative), else 0. **`SF` only reflects the most recent flag-setting operation, so this is meaningful only right after one — and `mov`/`lea` leave it alone. Writes only the low byte; zero-extend for a full-register boolean. No flags set.**

column5: seto - set byte if overflow flag set (OF=1)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 if the overflow flag is set (signed overflow occurred in the previous operation), else 0. **`OF` indicates signed overflow specifically — for unsigned wraparound test `CF` (`setb`) instead. It writes only 8 bits, so zero-extend if you need a clean boolean. Reads flags, sets none.**

column5: setp - set byte if parity even (PF=1)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 if the parity flag is set (the low byte of the last result had an even number of set bits), else 0. Mostly used after floating-point `comiss`/`ucomiss` to detect unordered (NaN) results. **`PF` only reflects the low 8 bits of an integer result, not the whole value, so it is rarely a useful integer test outside the FP-unordered idiom. Writes only 8 bits; zero-extend as needed. No flags set.**

column5: sets - set byte if sign flag set (SF=1)
column6: Sets the byte destination to 1 if the sign flag is set (the previous result was negative), else 0. **It tests `SF` directly, which reflects the high bit of the last arithmetic result — meaningful only after an instruction that actually updated `SF` (`lea`/`mov` don't). Writes only 8 bits, so zero-extend for a clean value. Sets no flags.**

column5: setssbsy - mark shadow stack busy
column6: Sets the 'busy' bit in the supervisor shadow stack token pointed to by the current `SSP`, marking the shadow stack as in-use during CET shadow-stack initialization or switching. **It is privileged and only meaningful when CET supervisor shadow stacks are enabled; it faults if the token is malformed or the feature is off. This is OS/setup plumbing, not application code.** Flags are left undefined.

column5: sfence - store memory fence
column6: Orders memory store operations: all stores issued before the `sfence` become globally visible before any store after it. It is used to enforce write ordering, notably around non-temporal stores (`movnt*`) and weakly-ordered memory. **It fences stores only — loads are not ordered by it, so use `mfence` (or `lfence`) if you also need to order reads. On normal write-back memory regular x86 store ordering already holds, so `sfence` matters mainly with non-temporal/WC writes.** No flags affected.

column5: sgdt - store GDT register to memory
column6: Stores the global descriptor table register (limit plus base) into a memory operand — 2 bytes of limit followed by the 8-byte base in 64-bit mode. Mainly used by system software and diagnostics. **It is unprivileged (executable from user mode), which is itself a known information leak; the memory operand must be large enough for the 10-byte pseudo-descriptor in long mode. There is no register-destination form.** No flags affected.

column5: sha1msg2 - SHA-1 message-schedule step
column6: Performs the final part of the SHA-1 message-schedule computation, combining two `xmm` operands to produce four expanded message words `W` for upcoming rounds. It is one of the SHA-NI helper instructions that accelerate SHA-1 hashing. **It only does the message expansion, not the round compression — it must be sequenced correctly with `sha1msg1`, `sha1nexte`, and `sha1rnds4`, feeding the right `W` words in order, or the hash is wrong. Requires the SHA CPUID feature.** No flags affected.

column5: sha1nexte - compute SHA-1 next E value
column6: Computes the next SHA-1 state variable `E` after a group of rounds, adding the rotated `A` value from the source into the appropriate dword of the destination `xmm` register. Used together with `sha1rnds4` to build a hardware-accelerated SHA-1 round. **This is a fixed-form `xmm`-only instruction with no immediate and no flag effects** — it operates purely on packed message/state data, so check for `SHA` CPUID support before emitting it and don't expect it to touch EFLAGS.

column5: sha1rnds4 - four SHA-1 rounds
column6: Performs four rounds of the SHA-1 message-scheduling and compression operation on `xmm` state, with an 8-bit immediate selecting which of the four round-constant/function groups (0-3) to apply. Pair it with `sha1nexte` to maintain the `E` variable across round groups. **The immediate is mandatory and only its low two bits matter; it is not a shift count or a flag-affecting operand**, and the instruction leaves EFLAGS untouched.

column5: sha256msg2 - SHA-256 message schedule
column6: Performs the final calculation step of the SHA-256 message schedule, combining intermediate sigma values across two `xmm` source operands to produce four scheduled message dwords in the destination. Typically used after `sha256msg1` and before `sha256rnds2`. **It is `xmm`-only with no immediate and no flag effects** — verify `SHA` CPUID support and treat it as pure dataflow on packed dwords.

column5: sha256rnds2 - two SHA-256 rounds
column6: Performs two rounds of the SHA-256 compression function, updating the working state in the destination `xmm` from message/constant data in the source, with the pre-loaded `xmm0` supplying the round constants plus message as an implicit operand. **`xmm0` is an implicit input you must set up before the instruction — it is not encoded in the operands** — and like the other SHA primitives it ignores and does not modify EFLAGS.

column5: shld - double-precision shift left
column6: Shifts the destination operand left by the count, filling the vacated low bits with the high bits of the source operand instead of zeros, which makes it handy for multi-word shifts and bit-funnel operations. The count comes from an immediate or from `cl`. **The count is masked to 5 bits (6 bits for 64-bit operands), so a shift of 32 or more does not do what you might expect, and the result of `OF`/`AF` is left undefined for counts other than 1**; `CF` receives the last bit shifted out.

column5: shrd - double-precision shift right
column6: Shifts the destination operand right by the count, filling the vacated high bits with the low bits of the source operand rather than sign or zero, the right-hand companion to `shld` for funnel shifts and multi-word arithmetic. The count is an immediate or `cl`. **The count is masked to 5 bits (6 bits in 64-bit operand size) and `OF`/`AF` are undefined for any count other than 1**, while `CF` holds the last bit shifted out of the destination.

column5: sidt - store IDT register
column6: Stores the interrupt descriptor table register (`IDTR`), its limit and base, to a 10-byte (in 64-bit mode) memory operand. It is unprivileged and readable from any ring, which is why it features in some sandbox-escape and red-pill techniques. **The destination must be memory, not a register, and the operand layout is 2-byte limit followed by an 8-byte base in long mode; it affects no flags.**

column5: SIMD FP exceptions - MXCSR overview
column6: This entry is a documentation fragment rather than a real opcode — it refers to the SIMD floating-point exception machinery shared by SSE/AVX instructions, where masked exceptions (invalid, denormal, divide-by-zero, overflow, underflow, precision) set sticky status bits in `MXCSR` and unmasked ones raise `#XF`. **There is nothing to assemble here; control rounding and exception masking through `MXCSR` via `ldmxcsr`/`stmxcsr`, and remember none of these conditions affect the integer EFLAGS.**

column5: sldt - store LDT selector
column6: Stores the segment selector from the local descriptor table register into a 16/32/64-bit register or a 16-bit memory operand. It is essentially a system-management relic that is rarely meaningful under a flat 64-bit memory model. **Only the low 16 bits (the selector) are stored; a register destination is zero- or sign-padded depending on operand size, and no flags change.**

column5: smsw - store machine status word
column6: Stores the low 16 bits of `CR0` (the legacy machine status word) into a register or memory operand. A register destination with a larger operand size receives the full low part of `CR0`, not just 16 bits, which surprises people expecting a clean 16-bit value. **It is unprivileged, reads `CR0` rather than writing it, and affects no flags — use `mov` from `CR0` if you actually need the full control register.**

column5: sqrtpd - packed double sqrt
column6: Computes the square roots of the packed double-precision floating-point elements of the source and writes them to the destination `xmm`/`ymm`. Rounding follows the `MXCSR` rounding-control field. **A negative input (other than -0.0) produces a NaN and signals the invalid-operation exception, which only traps if unmasked in `MXCSR`; this is an FP-domain instruction that leaves EFLAGS alone.**

column5: sqrtps - packed single sqrt
column6: Computes the square roots of the packed single-precision floating-point elements of the source into the destination register. Rounding obeys `MXCSR`. **For a fast reciprocal-square-root approximation prefer `rsqrtps`, but note `sqrtps` here is the exact, correctly-rounded operation; negative operands yield NaN with the invalid flag, and no EFLAGS bits are touched.**

column5: sqrtsd - scalar double sqrt
column6: Computes the square root of the low double-precision element of the source and stores it in the low quadword of the destination, leaving the upper bits of the destination unchanged. Rounding follows `MXCSR`. **Because the high lane is preserved rather than zeroed, be deliberate about which register holds your scalar; a negative input gives NaN and the invalid flag, and EFLAGS are unaffected.**

column5: sqrtss - scalar single sqrt
column6: Computes the square root of the low single-precision element of the source and writes it into the low dword of the destination, preserving the upper bits of the destination register. **The unchanged upper lanes mean this is not a full-register write — clear or set them separately if needed — and a negative argument produces NaN with the invalid-operation flag; integer flags are never modified.**

column5: stac - set AC flag (SMAP)
column6: Sets the alignment-check / access-control (`AC`) flag in EFLAGS, which under SMAP temporarily permits supervisor code to access user-mode pages. It is the counterpart to `clac` and is privileged in the sense that it is only meaningful in kernel context. **Wrap user-memory accesses tightly in `stac`/`clac` so you don't leave the window open, and remember it only changes `AC` — no other flag is affected.**

column5: stc - set carry flag
column6: Sets the carry flag `CF` to 1, with no other effect. Useful for seeding a multi-word add/subtract-with-carry chain or returning a boolean carry condition from a routine. **It modifies only `CF`; pair it with `clc` to clear and `cmc` to toggle, and don't expect it to disturb `ZF`, `SF`, or the others.**

column5: std - set direction flag
column6: Sets the direction flag `DF` to 1, causing the string instructions (`movs`, `stos`, `lods`, `cmps`, `scas`) to auto-decrement their pointer registers instead of incrementing. **Most ABIs require `DF` clear on function entry and exit, so if you `std` you must `cld` before returning or calling out — leaving it set is a classic source of mysterious memory corruption; it changes only `DF`.**

column5: sti - set interrupt flag
column6: Sets the interrupt-enable flag `IF`, re-enabling maskable hardware interrupts. It is privileged and only valid when the current privilege level allows it. **There is a one-instruction delay before interrupts are actually recognized (the instruction following `sti` is shielded), which is exactly why the `sti; hlt` idiom avoids a lost-wakeup race; it affects only `IF`.**

column5: stmxcsr - store MXCSR
column6: Stores the 32-bit `MXCSR` control/status register to a memory operand, capturing the current SSE rounding mode, exception masks, and sticky exception flags. Pair it with `ldmxcsr` to save and restore SSE state. **The destination must be a 32-bit memory location, not a register, and reading `MXCSR` this way does not clear its sticky exception bits; no EFLAGS are involved.**

column5: stos - store accumulator to string
column6: Stores `al`/`ax`/`eax`/`rax` to the memory location pointed to by `rdi`, then advances `rdi` by the element size, with the `b`/`w`/`d`/`q` suffix or operand selecting the width. Prefix with `rep` to fill `rcx` elements, the canonical memset building block. **The destination is implicitly `[rdi]` and the direction depends on `DF` (clear it with `cld` first to count up), so set up `rax`, `rdi`, and `rcx` before `rep stos`; it modifies no flags.**

column5: str - store task register
column6: Stores the segment selector of the current task register (`TR`) into a register or 16-bit memory operand. It is a system/legacy-task instruction with little use under modern flat 64-bit operating systems. **Despite the name it has nothing to do with strings — only the 16-bit selector is written, and it affects no flags.**

column5: sttilecfg - store AMX tile config
column6: Stores the current AMX tile configuration (the palette and per-tile row/column dimensions) into a 64-byte memory operand, the counterpart of `ldtilecfg`. It is part of the Intel AMX matrix-tile extension. **Requires AMX to be enabled and the destination must be a 64-byte memory region; it touches no EFLAGS and is meaningless unless tiles have been configured.**

column5: stui - set user interrupt flag
column6: Sets the user interrupt flag (`UIF`) as part of the user-interrupt (UINTR) facility, enabling delivery of user-level interrupts to the current thread. It is the counterpart of `clui`. **It requires UINTR support and acts on the dedicated user-interrupt state rather than the normal `IF` in EFLAGS — do not confuse it with `sti`, and it leaves the standard arithmetic flags unchanged.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: sub - subtract
column6: Subtracts the source operand from the destination and stores the difference in the destination, setting flags exactly as a `cmp` would. It is the workhorse for decrementing, pointer arithmetic, and stack-frame allocation. **For multi-word subtraction continue with `sbb` to pull in the borrow; the borrow is reported in `CF`, signed overflow in `OF`, and you read those with the appropriate `jb`/`jl`-style branches after deciding signed versus unsigned.**

column5: subpd - subtract packed doubles
column6: Subtracts the packed double-precision elements of the source from those of the destination, element by element, writing the differences back to the destination `xmm`/`ymm`. Rounding and exceptions follow `MXCSR`. **Subtraction is not commutative, so order the operands as destination-minus-source; this is an FP-domain op that signals SSE exceptions through `MXCSR` and never affects EFLAGS.**

column5: subps - subtract packed singles
column6: Subtracts the packed single-precision elements of the source from those of the destination lane by lane, storing the results in the destination register. **Remember the operation is destination minus source, not the reverse; results round per `MXCSR`, exceptions go through `MXCSR` not EFLAGS, and the integer flags are left untouched.**

column5: subsd - subtract scalar double
column6: Subtracts the low double-precision element of the source from the low double of the destination and stores the difference in the destination's low quadword, leaving its upper bits unchanged. **Only the low lane is computed and the high lane of the destination is preserved (not zeroed), so manage the upper bits deliberately; ordering is destination-minus-source and no EFLAGS are modified.**

column5: subss - subtract scalar single
column6: Subtracts the low single-precision element of the source from the low single of the destination, writing the result into the destination's low dword and preserving its upper bits. **As with the other scalar SSE ops the high lanes are left intact rather than cleared, the operation is destination-minus-source, and SSE exceptions surface in `MXCSR` while EFLAGS stay unchanged.**

column5: swapgs - swap GS base with kernel base
column6: Exchanges the current `GS` base address with the value held in the `IA32_KERNEL_GS_BASE` MSR, the standard trick a kernel uses on entry from user mode to obtain its per-CPU data pointer. **It is privileged and you must call it exactly once on each kernel entry and once again on exit — calling it an even number of times by mistake leaves you pointing at the wrong base; it modifies no flags.**

column5: syscall - fast system call
column6: Transfers control to the kernel system-call entry point in `IA32_LSTAR`, saving the return address in `rcx` and the prior `RFLAGS` in `r11`, and masking flags per `IA32_FMASK`. It is the standard fast kernel-entry mechanism in 64-bit mode. **It clobbers `rcx` and `r11`, so anything you needed in those registers is lost across the call, and the user/kernel stack switch is the kernel's responsibility — `syscall` itself does not change `rsp`.**

column5: sysenter - fast ring-3 to ring-0 entry
column6: Performs a fast transition from user to supervisor code using the `SYSENTER` MSRs for the target CS, EIP, and SS:ESP, clearing the interrupt flag on entry. It is the legacy fast-call mechanism largely superseded by `syscall` in 64-bit mode. **It does not save a return address — the OS must arrange that itself — and because it disables interrupts (`IF` cleared), the entry stub runs with interrupts off until re-enabled.**

column5: sysexit - fast return to user (sysenter)
column6: Performs the privileged fast transition from supervisor back to user code corresponding to `sysenter`, loading CS/SS from the `SYSENTER` MSRs and taking the target `eip`/`esp` from `edx`/`ecx`. **It is privileged and the target user address and stack must be placed in `edx` and `ecx` before the instruction; it pairs only with `sysenter`, not with `syscall`, and does not by itself restore EFLAGS.**

column5: sysret - fast return to user (syscall)
column6: Performs the privileged fast return from a `syscall` handler back to user mode, restoring `rip` from `rcx` and `RFLAGS` from `r11` and loading user CS/SS from the `STAR` MSR. **It is the mandatory partner of `syscall` and depends on `rcx` and `r11` still holding the saved return address and flags, so do not clobber them before returning; the 64-bit form requires a canonical return address or it faults in kernel context.**

column5: test - logical compare (AND, discard)
column6: Computes the bitwise AND of its two operands to set the flags but discards the result, the read-only counterpart of `and` just as `cmp` is to `sub`. It is the standard way to check whether specific bits are set or whether a register is zero (`test reg, reg`). **It always clears `CF` and `OF`, leaves `AF` undefined, and sets `ZF`/`SF`/`PF` from the AND — so branch with `jz`/`jnz` on the masked bits rather than the signed/unsigned comparisons you'd use after `cmp`.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: testui - copy the user interrupt flag into CF
column6: `testui` reads the user interrupt flag (UIF) and copies it into `CF`, also reflecting it in `ZF`; it is part of the user-interrupt (UINTR) feature. No operands are taken. **Despite the name it is nothing like `test`: it does not AND anything, and it leaves `PF`, `AF`, `SF`, and `OF` undefined — only `CF` (and `ZF`) carry meaningful results.** Requires CPL-3 user-interrupt support to be enabled or it faults with #UD.

column5: tileloadd - load an AMX tile from memory
column6: `tileloadd` loads a 2-D AMX tile register (`tmm0`-`tmm7`) from memory, with the source given as a base plus a stride register that supplies the per-row byte pitch. `tileloaddt1` is the same but hints that the data is non-temporal (T1). **The tile's row and column dimensions come from the tile configuration set by `ldtilecfg`, not from the instruction — you must `ldtilecfg` before any tile op or it faults, and the stride is a byte pitch, not an element count.** Affects no flags.

column5: tilerelease - reset AMX tile config to init state
column6: `tilerelease` returns the AMX tile registers and tile configuration to the initialized (zeroed/unconfigured) state, marking `TILECFG` as no longer in use. It takes no operands and touches no flags. **Issue it when you are done with tiles so the OS can avoid saving/restoring tile state on context switches; after `tilerelease` you must `ldtilecfg` again before using any tile.**

column5: tilestored - store an AMX tile to memory
column6: `tilestored` writes a 2-D AMX tile register out to memory, using a base plus a stride register that gives the byte pitch between successive rows. The dimensions stored come from the active tile configuration. **As with `tileloadd`, you must have run `ldtilecfg` first, and the stride operand is a per-row byte pitch — getting it wrong silently scatters rows at the wrong addresses.** No flags are affected.

column5: tilezero - zero an AMX tile register
column6: `tilezero` clears a single AMX tile register (`tmm0`-`tmm7`) to all zeros. It takes only the destination tile and affects no flags. **It still requires a valid tile configuration from `ldtilecfg` because the tile must already have defined dimensions; use it to reset an accumulator tile before a `tdpb*`/`tdpfp16ps` matrix-multiply sequence.**

column5: tion breakpoint is not re - PDF artifact
column6: **This is not a real instruction.** It is a broken line of prose (the tail of a word ending in "...tion" followed by "breakpoint is not re-triggered...") pulled out of a manual's running text, not a mnemonic. No opcode, no operands, nothing to encode. **Drop it from the instruction set — if you actually want a debug breakpoint, use `int3`.**

column5: tpause - timed wait until a TSC deadline
column6: `tpause` puts the logical processor into an optimized (C0.1) or power-saving (C0.2) wait state until the time-stamp counter reaches the 64-bit deadline in `edx:eax`, or the wait is otherwise interrupted. The input register selects the wait state; `CF` is set on early/abnormal wake. **The deadline is an absolute TSC value in `edx:eax`, not a duration — compute it as `rdtsc` plus your delay, and pair it with `umonitor`/`umwait` only if you want address-triggered wakeups.**

column5: tzcnt - count trailing zero bits
column6: `tzcnt` counts the number of least-significant zero bits in the source before the first set bit, writing the count to the destination. If the source is zero it returns the operand size in bits and sets `CF`; `ZF` is set when the result is zero (i.e. bit 0 was set). **Unlike `bsf`, `tzcnt` is defined for a zero input, and it sets `CF`/`ZF` rather than only `ZF` — but on CPUs without BMI1 the encoding decodes as `bsf`, which has different zero-input behavior, so guard it by CPUID.**

column5: ud - raise an invalid-opcode (#UD) exception
column6: The `ud` family (`ud0`, `ud1`, `ud2`) are guaranteed-to-be-undefined opcodes that always raise an invalid-opcode (#UD) exception, used as deliberate trap/poison instructions. They take no architectural action and affect no flags. **`ud2` is the canonical choice for marking unreachable code or compiler `__builtin_trap`; unlike an arbitrary illegal byte sequence its #UD behavior is architecturally guaranteed and stable across CPUs.**

column5: uiret - return from a user interrupt handler
column6: `uiret` returns from a user-interrupt handler, popping the saved `RIP`, `RFLAGS`, and stack pointer that the hardware pushed when delivering the user interrupt, all at CPL 3. It is the user-mode analogue of `iret`. **It is only valid when user-interrupt delivery (UINTR) is enabled and you are inside a user-interrupt handler; the stack frame layout is fixed by hardware, so do not hand-roll the popped frame or `uiret` will fault or return to the wrong place.**

column5: umonitor - arm address monitor (user level)
column6: `umonitor` arms the hardware address-monitoring range around the linear address in the supplied general register, so a subsequent `umwait` can wake when that range is written. It is the unprivileged (ring-3) counterpart of `monitor` and affects no flags. **The address comes from a register (default `DS` segment) and is rounded to the monitor granularity reported by CPUID; always pair it immediately with `umwait`, and re-arm after each wake because a single store disarms the monitor.**

column5: umwait - wait on monitored address or TSC deadline
column6: `umwait` waits in a low-power C0.1/C0.2 state until the address armed by `umonitor` is written, the TSC reaches the deadline in `edx:eax`, or the wait is otherwise interrupted; the input register selects the wait state, and `CF` reports whether the deadline expired. **It must follow a `umonitor` that armed the address, and the deadline in `edx:eax` is an absolute TSC value, not a delay — check `CF` on wake to tell a timeout from a real store-triggered wakeup.**

column5: vaddph - add packed FP16 (half-precision) values
column6: `vaddph` adds two vectors of IEEE-754 half-precision (FP16) elements lane-by-lane, writing the packed sums to the destination, with EVEX masking and an optional embedded rounding/SAE control. It is part of AVX-512 FP16. **It requires AVX512-FP16 support, and FP16's tiny dynamic range overflows to infinity and loses precision far sooner than f32/f64 — accumulate long reductions in higher precision (e.g. via `vcvtph2ps`) to avoid surprises.**

column5: vaddsh - add scalar FP16 values
column6: `vaddsh` adds the low half-precision (FP16) elements of two sources, writes the scalar sum to the low element of the destination, and copies the remaining upper elements from the first source; EVEX supports masking and embedded rounding/SAE. **Only the low FP16 lane is computed — the rest pass through from the first source, not zeroed — and it needs AVX512-FP16; watch FP16's narrow range when summing.**

column5: valignd - concatenate and shift dword/qword vectors
column6: `valignd`/`valignq` concatenate the destination/second source and the third source into a double-width temporary, then shift it right by an immediate count of elements and keep the low destination-width portion — effectively a cross-element rotate/align by 32-bit (`d`) or 64-bit (`q`) granularity, with EVEX masking. **The shift count is in elements, not bytes, and the concatenation order matters: the result pulls from the low source first as the count grows, so it is easy to get the operand order backwards versus `palignr`.**

column5: vbcstnebf162ps - broadcast a BF16 element as FP32
column6: `vbcstnebf162ps` loads a single BF16 element from memory, converts it to FP32, and broadcasts that f32 across all elements of the destination; it is part of the AVX-NE-CONVERT extension. **The source is a memory BF16 element (not a register lane) and the broadcast fills the whole destination with one value — use it to splat a BF16 constant into an f32 vector; it needs AVX-NE-CONVERT support.**

column5: vbcstnesh2ps - broadcast an FP16 element as FP32
column6: `vbcstnesh2ps` loads one half-precision (FP16) element from memory, converts it to FP32, and broadcasts that value across every element of the destination; it is an AVX-NE-CONVERT instruction. **The source must be a memory FP16 element and the result is a full-width f32 splat — handy for replicating an FP16 scalar into an f32 vector without AVX512-FP16; it requires AVX-NE-CONVERT.**

column5: vblendmpd - blend f64/f32 vectors by opmask
column6: `vblendmpd`/`vblendmps` select each double-precision (`pd`) or single-precision (`ps`) element from one of two sources according to the corresponding bit of an opmask (`k`) register, merging them into the destination. **Unlike `vpblendd`/old blends the selector is a `k`-mask register, not an immediate, so the per-lane choice is data-driven; a clear mask bit takes the first source and a set bit the second — verify which way your mask polarity runs.**

column5: vbroadcast - splat scalar/128-bit FP across a vector
column6: The `vbroadcast` family loads a single floating-point scalar (`ss`/`sd`) or a 128-bit chunk (`f128`) and replicates it across all elements/lanes of the destination. **In AVX1 the scalar broadcasts (`vbroadcastss`/`vbroadcastsd`) require a memory source; broadcasting from a register lane is an AVX2 (and later) capability, so check the form your CPU supports before assuming a register operand is legal.**

column5: vcmpph - compare packed FP16 values to a k-mask
column6: `vcmpph` compares two vectors of half-precision (FP16) elements using an 8-bit predicate immediate and writes a per-lane result into an opmask (`k`) register; it is part of AVX512-FP16 and supports SAE. **The relation and its NaN/signaling behavior are encoded in the immediate, and the result is a `k`-mask, not flags — feed it to a masked op rather than a `jcc`; it requires AVX512-FP16.**

column5: vcmpsd - compare low f64, masked predicate
column6: This compares the low double-precision element of two operands using an 8-bit (VEX) or extended (EVEX) predicate immediate and produces an all-ones/all-zeros mask in the low qword (VEX) or a `k`-mask bit (EVEX); the upper element is copied from the first source. The garbled source row corresponds to `vcmpsd`. **The comparison kind lives entirely in the immediate (EQ, LT, LE, UNORD, NEQ, ...), and the ordered/unordered and signaling/quiet choice changes NaN behavior — pick the predicate deliberately, and remember it writes a mask, not `EFLAGS` (use `vcomisd`/`vucomisd` for flag-setting compares).

column5: vcmpsh - compare scalar FP16 values to a k-mask bit
column6: `vcmpsh` compares the low half-precision (FP16) elements of two sources against a predicate immediate and sets the low bit of an opmask (`k`) register accordingly, with optional SAE. **Only the scalar low lane participates, the predicate (including ordered/unordered, quiet/signaling) lives in the immediate, and the output is a mask bit rather than `EFLAGS` — use `vcomish` if you need flags; needs AVX512-FP16.**

column5: vcmpss - compare low f32, masked predicate
column6: This compares the low single-precision element of two operands against a predicate immediate and yields an all-ones/all-zeros lane mask (VEX) or a `k`-mask bit (EVEX), copying the remaining upper elements from the first source. The garbled source row corresponds to `vcmpss`. **The relation (EQ/LT/LE/UNORD/NEQ/etc.) and its signaling-vs-quiet NaN semantics are encoded in the immediate, so choose it carefully; the result is a SIMD mask, not flags — use `vcomiss`/`vucomiss` when you actually need `EFLAGS`.**

column5: vcomish - ordered FP16 compare, set EFLAGS
column6: `vcomish` performs an ordered compare of the low half-precision (FP16) elements of two operands and sets `ZF`, `PF`, and `CF` in `EFLAGS` like `comiss`, clearing `OF`, `SF`, and `AF`. **Because it sets the carry/zero/parity flags (not the signed flags), follow it with the unsigned conditions `ja`/`jae`/`jb`/`jbe`/`je` — never the signed `jg`/`jl`; an unordered (NaN) result sets `ZF=PF=CF=1`, so test `PF` to catch NaN. Requires AVX512-FP16.**

column5: vcompresspd - pack masked f64 elements densely
column6: This packs the double-precision elements whose opmask bits are set into contiguous low elements of the destination (or to memory), discarding the unselected lanes — the gather-free 'compress' primitive. The garbled source row corresponds to `vcompresspd`. **The number of elements written equals the popcount of the mask, and to memory it stores only that many qwords with no gaps; the inverse is `vexpandpd`, and you must supply the selector as a `k`-mask.**

column5: (operand note) vcompressps - pack masked f32 elements
column6: This row's text ('operand can be a zmm/ymm/xmm register or a 512/256/128-bit memory location') is an operand-encoding note that belongs to a compress-style instruction such as `vcompressps`, which packs the mask-selected single-precision elements into contiguous low elements of a register or memory destination. **The destination width is selectable (`zmm`/`ymm`/`xmm` or matching memory), and only popcount-of-mask elements are written with no gaps — the fragment itself is documentation, not a separate opcode.**

column5: vcvtdq2ph - convert packed i32 to FP16
column6: `vcvtdq2ph` converts packed signed 32-bit integers to half-precision (FP16) floating-point values, with EVEX masking and embedded rounding control; it is part of AVX512-FP16. **The output is FP16, whose ~11-bit significand cannot represent most 32-bit integers exactly, so large integers round — set the rounding mode deliberately, and expect precision loss; it requires AVX512-FP16.**

column5: vcvtne2ps2bf16 - convert two f32 vecs to one BF16 vec
column6: `vcvtne2ps2bf16` converts two source vectors of single-precision values to BF16 using round-to-nearest-even and interleaves the results into a single packed BF16 destination of twice the element count. It is part of AVX512-BF16/AVX-NE-CONVERT. **It takes two f32 sources and produces one BF16 vector with a fixed even/odd interleave, so mind which source lands in which output position; rounding is always RNE (no override).

column5: vcvtneebf162ps - convert even BF16 elements to FP32
column6: `vcvtneebf162ps` reads a BF16 source from memory and converts only its even-indexed elements to FP32, packing them into the destination; it is an AVX-NE-CONVERT instruction. **It deliberately consumes every other (even) element, halving the count — pair it with the odd-element variant `vcvtneobf162ps` to process a full BF16 array; the source is a memory operand and it needs AVX-NE-CONVERT.**

column5: vcvtneeph2ps - convert even FP16 elements to FP32
column6: `vcvtneeph2ps` loads an FP16 source from memory and converts its even-indexed elements to FP32, writing them packed into the destination; it is part of AVX-NE-CONVERT. **Only the even lanes are taken, so use it together with `vcvtneoph2ps` to cover the full FP16 input; the source is a memory operand and the extension is AVX-NE-CONVERT, not AVX512-FP16.**

column5: vcvtneobf162ps - convert odd BF16 elements to FP32
column6: `vcvtneobf162ps` reads a BF16 source from memory and converts its odd-indexed elements to FP32, packing them into the destination; it is an AVX-NE-CONVERT instruction. **It is the odd-element companion to `vcvtneebf162ps`; run both over the same source to convert every BF16 element, and note the source must be in memory and AVX-NE-CONVERT must be present.**

column5: vcvtneoph2ps - convert odd FP16 elements to FP32
column6: `vcvtneoph2ps` loads an FP16 source from memory and converts its odd-indexed elements to FP32, packing them into the destination; part of AVX-NE-CONVERT. **It is the odd-lane counterpart of `vcvtneeph2ps` — use the pair to convert all FP16 elements — and like the others it takes a memory source and requires AVX-NE-CONVERT.**

column5: vcvtneps2bf16 - convert packed f32 to BF16
column6: `vcvtneps2bf16` converts a vector of single-precision values to BF16 using round-to-nearest-even, packing the narrower results into the lower half of the destination; it is part of AVX512-BF16/AVX-NE-CONVERT. **BF16 keeps f32's exponent range but only ~8 bits of mantissa, so this is a lossy rounding step (always RNE) meant for ML workloads — recover precision with `vpmovzx`/shift back to f32 when needed.**

column5: vcvtpd2ph - pack FP64 lanes down to FP16
column6: Converts packed double-precision (FP64) values in the source to half-precision (FP16) values, writing them into the low half of the destination `xmm`. This is an AVX-512 FP16 (EVEX-encoded) instruction, so it requires AVX512-FP16 and is mask/broadcast aware via `{k}` and `{z}`. Each FP64 narrows to 16 bits, so the result occupies far fewer bytes than the input — the upper lanes of the destination are zeroed. **You need a CPU with AVX512-FP16 support and an EVEX encoding; there is no legacy SSE/VEX form, and rounding follows MXCSR unless you supply an embedded `{er}` rounding control on the register form.**

column5: vcvtph2dq - FP16 to signed dword ints
column6: Converts packed FP16 values from the source into packed signed 32-bit integers in the destination, rounding according to MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast support. Because each FP16 widens to a 32-bit integer, the input lane count is half the output byte width — half a `ymm` of FP16 fills a full `zmm` of dwords. **Out-of-range or NaN inputs produce the integer indefinite value `0x80000000`; use `vcvttph2dq` if you want truncation instead of MXCSR-directed rounding.**

column5: vcvtph2pd - FP16 to FP64 widen
column6: Widens packed FP16 values from the low portion of the source into packed double-precision (FP64) values in the destination. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, mask- and broadcast-aware. Each 16-bit input expands to 64 bits, so only the lowest few source lanes are consumed to fill the destination. **The conversion is exact for every FP16 value (FP64 can represent them all), but you still need AVX512-FP16; there is no rounding loss here, so MXCSR rounding mode is irrelevant.**
