column5: vcvtph2qq - FP16 to signed qword ints
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed signed 64-bit integers in the destination, rounding per MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast. Each 16-bit float becomes a 64-bit integer, so only the lowest source lanes are used. **Inputs that overflow the signed quadword range or are NaN yield the integer indefinite `0x8000000000000000`; reach for `vcvttph2qq` when you want truncation toward zero rather than the current rounding mode.**

column5: vcvtph2udq - FP16 to unsigned dword ints
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed unsigned 32-bit integers, rounding according to MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, mask- and broadcast-aware. Each FP16 widens to a 32-bit unsigned result, consuming half as many input lanes as output dwords. **Negative inputs (other than those that round to zero), overflows, and NaNs produce the unsigned integer indefinite `0xFFFFFFFF`; use `vcvttph2udq` for truncation semantics.**

column5: vcvtph2uqq - FP16 to unsigned qword ints
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed unsigned 64-bit integers, rounding per MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast support. Each 16-bit float yields a 64-bit unsigned integer, so only the lowest source lanes feed the result. **Negative, out-of-range, or NaN inputs become the unsigned indefinite `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF`; choose `vcvttph2uqq` if you need truncation toward zero instead of MXCSR rounding.**

column5: vcvtph2uw - FP16 to unsigned word ints
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed unsigned 16-bit integers, rounding according to MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, mask- and broadcast-aware. Input and output lanes are the same width here, so a full-width source fills a full-width destination. **Negative or out-of-range inputs and NaNs saturate to the unsigned word indefinite `0xFFFF`; use `vcvttph2uw` when truncation rather than rounding is required.**

column5: vcvtph2w - FP16 to signed word ints
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed signed 16-bit integers, rounding per MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast. Element widths match (16-bit in, 16-bit out), so lane counts are one-to-one across the whole register. **Out-of-range magnitudes and NaNs produce the signed word indefinite `0x8000`; if you want truncation toward zero, use `vcvttph2w` instead.**

column5: vcvtps2ph - pack FP32 down to FP16
column6: Converts packed single-precision (FP32) values to half-precision (FP16) values, writing the narrowed results into the low half of the destination. Unlike the FP16 ISA conversions, this is the older F16C/AVX form and takes an 8-bit immediate selecting the rounding mode (or MXCSR via imm bit 2). Each FP32 narrows to 16 bits, halving the byte footprint. **The rounding control lives in the immediate operand, not MXCSR by default — pass `0x04` in the imm8 to defer to MXCSR; the destination may be memory, which is handy for storing FP16 directly.**

column5: vcvtqq2ph - signed qword ints to FP16
column6: Converts packed signed 64-bit integers from the source into FP16 values in the low portion of the destination, rounding according to MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast support. Each 64-bit integer narrows to 16 bits, so the result occupies a quarter of the input's byte width and the upper destination lanes are zeroed. **Large integers lose precision when squeezed into FP16's 11-bit significand; rounding follows MXCSR unless you attach embedded `{er}` rounding on the register form.**

column5: vcvtsd2sh - scalar FP64 to FP16
column6: Converts the low FP64 value of the source to an FP16 value in the low 16 bits of the destination, copying the upper bits of the destination from the second source operand (the three-operand scalar convention). AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form. Only the lowest lane is converted; the rest of the destination is filled from the merge source. **This is a scalar three-operand op — bits `[127:16]` come from the second source, not the converted value, so supply the right merge register; rounding follows MXCSR unless overridden with `{er}`.**

column5: vcvtsh2sd - scalar FP16 to FP64
column6: Converts the low FP16 value of the source to an FP64 value in the low quadword of the destination, taking the upper bits from the second source operand. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form. The widening is exact since FP64 represents every FP16 value. **Only lane zero is converted; bits `[127:64]` are merged from the second source operand, so pick that register deliberately even though no rounding is involved.**

column5: vcvtsh2si - scalar FP16 to signed integer
column6: Converts the low FP16 value of the source to a signed integer in a general-purpose register (`r32` or `r64` depending on operand size and REX.W), rounding per MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form. The destination is a GPR, not a vector register. **Out-of-range or NaN inputs return the integer indefinite (`0x80000000` for 32-bit, `0x80000000_00000000` for 64-bit); use `vcvttsh2si` for truncation, and remember operand size is governed by REX.W.**

column5: vcvtsh2ss - scalar FP16 to FP32
column6: Converts the low FP16 value of the source to an FP32 value in the low doubleword of the destination, merging the upper bits from the second source operand. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form. The widening from FP16 to FP32 is exact. **Only lane zero is produced; bits `[127:32]` are copied from the second source, so choose that operand carefully — no rounding occurs since FP32 covers all FP16 values.**

column5: vcvtsh2usi - scalar FP16 to unsigned integer
column6: Converts the low FP16 value of the source to an unsigned integer in a general-purpose register (`r32` or `r64` per REX.W), rounding according to MXCSR. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form, destination is a GPR. **Negative, overflowing, or NaN inputs return the unsigned integer indefinite (all-ones); use `vcvttsh2usi` if you need truncation toward zero, and set REX.W to choose 32- vs 64-bit output.**

column5: vcvtsi2sh - signed int to scalar FP16
column6: Converts a signed integer from a general-purpose register or memory (doubleword or quadword, selected by REX.W) to an FP16 value in the low 16 bits of the destination, merging the upper bits from the second source operand. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form. **Large 32- or 64-bit integers can't be represented exactly in FP16 and get rounded per MXCSR (or embedded `{er}`); bits `[127:16]` come from the second source, and operand width depends on REX.W.**

column5: vcvtss2sh - scalar FP32 to FP16
column6: Converts the low FP32 value of the source to an FP16 value in the low 16 bits of the destination, copying the upper bits from the second source operand. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded scalar form. Only lane zero is narrowed; the remaining bits are merged. **This is the AVX512-FP16 scalar narrowing (distinct from the F16C `vcvtps2ph`, which takes an imm8 rounding control) — here rounding follows MXCSR unless you supply `{er}`, and bits `[127:16]` come from the second source.**

column5: vcvttph2udq - truncate FP16 to unsigned dword
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed unsigned 32-bit integers using truncation (round toward zero), ignoring the MXCSR rounding mode. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast support. Each FP16 widens to a 32-bit unsigned result. **This is the truncating variant — the fractional part is dropped regardless of MXCSR; negative, overflowing, or NaN inputs become the unsigned indefinite `0xFFFFFFFF`.**

column5: vcvttph2uw - truncate FP16 to unsigned word
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed unsigned 16-bit integers using truncation toward zero rather than MXCSR rounding. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, mask- and broadcast-aware. Element widths match at 16 bits, so lanes map one-to-one. **Truncation is forced regardless of MXCSR; negative or out-of-range values and NaNs saturate to the unsigned word indefinite `0xFFFF`.**

column5: vcvttph2w - truncate FP16 to signed word
column6: Converts packed FP16 values into packed signed 16-bit integers using truncation toward zero, ignoring the MXCSR rounding mode. AVX512-FP16, EVEX-encoded, with `{k}{z}` masking and broadcast. Input and output element widths are equal, so lane counts match across the register. **Truncation is always applied here; magnitudes outside the signed word range and NaNs produce the signed indefinite `0x8000`.**

column5: vcvttsh2si - truncate FP16 scalar to signed int
column6: `vcvttsh2si` converts the low half-precision (FP16) element of an XMM source to a signed integer in a general-purpose register, using truncation (round toward zero) regardless of the current rounding mode in `MXCSR`. The destination GPR size selects 32-bit or 64-bit output, e.g. `vcvttsh2si eax, xmm0` or `vcvttsh2si rax, xmm0`. **This is the truncating form** - unlike `vcvtsh2si` it ignores the rounding-control field, and an out-of-range or NaN source yields the integer indefinite value (0x80000000 / 0x8000000000000000), so range-check beforehand if you cannot tolerate that. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vcvttsh2usi - truncate FP16 scalar to unsigned int
column6: `vcvttsh2usi` converts the low FP16 element of an XMM source to an unsigned integer in a general-purpose register, truncating toward zero irrespective of the `MXCSR` rounding mode. The GPR width chooses a 32-bit or 64-bit unsigned result. **Because the result is unsigned, negative inputs, NaNs, and out-of-range values all produce the unsigned integer indefinite (all-ones) result** - there is no sign to preserve, so validate the source if you need a meaningful number. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vcvtudq2ph - packed u32 to FP16
column6: `vcvtudq2ph` converts packed unsigned doubleword (32-bit) integers from the source into packed half-precision (FP16) values in the destination. Because each result element is half the width of each source element, the FP16 outputs occupy the lower half of the destination register and the input can be a `zmm`/`ymm`/`xmm`, broadcast memory, or a `{rd-sae}`-controlled register. **Watch the width halving and the destination lane layout**, and note that values beyond FP16's ~2048 exact-integer range round per `MXCSR` (or the embedded rounding control). Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vcvtuqq2ph - packed u64 to FP16
column6: `vcvtuqq2ph` converts packed unsigned quadword (64-bit) integers into packed half-precision (FP16) values. Each 64-bit lane shrinks to a 16-bit result, so the FP16 outputs are packed into the low quarter of the destination and the source may use embedded rounding `{rd-sae}` or a broadcast. **Large 64-bit magnitudes overflow FP16's tiny exponent range and round to infinity, and most integers above ~2048 lose exactness** - this conversion is lossy by nature. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vcvtusi2sh - unsigned int to scalar FP16
column6: `vcvtusi2sh` converts a single unsigned integer in a general-purpose register or memory operand to a half-precision (FP16) value, writing it into the low element of the destination XMM while the upper FP16 elements are copied from a second source operand (the non-destructive VEX/EVEX source). The GPR operand width (`r/m32` or `r/m64`) selects 32-bit or 64-bit unsigned input, and `{rd-sae}` may supply embedded rounding. **Remember the three-operand form merges the high lanes from `src1`, so pick that source deliberately**, and large integers round to FP16 per the rounding control. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vcvtuw2ph - packed u16 to FP16
column6: `vcvtuw2ph` converts packed unsigned word (16-bit) integers into packed half-precision (FP16) values, element for element at the same lane width, with optional broadcast and embedded rounding `{rd-sae}`. Because source and destination elements are both 16-bit, the lane count is preserved. **Unsigned 16-bit values up to 65535 exceed FP16's exact-integer range (~2048), so most large inputs round** under the active rounding mode - this is expected, not a bug. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vcvtw2ph - packed signed word to FP16
column6: `vcvtw2ph` converts packed signed word (16-bit) integers into packed half-precision (FP16) values, lane-for-lane, supporting broadcast and `{rd-sae}` embedded rounding. The signed inputs are sign-interpreted before conversion. **As with all integer-to-FP16 conversions, magnitudes above ~2048 lose exact representation and round per `MXCSR` or the embedded control** - use FP32 if you need lossless integer round-trips. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vdbpsadbw - dual block packed SAD on bytes
column6: `vdbpsadbw` computes double-block packed sum-of-absolute-differences on unsigned bytes: an 8-bit immediate shuffles bytes of the second source into overlapping blocks, then four absolute-difference sums per 64-bit lane are produced as unsigned word results in the destination. It is the AVX-512 generalization of `psadbw` with a programmable second-operand byte selection. **The `imm8` controls how the source-2 bytes are gathered into the sliding windows, so the wrong immediate silently produces wrong sums** - and remember outputs are 16-bit words, not the single 64-bit sum that `psadbw` gives. Requires AVX-512-BW.

column5: vdivph - divide packed FP16 values
column6: `vdivph` divides packed half-precision (FP16) elements of the first source by the corresponding elements of the second source, writing packed FP16 quotients, with optional broadcast, masking, and embedded rounding `{er}`. Operates across the full `xmm`/`ymm`/`zmm` width, one division per 16-bit lane. **Division by zero raises the divide exception (or yields a signed infinity if the exception is masked), and FP16's limited precision means results are far coarser than FP32** - it touches no integer flags, only `MXCSR`. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vdivsh - divide scalar FP16 values
column6: `vdivsh` divides the low FP16 element of the first source by the low FP16 element of the second source and writes the quotient to the low lane of the destination, copying the upper 112 bits of the destination from `src1`. Embedded rounding `{er}` is available. **Only lane 0 is computed; the rest are merged from `src1`, so do not assume the high elements come from a division** - and as a scalar FP op it sets no EFLAGS, only `MXCSR` status bits. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vdpbf16ps - bf16 dot product into FP32
column6: `vdpbf16ps` takes pairs of bfloat16 values from the two sources, multiplies each pair, and accumulates the two products into the corresponding single-precision (FP32) element of the destination (which is also the accumulator). It is the core BF16 dot-product primitive used for neural-network inner loops. **The destination is read-modify-write - it accumulates into existing FP32 contents, so zero it first if you want a fresh sum**, and the intermediate bf16 multiplies have bf16's narrow precision even though the accumulation is FP32. Requires AVX-512-BF16.

column5: verr/verw - verify segment readable/writable
column6: `verr` checks whether the segment named by the 16-bit selector operand can be read from the current privilege level, and `verw` checks whether it can be written; both set `ZF=1` if the access would be permitted and `ZF=0` otherwise (also clearing ZF for null or invalid selectors). They let privileged code probe a selector without risking a fault. **The result lands in `ZF`, not in a register - test with `jz`/`jnz` immediately after**, and note `verw` only succeeds on writable data segments (code segments are never writable, and read-only/execute-only segments fail the respective check).

column5: vfcmaddcph - (conj) complex FMA packed FP16
column6: `vfcmaddcph` performs a complex fused multiply-add on packed FP16 data treated as interleaved (real, imaginary) pairs, multiplying by the complex conjugate of the second source and accumulating into the destination; the related `vfmaddcph` does the same without conjugation. Each 32-bit lane holds one complex FP16 number. **The destination is the accumulator (read-modify-write), so initialize it before the loop**, and the `c` form conjugates `src2` - mixing up the conjugated vs non-conjugated variant silently flips the sign of imaginary cross-terms. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vfcmaddcsh - (conj) complex FMA scalar FP16
column6: `vfcmaddcsh` does a scalar complex fused multiply-add on a single (real, imaginary) FP16 pair in the low 32 bits, conjugating the second source, while `vfmaddcsh` is the non-conjugating variant; the result accumulates into the low complex lane of the destination. **It operates only on lane 0 and accumulates into the existing destination value, so the high lanes and the prior accumulator both matter** - the `c` (conjugate) prefix changes the math, so choose the variant deliberately. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vfcmulcph - (conj) complex multiply packed FP16
column6: `vfcmulcph` multiplies packed complex FP16 (real, imaginary) pairs by the complex conjugate of the second source, writing the products to the destination; `vfmulcph` is the non-conjugating multiply. Unlike the FMA variants this overwrites the destination rather than accumulating. **There is no accumulation here - the destination is fully replaced**, and the `c` form conjugates `src2`, so picking the wrong variant inverts the imaginary part. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vfcmulcsh - (conj) complex multiply scalar FP16
column6: `vfcmulcsh` multiplies one scalar complex FP16 (real, imaginary) pair by the conjugate of the second source's low pair, writing the product to the low complex lane; `vfmulcsh` is the non-conjugating form. **Only lane 0 is computed and the upper destination lanes are merged from `src1`, while the result overwrites (does not accumulate into) the low lane** - and remember the conjugating `c` variant changes the sign of the imaginary cross-term. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vfixupimmpd - fix up special packed f64
column6: `vfixupimmpd` patches up special-case packed double-precision results (NaN, +/-infinity, +/-zero, negatives, etc.) according to a per-category 4-bit response selected from a control table in the second source, with an `imm8` choosing the token-to-response mapping. It is meant to clean up the output of approximation sequences like reciprocal or rsqrt before further use. **The `imm8` plus the table operand encode exactly which fixups happen, so this is driven entirely by that control data, not by arithmetic** - get the table or immediate wrong and special inputs pass through unfixed. Requires AVX-512F.

column5: vfixupimmps - fix up special packed f32
column6: `vfixupimmps` is the single-precision counterpart of `vfixupimmpd`: it classifies each packed FP32 element into a special-value category (NaN, infinity, zero, negative, and so on) and replaces it with a response chosen from a control table, with `imm8` selecting the mapping. It exists to sanitize the outputs of fast approximation routines. **Behavior is entirely table/immediate-driven, not numeric - the same input can be passed through, replaced, or turned into a NaN depending purely on the control operand.** Requires AVX-512F.

column5: vfixupimmsd - fix up special scalar f64
column6: `vfixupimmsd` applies the special-value fixup to a single scalar double-precision element in the low lane, choosing a per-category response from the control source under `imm8`, while the upper lane of the destination is merged from `src1`. Use it to repair one scalar approximation result. **Only lane 0 is fixed and the high lane comes from `src1`; the actual transformation is dictated by the control table and immediate**, so verify those rather than expecting arithmetic behavior. Requires AVX-512F.

column5: vfixupimmss - fix up special scalar f32
column6: `vfixupimmss` is the scalar single-precision fixup: it classifies the low FP32 element and substitutes a response selected from the control table per `imm8`, copying the upper destination lanes from `src1`. It sanitizes one scalar FP32 approximation result. **As with all the fixupimm forms, the output is determined by the control operand and immediate, not by computation - and only the low element is processed.** Requires AVX-512F.

column5: vfpclasspd - classify packed f64 into mask
column6: `vfpclasspd` tests each packed double-precision element against a set of floating-point categories selected by `imm8` (QNaN, SNaN, +/-infinity, denormal, negative zero, finite negative, etc.) and writes a one-bit-per-element result into an opmask register `k`. It classifies without computing anything. **The destination is a `k` mask, not a vector, and the `imm8` is a bitfield OR of category selectors - set the bits for every category you want to catch**, since an unset bit means that category reports false. Requires AVX-512DQ.

column5: vfpclassph - classify packed FP16 into mask
column6: `vfpclassph` tests each packed half-precision (FP16) element against the floating-point categories chosen by `imm8` and writes one result bit per element into an opmask register `k`. It is the FP16 analog of `vfpclasspd`/`vfpclassps`. **The result is a `k` mask and the `imm8` is an OR of category-select bits, so you must enable every category you care about**; the instruction performs no arithmetic and sets no EFLAGS. Requires AVX-512-FP16.

column5: vfpclassps - classify packed f32 into mask
column6: `vfpclassps` tests each packed single-precision element against the FP categories selected by `imm8` (NaN kinds, infinities, zeros, denormals, sign classes) and writes a one-bit-per-element verdict into an opmask `k` register. Use it to branch or mask on special values without arithmetic. **The output goes to a `k` mask and the `imm8` is a category bitmask - forgetting to set a category's bit makes that category test as false**, and the instruction leaves EFLAGS untouched. Requires AVX-512DQ.

column5: vfpclasssd - test class of scalar double
column6: `vfpclasssd` tests the low `float64` element of the source against a class predicate given by an 8-bit immediate (each bit selects a category: QNaN, negative, +0, -0, +inf, -inf, denormal, SNaN) and writes a single result bit into the destination opmask `k` register; the upper mask bits are zeroed. This is an EVEX/AVX-512 classification probe, not an arithmetic op. **It sets no EFLAGS and never raises an FP exception even on NaN/denormal inputs — the only output is the mask bit, so you must `kmov` it out or branch on it via `kortest`, and an optional writemask `{k}` can suppress the result.**

column5: vfpclasssh - test class of scalar FP16
column6: `vfpclasssh` examines the low `float16` element of the source and, for each class selected by the immediate (NaN, signed zeros, infinities, denormal, SNaN), sets the low bit of the destination opmask accordingly. It is the half-precision counterpart of `vfpclasssd`/`vfpclassss` and requires AVX-512 FP16. **No flags and no FP exceptions are produced — the answer lives entirely in the `k` mask, so test it with `kortest`/`ktest` rather than expecting ZF/PF to be touched.**

column5: vfpclassss - test class of scalar single
column6: `vfpclassss` tests the low `float32` element against the immediate class bitmap and deposits one result bit in the destination opmask register, zeroing the rest. Use it to classify a scalar without the side effects of a compare. **Like the other `vfpclass*` forms it touches no EFLAGS and signals no FP exceptions — even a signaling NaN is merely reported in the mask — so read the result out of `k` with `kmov`/`kortest` instead of relying on condition codes.**

column5: vgetexpph - extract FP16 exponents as FP16
column6: `vgetexpph` computes, for each packed `float16` element, the unbiased exponent as a floating value — effectively `floor(log2(|x|))` returned in FP16 form — which is the exponent half of an IEEE range-reduction step paired with `vgetmantph`. It supports EVEX masking and broadcast and requires AVX-512 FP16. **It writes no EFLAGS; special inputs follow fixed rules (e.g. `±0`→`-inf`, `±inf`→`+inf`, NaN→QNaN) rather than setting condition codes, so handle those cases by checking the values, not the flags.**

column5: vgetexpsh - extract scalar FP16 exponent
column6: `vgetexpsh` returns the unbiased exponent of the low `float16` element as an FP16 value and copies the remaining elements of the second source through to the destination. It is the scalar form of `vgetexpph`, used together with `vgetmantsh` for argument reduction. **No flags are affected and there are no condition-code outputs — denormals are normalized for the exponent and specials map to fixed results, so you inspect the produced value rather than EFLAGS.**

column5: vgetmantph - normalized FP16 mantissas
column6: `vgetmantph` extracts the normalized mantissa of each packed `float16` element into a chosen interval (selected by the immediate, e.g. `[1,2)` or `[0.5,1)`) and applies an immediate-controlled sign rule, producing the mantissa half of an IEEE range reduction paired with `vgetexpph`. EVEX masking and broadcast are supported under AVX-512 FP16. **It sets no EFLAGS; specials are handled by fixed substitution (e.g. NaN→QNaN, infinities/zeros mapped per the rules) rather than reported in flags, so test the result value if you need to detect them.**

column5: vgetmantps - normalized float32 mantissas
column6: `vgetmantps` returns the normalized mantissa of each packed `float32` element in an immediate-selected range with an immediate-selected sign treatment, the companion of `vgetexpps` for software range reduction. It is an EVEX/AVX-512 op supporting writemasking and a broadcast source. **No condition codes are produced — the immediate fully controls normalization interval and sign handling, and special inputs follow defined mappings, so there is nothing to read from EFLAGS afterward.**

column5: vgetmantsd - normalized scalar double mantissa
column6: `vgetmantsd` extracts the normalized mantissa of the low `float64` element into an immediate-chosen interval with an immediate-chosen sign rule, passing the upper element of the source through unchanged. It is the scalar double form used with `vgetexpsd`. **It affects no flags; normalization range and sign come entirely from the immediate and specials are substituted by rule, so do not look to EFLAGS for the outcome.**

column5: vgetmantsh - normalized scalar FP16 mantissa
column6: `vgetmantsh` produces the normalized mantissa of the low `float16` element in an immediate-selected interval and sign, copying the other elements from the second source. It pairs with `vgetexpsh` for half-precision range reduction and needs AVX-512 FP16. **No EFLAGS are touched — interval and sign are immediate-controlled and special values map to fixed results, so inspect the value, not the condition codes.**

column5: vgetmantss - normalized scalar single mantissa
column6: `vgetmantss` extracts the normalized mantissa of the low `float32` element into an immediate-controlled range and sign while merging the upper elements from the source, the scalar partner of `vgetexpss`. **It writes no flags; everything about normalization is driven by the immediate and specials are handled by defined substitution, so the result is conveyed only through the destination register, never through EFLAGS.**

column5: vmaskmov - conditional packed load/store
column6: `vmaskmov` (the `vmaskmovps`/`vmaskmovpd`/`vpmaskmov*` AVX family) conditionally loads or stores packed elements: each lane is moved only if the high bit of the corresponding mask-register lane is set, and on load the masked-out destination lanes are zeroed while on store the masked-out memory bytes are left untouched. This is the VEX (pre-AVX-512) per-element predication mechanism, where the mask lives in an xmm/ymm register rather than a `k` mask. **Masked-out store lanes genuinely do not fault, which lets you do edge-of-array stores safely — but the mask bit examined is the sign bit of each lane only, not the whole element, so set the top bit, and it touches no EFLAGS.**

column5: vmaxph - packed FP16 maximum
column6: `vmaxph` returns, per packed `float16` lane, the larger of the two source operands, supporting EVEX masking and broadcast under AVX-512 FP16. **As with all x86 max ops the NaN and signed-zero behaviour is asymmetric: if either operand is NaN, or the values are `+0` and `-0`, the result is the second source operand — so `max(a,b)` is not commutative for those cases. It sets no EFLAGS.**

column5: vmaxsh - scalar FP16 maximum
column6: `vmaxsh` computes the maximum of the low `float16` elements of the two sources and passes the upper elements of the first source through to the destination. **Its NaN/zero handling matches the rest of the family: a NaN operand or a `+0`/`-0` tie yields the second source operand, so the operation is order-sensitive — and no flags are affected.**

column5: vminph - packed FP16 minimum
column6: `vminph` returns the per-lane minimum of two packed `float16` sources, with EVEX writemasking and broadcast available under AVX-512 FP16. **Like `minss`/`minps`, if either operand is NaN or the inputs are `±0`, the result is the second source operand, making the op non-commutative in exactly those corner cases. No EFLAGS are written.**

column5: vminsh - scalar FP16 minimum
column6: `vminsh` takes the minimum of the low `float16` elements of its two sources and copies the remaining elements from the first source into the result. **Watch the standard min quirk: a NaN input or a `+0`/`-0` comparison returns the second source operand rather than a defined smaller value, so it does not behave like a clean commutative min — and it leaves all flags untouched.**

column5: vmovsh - move scalar FP16
column6: `vmovsh` moves a single `float16` value between an xmm register and memory, or merges it into the low lane of a destination register. Register-to-register forms take a separate source for the upper lanes (merge semantics), while a load from memory zeroes the rest of the destination. **Like other scalar moves it computes nothing and sets no flags; just mind whether the form you pick merges the upper bits or zeroes them, since that distinction trips up FP16 register reuse.**

column5: vmovw - move 16-bit word to/from xmm
column6: `vmovw` transfers a 16-bit word between the low element of an xmm register and either a general-purpose register or memory, zeroing the upper bits of the xmm destination on a load. It is the AVX-512 FP16 word analogue of `vmovd`/`vmovq`. **It is a pure data move with no flag effects; remember that loading into an xmm zero-extends through the rest of the register, so you cannot use it to merge a word into an existing vector.**

column5: vmulph - multiply packed FP16
column6: `vmulph` multiplies two packed `float16` sources lane-by-lane, supporting EVEX masking, broadcast, and embedded rounding control under AVX-512 FP16. **It sets no EFLAGS; FP16's tiny dynamic range means products overflow to infinity or underflow to zero far sooner than with `float32`, so scale your operands accordingly rather than expecting any saturation flag.**

column5: vmulsh - multiply scalar FP16
column6: `vmulsh` multiplies the low `float16` elements of its two sources and copies the upper elements of the first source into the destination, with optional embedded rounding. **No flags are affected; as with `vmulph` the limited FP16 exponent range makes overflow/underflow easy, and the result is reported only in the register, never via condition codes.**

column5: vol. 2d 8 - PDF page-header artifact
column6: **This is not a real instruction.** "vol. 2d 8" with title "16" is a running page header / volume-and-page reference ("Vol. 2D 8-16") from the Intel SDM that the extractor mistook for a mnemonic. It has no opcode or operands. **Delete this entry from the instruction table; it is pagination text.**

column5: (not an instruction) - PDF artifact
column6: This row is not a real instruction; `vol. 2d 8` / `4` is a fragment of the Intel SDM Volume 2D page footer that leaked into the extraction. **There is no opcode here — skip it. If you intended a specific VEX/EVEX instruction whose description begins on Intel SDM Vol. 2D page 8-4, re-extract that page rather than treating this footer text as a mnemonic.**

column5: (not an instruction) - PDF artifact
column6: This row is not a real instruction; `vol. 2d 8` / `6` is another Intel SDM Volume 2D page-footer fragment captured by the PDF extractor. **No opcode exists for it — skip it. The real instruction text would be on the referenced SDM page; nothing assemblable is encoded by this footer.**

column5: vp2intersectd - dword set intersection to mask pair
column6: `vp2intersectd` (and the qword `vp2intersectq`) compares every `dword`/`qword` element of one source against every element of the other and writes a pair of opmask registers `k:k+1` indicating, for each source, which of its lanes has a match in the other vector. It is meant to accelerate sparse/set-intersection kernels. **The destination is an even/odd opmask register pair selected by the encoded `k` (the low bit is ignored), and the instruction sets no EFLAGS — you consume the two masks with `kmov`/`kortest`, not condition codes.**

column5: vp4dpwssd - 4-iteration signed-word dot product
column6: `vp4dpwssd` performs four chained rounds of signed 16-bit-word pairwise multiply-add into 32-bit `dword` accumulators, reading four consecutive source registers as a block and a single memory operand, for AVX-512_4VNNIW. **The first source must be a register aligned to a group of four (the hardware reads `src1`..`src1+3` as one tile), and the memory operand is 16-byte aligned by requirement — get the register grouping wrong and you feed the wrong tiles; it writes no flags.**

column5: vpblendd - blend dwords by immediate
column6: `vpblendd` selects each `dword` lane of the result from one of two source vectors according to the bits of an 8-bit immediate (bit set → take from the second source). It is a VEX/AVX2 immediate blend with no per-element mask register. **The selection is fixed at assemble time by the immediate — there is no runtime mask and the high immediate bits beyond the lane count are ignored for xmm — and it touches no EFLAGS; use `vpblendvb` if you need a runtime variable mask.**

column5: vpblendmb - blend bytes/words by opmask
column6: `vpblendmb` (byte) and `vpblendmw` (word) merge two source vectors element-by-element under control of an opmask `k` register: where the mask bit is 1 the element comes from the second source, otherwise from the first. These are EVEX/AVX-512BW blends using a true runtime mask. **The blend mask is the explicit `k` operand here, not the usual `{k}` writemask, so do not confuse the two — and the instruction sets no flags.**

column5: vpblendmd - blend dwords/qwords by opmask
column6: `vpblendmd` (dword) and `vpblendmq` (qword) blend two source vectors per element according to an opmask `k`: a set bit selects the second source, a clear bit the first. They are the 32-/64-bit EVEX/AVX-512F counterparts of `vpblendmb`/`vpblendmw`. **The `k` register here is the selection control, distinct from an optional zeroing/merging writemask, and no EFLAGS are affected — the mask is the entire decision.**

column5: vpbroadcast - load integer and broadcast
column6: The `vpbroadcast` family (`vpbroadcastb/w/d/q`) replicates a single integer element — taken from the low lane of an xmm, from memory, or in AVX-512 from a general-purpose register — across every lane of the destination vector. **The element size is encoded in the mnemonic suffix, not inferred from the operand, so pick `vpbroadcastd` vs `vpbroadcastq` deliberately; it performs no arithmetic and sets no flags. The GPR-source form requires AVX-512.**

column5: vpbroadcastm - broadcast opmask to vector
column6: `vpbroadcastm` (the `vpbroadcastmb2q`/`vpbroadcastmw2d` forms) takes the low bits of an opmask `k` register and broadcasts the mask value, zero-extended, into every `qword` or `dword` lane of a vector register. It bridges the mask domain back into the vector domain. **The source is a `k` register, not a vector or memory, and it requires AVX-512CD; it sets no EFLAGS — use it to materialize a mask as integer data, not to test it.**

column5: vpcmpb - compare bytes into opmask
column6: `vpcmpb` (signed) and `vpcmpub` (unsigned) compare packed byte elements of two sources using a comparison predicate from a 3-bit immediate (eq, lt, le, neq, nlt, nle, plus false/true) and write a one-bit-per-lane result into a destination opmask `k`. **The comparison kind is in the immediate and the signedness is in the mnemonic — pick `vpcmpb` vs `vpcmpub` to match your data, since choosing wrong silently flips lt/gt results — and the only output is the `k` mask, with no EFLAGS touched.**

column5: vpcmpd - compare packed dwords into a mask
column6: Compares packed signed dwords in `zmm2`/`ymm`/`xmm` against `zmm3`/m512 using an immediate predicate (`0`=EQ, `1`=LT, `2`=LE, `4`=NE, `5`=NLT, `6`=NLE) and writes the boolean result of each lane into mask register `k1`, optionally AND-ed with a writemask `k2`. `vpcmpud` does the same on unsigned dwords. This is the AVX-512 replacement for the old `vpcmpeqd`/`vpcmpgtd` pair, which produced an all-ones SIMD result instead of a mask. **The comparison predicate is an `imm8`, not part of the mnemonic, and the destination is a `k` mask register, not an XMM/YMM/ZMM — pick `vpcmpd` for signed and `vpcmpud` for unsigned, since choosing wrong silently flips ordered comparisons around the sign boundary.**

column5: vpcmpq - compare packed qwords into a mask
column6: Compares packed signed qwords in the source vector against `zmm3`/m512 (or a broadcast m64) using an `imm8` predicate (`0`=EQ, `1`=LT, `2`=LE, `4`=NE, `5`=NLT, `6`=NLE) and stores one result bit per qword lane in mask `k1` under optional writemask `k2`. `vpcmpuq` performs the unsigned variant. **The result goes to a `k` mask register, not a vector — and `vpcmpq` (signed) versus `vpcmpuq` (unsigned) is the only thing distinguishing magnitude ordering when high bits are set, so match the variant to your data's signedness.**

column5: vpcmpw - compare packed words into a mask
column6: Compares packed signed words against the second source using an `imm8` predicate (`0`=EQ, `1`=LT, `2`=LE, `4`=NE, `5`=NLT, `6`=NLE) and writes one bit per word lane into mask register `k1`, optionally masked by `k2`. `vpcmpuw` is the unsigned form. This needs AVX-512BW. **The predicate is the immediate, not encoded in the mnemonic, and the destination is a `k` register — use `vpcmpw` for signed words and `vpcmpuw` for unsigned, or ordered comparisons will be wrong for values with the top bit set.**

column5: vpcompressb - pack mask-selected bytes densely
column6: Takes the byte (or word, with `vpcompressw`) lanes selected by mask `k1` and writes them contiguously, low-to-high, into the start of the destination register or memory, dropping the unselected lanes. It is the inverse of `vpexpandb` and is the byte/word analogue of `vpcompressd`/`vpcompressq`. This requires AVX-512_VBMI2. **The mask selects which elements survive, not where they land — output is always densely packed from lane 0, so the destination's upper lanes (for a register target with merge-masking) or trailing memory are untouched, and you must derive the written count from `popcnt(k1)` yourself.**

column5: vpdpbusd - multiply u8*s8, add to dword accumulator
column6: For each dword lane, multiplies four unsigned bytes from the first source by four signed bytes from the second source and adds the four products into the corresponding signed dword of the destination, which also serves as the accumulator. This is the AVX-512_VNNI fused multiply-add for INT8 dot products used heavily in neural-net inference. **The operand signedness is fixed and asymmetric — the first source is treated as unsigned bytes, the second as signed bytes — so swapping the operands changes the result; `vpdpbusd` does not saturate, so a long accumulation can overflow the dword silently (use `vpdpbusds` if you need saturation).**

column5: vpdpbusds - u8*s8 dot-product, saturating add
column6: Multiplies four unsigned bytes by four signed bytes per dword lane and adds the products into the destination's signed dword accumulator with signed saturation to `INT32` bounds. It is the saturating sibling of `vpdpbusd` from AVX-512_VNNI. **As with `vpdpbusd`, the first source is unsigned and the second is signed — the asymmetry is not interchangeable — and here the accumulate clamps to `0x7FFFFFFF`/`0x80000000` instead of wrapping, so use this variant when overflow must not corrupt unrelated bits.**

column5: vpdpwssd - multiply s16*s16, add to dword acc
column6: For each dword lane, multiplies two pairs of signed words from the two sources and adds both products into the destination's signed dword accumulator. This is the AVX-512_VNNI INT16 dot-product analogue of the older `pmaddwd`, but it accumulates into the destination instead of just producing the sum. **Both sources are signed words, and the add is non-saturating — a long running accumulation can overflow the 32-bit lane and wrap silently, so use `vpdpwssds` when you need saturation.**

column5: vpdpwssds - s16*s16 dot-product, saturating add
column6: Multiplies two pairs of signed words per dword lane and adds both products into the destination's signed dword accumulator with signed saturation to 32-bit bounds. It is the saturating counterpart of `vpdpwssd` in AVX-512_VNNI. **The accumulate clamps to `INT32` limits rather than wrapping, which protects against silent overflow but also means the result stops increasing once saturated — pick this variant deliberately when clamping is the desired behavior, not just to silence overflow.**

column5: vperm2f128 - shuffle 128-bit float lanes across 256
column6: Selects two 128-bit lanes from the concatenation of the two 256-bit float sources, controlled by an `imm8` (low nibble picks the result's low lane, high nibble the high lane), and can zero either output lane when the imm8's bit 3 or bit 7 is set. This is the AVX1 cross-lane float shuffle that ordinary in-lane permutes cannot do. **It only moves whole 128-bit halves, not individual elements — it cannot rearrange within a lane — and bits 3 and 7 of the immediate force the corresponding output lane to zero, which is easy to set accidentally.**

column5: vperm2i128 - shuffle 128-bit int lanes across 256
column6: Selects two 128-bit lanes from the concatenation of two 256-bit integer sources according to an `imm8` (low nibble selects the low output lane, high nibble the high), with bits 3/7 zeroing the respective lane. It is the AVX2 integer twin of `vperm2f128`. **Like its float counterpart it operates on whole 128-bit halves only and cannot permute elements within a lane; remember the immediate's bit-3/bit-7 zeroing behavior or you will blank a lane you meant to copy.**

column5: vpermb - fully permute bytes by index vector
column6: Uses each byte of the index source as a selector to gather an arbitrary byte from anywhere in the second source vector into the destination, performing a full cross-lane byte shuffle. This needs AVX-512_VBMI and, unlike `pshufb`, is not restricted to within-lane selection. **Only the low bits of each index byte that are needed to address the source width are used (5 bits for 32 bytes, 6 for 64) — high index bits are ignored rather than zeroing the lane, so out-of-intent indices wrap into a valid element instead of producing zero.**

column5: vpermd - fully permute dwords/words by index
column6: Uses each dword of the index source to select any dword from the second source into the corresponding destination lane, giving a full cross-lane dword shuffle; `vpermw` does the same at word granularity (AVX-512BW). Unlike `vpshufd`, `vpermd` crosses the 128-bit lane boundary. **Only the low index bits needed for the element count are honored (the rest are ignored, not masked to zero), and note `vpermd` has no immediate form — the selectors come from a register/memory source, not an `imm8`.**

column5: vpermi2b - permute bytes from two tables, keep index
column6: Treats the two source operands as a single concatenated 2N-byte table and uses each byte of the destination (which holds the indices) to select an element from either table, writing the result back over the index register. This AVX-512_VBMI op lets one index vector address twice the elements. **The destination is both the index input and the result output, so the original index values are destroyed — if you need to reuse the indices, copy them first or use `vpermt2b`, which overwrites a table instead.**

column5: vpermi2w - permute from two tables, overwrite index
column6: Concatenates the two sources into one logical table and uses the index lanes held in the destination to select elements from either table, overwriting the index register with the gathered result; the suffix selects element size (`w` word, `d` dword, `q` qword, `ps`/`pd` float). **The index register is clobbered by the output — these `vpermi2*` forms consume the indices in place, so preserve them beforehand or switch to the matching `vpermt2*` form when you need to keep the index vector live.**

column5: vpermilpd - in-lane permute of double pairs
column6: Permutes the two double-precision elements within each 128-bit lane independently, selecting per lane from either an immediate or the low bit of each control element in a register source. It is purely in-lane — the two 128-bit halves of a 256-bit operand never exchange data. **This is an in-lane shuffle only: it cannot move a double across the 128-bit lane boundary; for cross-lane double rearrangement you need `vpermpd`. With a register control, only the selector bit at each element's position matters, not the whole control value.**

column5: vpermilps - in-lane permute of single floats
column6: Permutes the four single-precision elements within each 128-bit lane independently, with the per-element selection coming from either an `imm8` or a register control vector. Both 128-bit lanes use the same immediate pattern when the imm form is used. **Like `vpermilpd` this never crosses the 128-bit lane boundary — element 0 of the high lane can only come from the high lane — so use `vpermps` when you need a genuine cross-lane single-precision permute.**

column5: vpermpd - full cross-lane permute of doubles
column6: Selects any of the source's double-precision elements into each destination lane, either by an `imm8` (for the four-element 256-bit case) or by a per-lane index in a register source for wider forms, crossing the 128-bit lane boundary freely. This is the cross-lane complement to the in-lane `vpermilpd`. **Unlike the in-lane permutes it genuinely moves doubles between lanes, so it is the right tool for transposes and broadcasts that `vpermilpd` cannot express; the immediate form packs one 2-bit selector per element.**

column5: vpermps - full cross-lane permute of singles
column6: Uses each dword index in the control source to gather any single-precision element from the second source into the corresponding destination lane, crossing 128-bit lane boundaries. It is the float counterpart of `vpermd` and the cross-lane sibling of `vpermilps`. **There is no immediate form — the selectors live in a register/memory index operand — and only the low index bits for the element count are used, so stray high bits wrap to a valid lane rather than zeroing.**

column5: vpermq - full cross-lane permute of qwords
column6: Permutes the qword elements of the source into the destination across the full 128-bit lane boundary, selected either by an `imm8` (one 2-bit field per qword in the 256-bit case) or by a register index for wider AVX-512 forms. It is the integer/qword cross-lane shuffle that in-lane ops cannot do. **The `imm8` form selects each output qword from any source qword regardless of lane, which is exactly what makes it useful for 256-bit reductions and transposes; do not confuse it with the in-lane `vpshufd`-style shuffles that stay within 128 bits.**

column5: vpermt2b - permute bytes from two tables, keep table
column6: Concatenates the two source tables and uses an index vector to gather bytes from either table into the destination, but unlike `vpermi2b` it overwrites one of the tables rather than the index register. Requires AVX-512_VBMI. **Here the table operand is the one clobbered by the result, and the index register survives — choose `vpermt2b` over `vpermi2b` when you want to reuse the same index vector across several permutes and can afford to lose the table.**

column5: vpermt2w - permute from two tables, overwrite a table
column6: Gathers elements from a logical two-table concatenation using an index vector, writing the result over one of the source tables; the suffix sets element width (`w`/`d`/`q`/`ps`/`pd`). It is the table-overwriting counterpart to the index-overwriting `vpermt2w` family. **The destination doubles as one of the input tables and is destroyed, while the index vector is preserved — the mirror image of the `vpermi2*` forms — so pick the family based on whether you need to keep the index or the table.**

column5: vpexpandb - scatter dense bytes to masked lanes
column6: Reads contiguous bytes (or words, with `vpexpandw`) from the source and distributes them into the destination lanes selected by mask `k1`, leaving unselected lanes either zeroed or merged depending on the masking mode. It is the inverse of `vpcompressb` and needs AVX-512_VBMI2. **The source is read densely from the start but the destination placement is sparse, driven entirely by the mask — the number of source elements consumed equals `popcnt(k1)`, so a mask with too few bits set leaves later source bytes unread.**

column5: vpexpandq - scatter dense qwords to masked lanes
column6: Loads consecutive qwords from dense memory or a register and places them into the destination lanes whose mask bit in `k1` is set, with the remaining lanes zeroed or left intact per the masking mode. It is the qword inverse of `vpcompressq`. **It consumes exactly `popcnt(k1)` qwords from the source, placing the i-th read element into the i-th set mask lane — the source advances only for set bits, so the source need not be as wide as the destination vector.**

column5: vpmaskmovd - masked dword vector load/store
column6: Conditionally loads or stores packed dwords (`vpmaskmovd`) or qwords (`vpmaskmovq`) between memory and a vector register, where the high bit of each element of a mask vector decides whether that lane participates; masked-out lanes read as zero on a load and are left untouched in memory on a store. This is the AVX2 integer form of `vmaskmovps`/`vmaskmovpd` and uses a vector mask, not a `k` register. **Masked-out elements on a store generate no memory write and therefore suppress faults for those addresses, which is what makes this safe at array boundaries; the mask is taken from each element's top bit, not a `k` mask register.**

column5: vpmovdb - narrow dwords to bytes
column6: Truncates each packed dword down to a byte and writes the bytes to the destination register or memory; `vpmovsdb` saturates signed dword to signed byte, and `vpmovusdb` saturates unsigned dword to unsigned byte. The destination is one-quarter the width of the source. **Plain `vpmovdb` just keeps the low 8 bits and discards the rest with no saturation, so values outside `-128..255` wrap — reach for the `s`/`us` variants when out-of-range inputs must clamp instead of wrap, and remember the destination width is a quarter of the source.**

column5: vpmovdw - narrow dwords to words
column6: Converts each packed dword to a word, writing a half-width result; `vpmovdw` truncates the low 16 bits, `vpmovsdw` saturates signed-to-signed, and `vpmovusdw` saturates unsigned dword to unsigned word. **The plain form silently discards the high 16 bits of each lane, so any value not fitting in 16 bits wraps — use `vpmovsdw`/`vpmovusdw` if you need saturation, and size the destination as half the source width.**

column5: vpmovqb - narrow qwords to bytes
column6: Down-converts each packed qword to a single byte, producing a destination one-eighth the source width; `vpmovqb` truncates to the low 8 bits, while `vpmovsqb` and `vpmovusqb` apply signed and unsigned saturation respectively. **The truncating form throws away 56 bits per lane with no overflow signal, so unless the inputs already fit in a byte you almost certainly want the saturating `s`/`us` variant; the result occupies only the low eighth of the destination register.**

column5: vpmovqd - narrow packed qwords down to dwords
column6: Truncates each packed 64-bit element of the source down to 32 bits, writing half-width results to the destination; the result vector is half the element-width of the source. The `vpmovsqd`/`vpmovusqd` forms instead saturate to the signed/unsigned dword range rather than discarding the high bits. **Because the destination holds only half as many bytes as the source, the output is a narrower register (e.g. `zmm`->`ymm`), and a memory destination writes exactly that half-width — mask bits apply per element, so plan your `{k}` writemask around the smaller element count.**

column5: vpmovqw - narrow packed qwords down to words
column6: Down-converts each packed 64-bit element to a 16-bit word, shrinking the data to a quarter of its original width. `vpmovsqw` and `vpmovusqw` saturate into the signed or unsigned word range instead of plain truncation. **The destination is one quarter the byte size of the source (a `zmm` source yields only 128 bits of output), so a register destination is an `xmm` and a memory destination writes just those bytes — size your writemask `{k}` to the source's element count, not the destination's.**

column5: vpmovwb - narrow packed words down to bytes
column6: Converts each packed 16-bit word to an 8-bit byte, producing a result half the width of the source. `vpmovswb`/`vpmovuswb` clamp to the signed/unsigned byte range; the plain form just keeps the low 8 bits of each word. **The destination is half the source's byte size (e.g. `zmm`->`ymm`), and with so many byte lanes the per-element `{k}` writemask must be sized for the word count, not the byte count — easy to off-by-two if you reuse a byte-granular mask.**

column5: vpmultishiftqb - gather unaligned byte fields from qwords
column6: For each output byte, reads an index, then extracts 8 contiguous (possibly bit-unaligned) bits from the corresponding source quadword starting at that bit offset, wrapping within the 64-bit lane. It is effectively a per-byte arbitrary bit-field extractor used to pack and unpack sub-byte data. **Indices are bit offsets taken modulo 64 and the extraction stays inside its own qword lane — it never crosses into the neighbouring quadword, so you cannot pull a field that straddles a 64-bit boundary.**

column5: vpopcnt - count set bits in each packed element
column6: Counts the number of 1 bits in each packed element (byte, word, dword, or qword depending on the suffix) and writes that population count into the same element of the destination. Each lane is independent. **This is the SIMD population count and needs AVX512_VPOPCNTDQ/BITALG support; unlike scalar `popcnt` it touches no flags, and the byte/word forms in particular require the BITALG feature, so guard it behind a CPUID check before emitting.**

column5: vprold - rotate packed dword/qword bits left
column6: Rotates the bits of each packed dword (`vprold`/`vprolvd`) or qword (`vprolq`/`vprolvq`) element left, with the bits shifted off the top re-entering at the bottom. The non-`v` forms take one immediate count applied to every lane; the `vprolv*` forms take a per-element variable count from a second vector. **Rotate counts are taken modulo the element width (32 or 64), and unlike scalar rotates this leaves no carry and touches no flags — there is no shared count register, the imm or the per-lane vector supplies it.**

column5: vprord - rotate packed dword/qword bits right
column6: Rotates the bits of each packed dword or qword element right, with low bits wrapping back into the high end. `vprord`/`vprorq` use one immediate for all lanes, while `vprorvd`/`vprorvq` rotate each lane by its own count from a second source vector. **Counts wrap modulo the element width (32 or 64) and no flags are produced; to get a left rotate by `n` you can equivalently rotate right by `width - n`.**

column5: vpshld - funnel-shift packed elements left
column6: Concatenates each element of the first source (high part) with the matching element of the second source (low part), shifts the double-width value left by an immediate, and keeps the upper half as the result. It is a packed funnel (double) shift that pulls fresh bits in from the second operand instead of zeros. **The shift count is an immediate masked to the element width, and operand order matters: the first source supplies the high bits that survive, the second feeds in from the right — swap them and you get garbage.**

column5: vpshldv - variable funnel-shift packed left
column6: Like `vpshld` but the left funnel-shift amount for each element comes from a third source vector rather than an immediate, so every lane can shift by a different count. Each element concatenates the two sources, shifts the pair left, and keeps the high half. **The per-lane counts are masked to the element width, and this is a three-source form that overwrites the destination/first-source in place — make sure the writemask and the count vector line up element-for-element.**

column5: vpshrd - funnel-shift packed elements right
column6: Concatenates each element of the second source (high part) with the first source (low part), shifts the double-width value right by an immediate, and keeps the low half. It is the right-going packed funnel shift, pulling bits in from the adjacent operand instead of shifting in zeros. **The count is an immediate masked to the element width and operand order is the mirror of `vpshld`: here the low half of the result comes from the first source — getting the two sources backwards silently corrupts the result.**

column5: vpshrdv - variable funnel-shift packed right
column6: Performs a right funnel shift per element where the shift amount comes from a third source vector, letting each lane shift by its own count. Each element concatenates the two data sources and keeps the low half of the shifted double-width value. **Counts are masked to the element width per lane, and as a three-source in-place op the destination doubles as the first source — confirm the count vector is element-aligned with the data or lanes shift by the wrong amount.**

column5: vpshufbitqmb - gather bits from qwords into a mask
column6: For each byte index in the second source, selects a single bit (0-63) from the corresponding source quadword and writes it into the matching bit position of a destination mask register. It effectively shuffles/extracts up to 64 bits out of each qword into a `k` mask. **The destination is a mask register, not a vector; indices are taken modulo 64 within each qword lane and only the low 6 bits of each index byte matter, so out-of-range index bytes still select a valid bit rather than zeroing.**

column5: vpsllvw - per-element logical shift left
column6: Shifts each packed word/dword/qword element left by a count taken from the same lane of a second vector, filling the vacated low bits with zeros. Every element can shift by a different amount. **Unlike the immediate/`xmm`-count shifts, the count is per element and is NOT masked to the element width: any count greater than or equal to the element size produces a zero result for that lane, so don't rely on count-modulo behaviour here.**

column5: vpsravw - per-element arithmetic shift right
column6: Arithmetically shifts each packed element right by its own per-lane count from a second vector, replicating the sign bit into the vacated high bits so negative values stay negative. **Counts are per element and unsaturated: a count at or beyond the element width fills the whole lane with copies of the sign bit (all-ones for negatives, zero for non-negatives) rather than wrapping — use this form, not `vpsrlv*`, when you need sign extension.**

column5: vpsrlvw - per-element logical shift right
column6: Shifts each packed word/dword/qword element right by a count drawn from the corresponding lane of a second vector, shifting zeros into the high bits. Each lane shifts independently. **The per-lane count is not reduced modulo the element width: a count at or above the element size zeroes that lane entirely, which differs from scalar shifts that mask the count — and the word-granular `vpsrlvw` needs AVX512BW.**

column5: vpternlogd - arbitrary 3-input bitwise logic
column6: Computes any Boolean function of three packed source operands bit-by-bit, where the 8-bit immediate is the truth table: bit `n` of imm8 gives the output for the input combination whose value is `n` (formed from the three source bits). One instruction can express AND/OR/XOR/MUX/majority and every other 3-input function. **The destination is also the first source operand and is overwritten in place, so it must hold one of your three inputs; pick the imm8 by writing out the truth table (e.g. 0xCA for select, 0x96 for three-way XOR) rather than guessing.**

column5: vptestnmb - per-element AND, set mask if zero
column6: Bitwise-ANDs the two source elements lane by lane and sets the corresponding bit in the destination mask register to 1 when that AND is zero (the NAND-style test), or 0 otherwise. The width suffix selects byte/word/dword/qword granularity. **The result goes into a `k` mask register, not a vector, and the sense is inverted relative to `vptestmb`: a set mask bit means the lanes had no common bits — confirm you want the zero test, not the non-zero one.**

column5: vrangepd - packed min/max/range select, float64
column6: Performs an immediate-selected range operation on each pair of packed double-precision values — min, max, min-by-absolute-value, or max-by-absolute-value — with separate immediate control over how the result's sign is taken. It generalizes packed min/max with sign and magnitude handling in one op. **The low bits of the imm8 pick the compare (min/max/abs variants) and the high bits pick the sign-control, so read the two immediate fields separately; NaN and signed-zero handling follow the range rules, not plain `vmaxpd`/`vminpd` semantics.**

column5: vrangeps - packed min/max/range select, float32
column6: Applies an immediate-controlled range operation (min, max, or their absolute-value variants) to each pair of packed single-precision values, with a separate immediate field choosing how the result sign is determined. **As with `vrangepd`, the imm8 splits into a compare-select field and a sign-select field — set both deliberately; the special-value behaviour for NaNs and signed zeros differs from a naive `vmaxps`/`vminps`.**

column5: vrangesd - scalar min/max/range select, float64
column6: Computes the immediate-selected range operation (min/max/abs-min/abs-max with sign control) on the low double-precision element of two sources and copies the upper element unchanged from the first source into the destination. **Only the low 64-bit lane is processed; the high lane is passed through from the first operand, so this is a scalar op despite the vector registers — and the imm8 again carries both the compare and the sign-control fields.**

column5: vrangess - scalar min/max/range select, float32
column6: Performs the immediate-controlled range operation on the low single-precision element of two operands, leaving the upper elements of the destination taken from the first source. **As a scalar form only the low 32-bit element is computed while the rest passes through from the first operand; remember the imm8 encodes both which range compare and which sign rule to apply.**

column5: vrcp14pd - approximate reciprocals, packed float64
column6: Produces an approximate reciprocal (1/x) of each packed double-precision element with a relative error of at most about 2^-14. It is the fast-estimate path, far quicker than a true `vdivpd`. **The 14-bit accuracy is not full precision — follow it with a Newton-Raphson refinement step if you need correct doubles; the approximation is only guaranteed to that error bound, so don't treat the raw result as exact.**

column5: vrcp14ps - approximate reciprocals, packed float32
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal of each packed single-precision element, accurate to roughly 2^-14 relative error, as a fast alternative to dividing by `x`. **This is an estimate, not a correctly-rounded reciprocal; a single Newton-Raphson iteration brings it to near full single precision, so add that step when accuracy matters.**

column5: vrcp14sd - approximate reciprocal, scalar float64
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal of the low double-precision element (error bound ~2^-14) and copies the upper element of the destination from the first source. **Only the low lane is computed and the result is an approximation — refine with Newton-Raphson for full precision, and remember the high 64 bits are passed through from the first operand, not zeroed.**

column5: vrcp14ss - approximate reciprocal, scalar float32
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal of the low single-precision element to about 2^-14 relative error, with the upper elements of the destination taken from the first source. **It is a fast estimate only; apply a Newton-Raphson step for near-exact results, and note the unmodified high elements come from the first operand.**

column5: vrcpph - approximate reciprocals, packed FP16
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal of each packed half-precision (FP16) element. Given FP16's limited mantissa the estimate is close to the format's full precision in one step. **Requires AVX512-FP16 support; it is still an approximation and not IEEE-correctly-rounded, so verify the error bound is acceptable for your FP16 use before relying on it.**

column5: vrcpsh - approximate reciprocal, scalar FP16
column6: Computes an approximate reciprocal of the low half-precision element, copying the remaining upper elements of the destination from the first source. **Needs AVX512-FP16; only the low FP16 lane is computed and the result is an approximation rather than a rounded reciprocal — the upper bits pass through from the first operand.**

column5: vreducepd - extract fractional part, packed float64
column6: Subtracts from each packed double the value rounded to a chosen number of fixed-point fraction bits, leaving the low-order remainder — effectively `x - round(x)` scaled by an immediate-selected number of fraction bits. It isolates the fractional residue with controllable precision. **The imm8 selects both the number of retained fraction bits and the rounding mode; this is not a horizontal reduce-to-one-value despite the name, it operates per lane and returns a residue.**

column5: vreduceph - extract fractional part, packed FP16
column6: Computes the reduction (fractional residue after rounding to an immediate-controlled number of fraction bits) for each packed FP16 element. **Requires AVX512-FP16; the imm8 controls fraction-bit count and rounding, and as with the other `vreduce*` forms this is a per-element residue, not a horizontal reduction across the vector.**

column5: vreduceps - extract fractional part, packed float32
column6: For each packed single-precision element computes `x` minus its value rounded to an immediate-selected number of fraction bits, yielding the low-order residue with controllable precision. **The imm8 picks the fraction-bit count and rounding mode; despite 'reduction' in the name nothing is summed across lanes — each element independently produces its fractional remainder.**

column5: vreducesd - extract fractional part, scalar float64
column6: Computes the reduction (fractional residue) of the low double-precision element with imm8-controlled fraction bits and rounding, passing the upper element through from the first source. **Only the low lane is processed and the high 64 bits come from the first operand; the imm8 governs both precision and rounding, and this is a per-element residue op, not a horizontal reduce.**

column5: vreducesh - extract fractional part, scalar FP16
column6: Computes the fractional residue of the low FP16 element after rounding to an immediate-selected number of fraction bits, with the upper elements of the destination taken from the first source. **Requires AVX512-FP16; only the low half-precision lane is computed, the imm8 sets fraction bits and rounding, and the remaining elements pass through from the first operand.**

column5: vreducess - extract fractional part, scalar float32
column6: Computes the reduction (post-rounding fractional residue) of the low single-precision element using imm8-selected fraction bits and rounding, leaving the upper elements taken from the first source. **Scalar: only the low 32-bit lane is processed while the rest passes through from the first operand, and the imm8 carries both the fraction-bit count and the rounding control.**

column5: vrndscaleph - round packed FP16 to N fraction bits
column6: Rounds each packed half-precision (FP16) element of the source to a value with a specified number of fraction bits, controlled by an 8-bit immediate that selects the rounding mode (nearest/down/up/truncate) and the fraction-bit count. Writes the rounded FP16 lanes to the destination under the usual EVEX mask. This is the FP16 analog of `vroundps`/`vrndscaleps`. **Requires AVX-512-FP16; it is EVEX-only, has no flag effects, and the immediate's mode field overrides MXCSR rounding while the suppress-all-exceptions bit can mask precision faults — get the immediate encoding wrong and you silently round differently than intended.**

column5: vrndscalesh - round scalar FP16 to N fraction bits
column6: Rounds the low half-precision (FP16) element of the source to a value with the number of fraction bits and rounding mode chosen by an 8-bit immediate, leaving the upper bits of the destination copied from the first source. The scalar counterpart to `vrndscaleph`. **AVX-512-FP16, EVEX-only, affects no EFLAGS; the immediate selects rounding independent of MXCSR, so a stale or copy-pasted immediate gives wrong rounding even though the assembler accepts it.**

column5: vrsqrtph - approx reciprocal sqrt of packed FP16
column6: Computes an approximation of 1/sqrt(x) for each packed half-precision (FP16) element of the source and stores the FP16 results to the destination under mask. This is a fast estimate, not an exact result. **AVX-512-FP16; sets no flags. The result is only an approximation (relative error roughly 2^-14), so it is meant as a Newton-Raphson seed — do not treat it as a correctly-rounded reciprocal square root.**

column5: vrsqrtsh - approx reciprocal sqrt of scalar FP16
column6: Computes an approximation of 1/sqrt(x) for the low half-precision (FP16) element and writes it to the destination's low lane, copying the upper bits from the first source. **AVX-512-FP16, EVEX-only, no flag effects. As with all the reciprocal-estimate ops this is approximate (about 2^-14 relative error) — refine with a Newton-Raphson step if you need accuracy.**

column5: vscalefpd - scale packed f64 by 2^floor(exp)
column6: Computes `x * 2^floor(y)` for each packed double-precision lane, taking x from the first source and the exponent y from the second source/memory operand, and writes the results under EVEX mask. It is effectively a vectorized `scalbn`/`ldexp`. **AVX-512; sets no flags. The exponent operand is floored before use and the result honors MXCSR rounding/overflow — useful for fast power-of-two scaling without touching the mantissa, but remember y is the second source, not an immediate.**

column5: vscalefph - scale packed FP16 by 2^floor(exp)
column6: Computes `x * 2^floor(y)` for each packed half-precision (FP16) lane, x from the first source and the exponent y from the second source, writing results under mask. The FP16 form of `scalbn`. **AVX-512-FP16, no flag effects. The exponent source is floored before scaling, so fractional bits in y are ignored — pass the intended integer exponent in the second operand.**

column5: vscalefps - scale packed f32 by 2^floor(exp)
column6: Computes `x * 2^floor(y)` for each packed single-precision lane, x from the first source and the scale exponent y from the second source/memory, storing under EVEX mask. A vectorized `ldexp` for floats. **AVX-512; touches no EFLAGS. y is floored first and the operation respects MXCSR — handy for normalization and FP exponent manipulation without separate mantissa work.**

column5: vscalefsd - scale scalar f64 by 2^floor(exp)
column6: Computes `x * 2^floor(y)` for the low double-precision lane, x from the first source and exponent y from the second, copying the upper bits from the first source. Scalar `scalbn` for doubles. **AVX-512, EVEX-only, no flag effects. The exponent is floored, so only the integer part of y matters; the high lane is preserved from src1, not zeroed.**

column5: vscalefsh - scale scalar FP16 by 2^floor(exp)
column6: Computes `x * 2^floor(y)` for the low half-precision (FP16) lane, x from the first source and exponent y from the second, with the upper destination bits copied from src1. **AVX-512-FP16, EVEX-only, sets no flags. y is floored before scaling, and the upper lanes come from the first source rather than being cleared.**

column5: vscalefss - scale scalar f32 by 2^floor(exp)
column6: Computes `x * 2^floor(y)` for the low single-precision lane, x from src1 and exponent y from src2, preserving the upper bits of the destination from src1. Scalar `ldexp` for floats. **AVX-512, EVEX-only, no flag effects. Only the integer (floored) part of the exponent operand is used, and the high lanes are copied from the first source, not zeroed.**

column5: vsha512rnds2 - two rounds of SHA-512
column6: Performs two rounds of the SHA-512 compression function, updating the working state from the supplied message words and round constants packed in the source operands. Accelerates a SHA-512 hash loop in pairs of rounds. **Requires the SHA-512 (AVX) extension and operates on YMM register state; it sets no flags. You must supply correctly pre-arranged message schedule and constant words in the operands — the instruction does no message scheduling itself, so feed it the right state or you get a wrong digest.**

column5: vsm3msg1 - SM3 message expansion, part 1
column6: Performs the initial stage of the SM3 hash message-schedule expansion, combining input message words from the source operands to produce intermediate expanded words. Used together with `vsm3msg2` to build the SM3 message schedule. **Requires the SM3 extension; sets no flags. It is only half of the expansion — you must follow with `vsm3msg2` to finish each block of expanded words before feeding the SM3 round function.**

column5: vsm3msg2 - SM3 message expansion, part 2
column6: Performs the final stage of the SM3 hash message-schedule expansion, completing the expanded message words started by `vsm3msg1`. **Requires the SM3 extension; affects no flags. It must be paired with `vsm3msg1` in the correct order — running it on un-prepared input produces a bad schedule and a wrong hash.**

column5: vsm3rnds2 - two rounds of SM3 compression
column6: Performs two rounds of the SM3 hash compression function, advancing the working state using message words and an immediate that supplies round constants/index. **Requires the SM3 extension and sets no flags. The immediate encodes which round pair you are in, so it must increment correctly across the loop — a constant immediate silently computes the wrong digest.**

column5: vsm4key4 - four rounds of SM4 key expansion
column6: Performs four rounds of the SM4 block-cipher key schedule, deriving the next round keys from the current key state in the source operands. **Requires the SM4 extension; sets no flags. This produces round keys, not ciphertext — pair it with `vsm4rnds4` for encryption and make sure the key-expansion and encryption operands are not swapped.**

column5: vsm4rnds4 - four rounds of SM4 encryption
column6: Performs four rounds of SM4 block-cipher encryption, transforming the data state with the round keys supplied in the source operands. **Requires the SM4 extension and sets no flags. It consumes round keys produced by `vsm4key4`; passing raw key material instead of expanded round keys yields garbage ciphertext.**

column5: vsqrtph - square root of packed FP16 values
column6: Computes the IEEE square root of each packed half-precision (FP16) element of the source and writes the correctly-rounded results to the destination under EVEX mask. Unlike `vrsqrtph` this is an exact, fully-rounded square root, not an approximation. **AVX-512-FP16; sets no EFLAGS. Rounding follows MXCSR (or the EVEX embedded rounding control), and a negative input raises the invalid-operation flag and yields a QNaN.**

column5: vsqrtsh - square root of scalar FP16 value
column6: Computes the correctly-rounded square root of the low half-precision (FP16) element, writing it to the destination's low lane and copying the upper bits from the first source. **AVX-512-FP16, EVEX-only, no flag effects. The high lanes come from src1 rather than being zeroed, and a negative input gives a QNaN with the MXCSR invalid flag set.**

column5: vsubph - subtract packed FP16 values
column6: Subtracts each packed half-precision (FP16) element of the second source from the corresponding element of the first source and stores the results to the destination under EVEX mask. **AVX-512-FP16; sets no EFLAGS. Operand order matters (dest gets src1 - src2), and as with any FP subtract the NaN/Inf and signed-zero rules apply rather than integer wraparound.**

column5: vsubsh - subtract scalar FP16 value
column6: Subtracts the low half-precision (FP16) element of the second source from that of the first source, writing the difference to the destination's low lane and copying the upper bits from src1. **AVX-512-FP16, EVEX-only, no flag effects. The result is src1 - src2 in the low lane only; the upper lanes are preserved from the first source, not cleared.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: vtestpd - packed sign-bit test, sets ZF and CF
column6: Tests the sign bits of packed double-precision lanes: it ANDs the sign bits of the two source operands and sets `ZF` if that result is all zero, then ANDNs (src2 AND NOT src1 on the sign bits) and sets `CF` if that is all zero. No data is written — only flags change. **This is a flag-only test like `ptest` but restricted to the sign (high) bit of each lane; it clears `AF`, `PF`, `SF`, and `OF` to zero, so branch on `ZF`/`CF` (e.g. `jz`/`jc`) and do not expect the other flags to mean anything.**

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: vtestps - packed sign-bit test, sets ZF and CF
column6: Tests the sign bits of packed single-precision lanes: it sets `ZF` from the AND of the two operands' sign bits being all zero and `CF` from the ANDN (src2 AND NOT src1) being all zero, writing no destination data. **The single-precision form of `vtestpd`; it operates only on each lane's sign bit and forces `AF`, `PF`, `SF`, and `OF` to zero, so only `ZF` and `CF` carry meaning after it.**

column5: vucomish - unordered compare scalar FP16, set EFLAGS
column6: Performs an unordered compare of the two low half-precision (FP16) operands and sets `ZF`, `PF`, and `CF` to reflect the ordered/equal/less-than result, clearing `OF`, `SF`, and `AF`.
