A QNaN operand yields the unordered result (ZF=PF=CF=1) without raising an exception. **AVX-512-FP16; after it you branch with the unsigned conditions (`ja`/`jae`/`jb`/`jbe`/`je`) just like `ucomiss`, and you must check `PF` to detect unordered (NaN) cases since the equal/below encodings overlap with unordered.**

column5: vunpckhpd - interleave high packed f64 lanes
column6: This interleaves the high double-precision element from each 128-bit lane of two sources into the destination (the upper halves packed together). The garbled source row corresponds to `vunpckhpd`. **Like its low counterpart it is per-128-bit-lane, so wide `ymm`/`zmm` operands interleave only within each 16-byte lane, never across lanes.**

column5: vunpckhps - interleave high packed f32 lanes
column6: This interleaves the two high single-precision elements from each 128-bit lane of both sources into the destination. The garbled source row corresponds to `vunpckhps`. **It is a per-lane operation, so on `ymm`/`zmm` the high-element interleave happens independently inside each 16-byte lane rather than over the full register width.**

column5: vunpcklpd - interleave low packed f64 lanes
column6: This interleaves the low double-precision element from each 128-bit lane of two sources into the destination (low halves of the operands packed together). The garbled source row corresponds to `vunpcklpd`. **Like all the unpack instructions it operates per 128-bit lane, so with `ymm`/`zmm` it interleaves within each 16-byte lane rather than across the whole register — do not expect a full-width interleave on wide operands.**

column5: vunpcklps - interleave low packed f32 lanes
column6: This interleaves the two low single-precision elements from each 128-bit lane of both sources into the destination. The garbled source row corresponds to `vunpcklps`. **It works lane-by-lane on 128-bit chunks, so on `ymm`/`zmm` the interleave stays inside each 16-byte lane and does not cross lane boundaries — a common surprise when building wide shuffles.**

column5: vzeroall - zero all XMM/YMM/ZMM registers
column6: Sets the entire architectural vector register file (all XMM/YMM, and the low ZMM range) to zero in one instruction. Typically used to clear vector state before returning to non-AVX code. **Sets no flags. Issue it when transitioning out of AVX code to avoid the SSE/AVX state-switch stall penalty; note it wipes all vector registers, so do not run it while live floating-point values are still needed in those registers.**

column5: vzeroupper - zero upper bits of YMM/ZMM registers
column6: Zeroes the upper 128 bits of every YMM (and ZMM) register, leaving the low 128-bit XMM portions intact. This is the cheap way to avoid the costly AVX-to-SSE transition penalty. **Sets no flags. Emit `vzeroupper` before calling into SSE-only code (or returning from an AVX routine to a legacy ABI boundary) — forgetting it is a classic cause of mysterious performance cliffs, while the XMM low halves remain usable.**

column5: wait - wait for pending x87 FPU exceptions
column6: Suspends the integer pipeline until any pending unmasked x87 floating-point exception has been handled, synchronizing the FPU with the main processor. It is the standalone form that the assembler also emits as the `f`-prefix on instructions like `fstsw`/`finit`. **Sets no integer EFLAGS. It only matters for legacy x87 code with unmasked exceptions; modern SSE/AVX code does not need it, and `fwait` is just the alias the assembler accepts for the same opcode (0x9B).**

column5: wbinvd - write back and invalidate all caches
column6: Writes back all modified cache lines to memory and then invalidates every internal cache, also signaling external caches to do the same. Takes no operands and affects no flags. **Privileged (ring 0 only) and extremely expensive** — it flushes the entire cache hierarchy and can stall the machine for a long time, so it appears only in OS/firmware code, never in application code.

column5: wbnoinvd - write back caches without invalidating
column6: Writes back all modified (dirty) cache lines to memory but leaves the cache contents valid, unlike `wbinvd` which also invalidates. Takes no operands and touches no flags. **Privileged (ring 0) and CPUID-gated** — it requires the WBNOINVD feature, so on older parts you must fall back to `wbinvd`.

column5: wrfsbase/wrgsbase - write FS/GS base register
column6: Loads the FS (or GS) segment base address from a 32- or 64-bit general register, letting user code change its thread-local-storage base without a syscall. Affects no flags. **Requires CR4.FSGSBASE to be enabled by the OS** — if that bit is clear the instruction faults with #UD, and in 32-bit operand form the upper 32 bits of the base are zeroed.

column5: wrmsr - write to a model-specific register
column6: Writes the MSR selected by `ecx` with the 64-bit value in `edx:eax` (high dword in `edx`, low in `eax`). Affects no flags. **Privileged (ring 0) and serializing** — writing a reserved/unsupported MSR or a reserved bit raises #GP, and you must split your 64-bit value across `edx:eax`, not pass it in `rax`.

column5: wrmsrlist - write a list of MSRs from memory
column6: Writes a batch of MSRs in one instruction, taking parallel tables of MSR indices and values from memory pointed to by `rsi` and `rdi`, with a bitmap in `rcx` selecting which entries to process. Affects no flags. **Privileged and CPUID-gated (WRMSRLIST)** — the tables must be 8-byte aligned, and any faulting entry leaves `rcx` indicating progress so the operation can be restarted.

column5: wrmsrns - non-serializing write to an MSR
column6: Writes the MSR selected by `ecx` with `edx:eax`, exactly like `wrmsr` but without the architecturally serializing behavior, so it can be cheaper. Affects no flags. **Privileged and CPUID-gated (WRMSRNS)** — because it is non-serializing you must add your own fence/serialization if the new MSR value must take effect before later instructions; the value still goes in `edx:eax`, not `rax`.

column5: wrpkru - write the protection-key rights register
column6: Loads the PKRU register from `eax`, setting the per-key access-disable and write-disable bits used by memory protection keys; `ecx` and `edx` must both be zero. Affects no flags. **CPUID-gated (OSPKE) and order-sensitive** — it is not serializing, so it does not by itself fence prior loads/stores against the new permissions, and a nonzero `ecx`/`edx` raises #GP.

column5: wrssd/wrssq - write dword/qword to shadow stack
column6: Stores a 32-bit (`wrssd`) or 64-bit (`wrssq`) value to a shadow-stack page, the one sanctioned way to modify shadow-stack memory while CET shadow stacks are enabled. Affects no flags. **Requires CET shadow stacks active and a writable-shadow-stack mapping** — an ordinary `mov` to shadow-stack memory faults, and the target address must be naturally aligned for the operand size.

column5: wrussd/wrussq - kernel write to user shadow stack
column6: Stores a 32-bit (`wrussd`) or 64-bit (`wrussq`) value to a user-mode shadow-stack page, intended for supervisor code that needs to seed or fix up a user shadow stack. Affects no flags. **Privileged (ring 0) and CET-gated** — unlike `wrssd`/`wrssq` it always treats the access as user-mode for permission checks, so it #GPs if used from CPL 3 or against a supervisor shadow-stack page.

column5: xabort - abort an RTM transaction
column6: Immediately aborts the current RTM transactional region and rolls back all of its memory effects, passing the 8-bit immediate operand back to the fallback handler in the upper bits of `eax`. Does not affect the arithmetic flags directly. **Only meaningful inside an active transaction** — outside one it is effectively a no-op, and after the abort control resumes at the fallback address given to the original `xbegin`, not at the next instruction.

column5: xacquire/xrelease - HLE lock-elision prefixes
column6: These are prefix hints (encoded as `f2`/`f3`) placed on a locked read-modify-write or a store-to-release, asking the CPU to elide the lock and run the critical section transactionally under Hardware Lock Elision. They are prefixes, not standalone ops, and set no flags. **Purely a performance hint** — on a CPU without HLE they decode as the legacy `repne`/`rep` prefix and the instruction just runs normally, so correctness must never depend on elision actually happening.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: xadd - exchange then add, returning old value
column6: Atomically (with a `lock` prefix) loads the destination, writes the sum of source and destination into the destination, and writes the original destination value back into the source register. Updates `CF PF AF ZF SF OF` from the addition. **The source operand is overwritten with the old destination value** — that is the whole point for building atomic fetch-and-add counters, but it means you lose your input register, and you must prefix it with `lock` for the operation to be atomic across cores.

column5: xbegin - begin an RTM transaction
column6: Starts an RTM transactional region; the rel16/rel32 operand gives the fallback address taken if the transaction aborts. On entry it sets up speculative execution, and on abort `eax` carries status bits explaining why. **The fallback path is not optional** — an abort can be triggered at any time by another core, an interrupt, or a capacity overflow, so you must always provide a non-transactional retry/fallback at the `xbegin` target and never assume the transaction commits.

column5: xchg - exchange register/memory with register
column6: Swaps the contents of its two operands in a single instruction; at least one operand is a register and the other a register or memory. Touches no flags. **When one operand is memory, `xchg` is implicitly atomic with an automatic `lock`** even if you do not write the prefix, which makes an accidental memory `xchg` far more expensive than you might expect; `xchg eax, eax` is also the canonical encoding of a `nop`.

column5: xend - commit an RTM transaction
column6: Ends the current RTM transactional region and attempts to atomically commit all of its buffered memory effects; if the commit succeeds, execution continues normally past the region. Affects no arithmetic flags. **Executing `xend` with no transaction in progress raises #GP** — it must be paired with the matching `xbegin`, and a failed commit transfers control to the fallback handler rather than falling through.

column5: xgetbv - read an extended control register
column6: Reads the extended control register (XCR) selected by `ecx` and returns its 64-bit value in `edx:eax` (high dword in `edx`). Affects no flags. **Unprivileged but pass the right index in `ecx`** — XCR0 is index 0, and an out-of-range index raises #GP; the result is split across `edx:eax`, so don't expect the full value in `rax`.

column5: xlat - table lookup translate via AL/RBX
column6: Performs a byte table lookup: it loads `al` from the byte at `[rbx + al]` (zero-extended), translating `al` through a 256-entry table whose base is in `rbx`. Affects no flags. **Both operands are completely implicit** — the index is the current `al`, the table base is `rbx`/`ebx`, and `al` is read as an unsigned 0-255 offset, so you must set up both registers before the instruction; the operand form `xlat` accepts is purely documentary.

column5: xor - bitwise exclusive OR
column6: Computes the bitwise XOR of source into destination, storing the result in the destination. Clears `CF` and `OF`, sets `SF`, `ZF`, and `PF` from the result, and leaves `AF` undefined. **`xor reg, reg` is the idiomatic way to zero a register** — it is shorter than `mov reg, 0` and the CPU recognizes it as a zeroing idiom, but unlike `mov` it does write the flags (notably setting `ZF`), so don't slip one in between a `cmp` and a conditional jump.

column5: xorpd - packed double-precision bitwise XOR
column6: Bitwise-XORs two 128-bit XMM operands as packed doubles, storing the result in the destination; it is a pure bit operation despite the floating-point name. Touches no flags. **It does not interpret the bits as floating-point at all** — its main use is sign manipulation, e.g. XOR with a mask of the sign bits to negate doubles, and the legacy form leaves the upper bits of a YMM/ZMM untouched while the VEX form `vxorpd` zeroes them.

column5: xorps - packed single-precision bitwise XOR
column6: Bitwise-XORs two 128-bit XMM operands as packed singles, writing the result to the destination; like `xorpd` it is a bit operation, not arithmetic. Affects no flags. **`xorps xmm, xmm` is the classic idiom to zero an XMM register** and to flip sign bits of floats; it is one byte shorter than the `pd` form, but mixing it with double-precision data can incur a domain-crossing penalty on some microarchitectures.

column5: xresldtrk - resume suspended load-address tracking
column6: Resumes the tracking of load addresses inside a transactional region after it was paused by `xsusldtrk`, re-enabling the monitoring of loads for transactional conflict detection. Affects no flags. **CPUID-gated (TSXLDTRK) and meaningful only in a transaction** — it must be paired with a prior `xsusldtrk`, and using it outside an active region has no useful effect.

column5: xrstor - restore extended processor state
column6: Restores x87, SSE, AVX, and other extended state from the 512+ byte XSAVE area at the memory operand, restoring only the components selected by the requested-feature bitmap in `edx:eax` and present in the area's header. Affects no flags. **The save area must be 64-byte aligned and `edx:eax` must be a valid feature mask** — a misaligned address or restoring a component the area never saved raises #GP, and the mask is split across `edx:eax`.

column5: xrstors - restore extended state (supervisor)
column6: Like `xrstor` but additionally handles supervisor state components and only works with the compacted save-area format. Restores the components chosen by `edx:eax` from the 64-byte-aligned memory area. Affects no flags. **Privileged (ring 0) and compacted-format only** — feeding it a standard (non-compacted) XSAVE area, a misaligned pointer, or an invalid mask raises #GP, so it is not a drop-in replacement for `xrstor`.

column5: xsave - save extended processor state
column6: Saves the x87, SSE, AVX, and other extended state components selected by the bitmask in `edx:eax` into the XSAVE area at the memory operand. Affects no flags. **The destination must be 64-byte aligned and you must supply the feature mask in `edx:eax`** — only the bits set there (and supported by the CPU) are written, and it uses the larger standard (non-compacted) layout, so size the buffer from CPUID, not from a guess.

column5: xsavec - save extended state, compacted format
column6: Saves the selected extended-state components into the XSAVE area using the compacted format, which omits gaps for disabled components and so produces a smaller image than `xsave`. The component mask comes from `edx:eax`. Affects no flags. **The 64-byte-aligned area it writes is compacted and must be restored with `xrstors`/compacted-aware `xrstor`** — you cannot mix a compacted save with a standard-format restore.

column5: xsaveopt - save extended state, optimized
column6: Saves extended state like `xsave` but skips writing components that have not changed since the last restore, using internal modified/init tracking to reduce memory traffic. Mask comes from `edx:eax`, area is 64-byte aligned. Affects no flags. **Because it may legitimately leave parts of the buffer stale, you must pair it with the matching `xrstor` and not assume every selected component was actually written** — it is an optimization over `xsave`, not a format change.

column5: xsaves - save extended state (supervisor)
column6: Saves both user and supervisor extended-state components into a compacted XSAVE area, combining the optimization of `xsaveopt` with access to supervisor state. Mask comes from `edx:eax`. Affects no flags. **Privileged (ring 0) and compacted-format only** — it can save components ordinary `xsave` cannot, its output must be restored with `xrstors`, and the area must be 64-byte aligned or it #GPs.

column5: xsetbv - write an extended control register
column6: Writes the extended control register (XCR) selected by `ecx` with the 64-bit value in `edx:eax`, typically used to program XCR0 and enable AVX/AVX-512 state. Affects no flags. **Privileged (ring 0) and validity-checked** — setting an unsupported or inconsistent XCR0 bit combination (e.g. enabling AVX state without SSE state) raises #GP, and the value must be placed in `edx:eax`, not `rax`.

column5: xsusldtrk - suspend load-address tracking
column6: Suspends the tracking of load addresses within a transactional region so that subsequent loads are not added to the transaction's read set, which can avoid spurious aborts for accesses you know are safe. Affects no flags. **CPUID-gated (TSXLDTRK) and must be resumed** — loads done while tracking is suspended escape conflict detection, so pair it with `xresldtrk` and only suspend over regions where dropped tracking is genuinely safe.

column4: CF PF AF ZF SF OF
column5: xtest - test for transactional execution
column6: Tests whether the processor is currently executing inside an RTM or HLE transactional region and reports the result in `ZF`: `ZF` is cleared when a transaction is active and set otherwise. It leaves the other listed flags (`CF PF AF SF OF`) effectively unaffected/undefined for branching purposes. **Branch on `ZF` with `jnz` to detect 'in a transaction'** — note the polarity is inverted from intuition (clear `ZF` means transactional), and it is CPUID-gated on RTM/HLE support.
